context: ...the book of Psalms in the 90th chapter gives us this truth concerning our lives, and I want you to turn there quickly, Psalm 90, I want you to look at what the Bible says in verse number 9...
point_summary: Human life, like Joseph's bewildering ordeal, passes as a story being told—its meaning and outcome known to God alone, even when hidden from the one living it.

context: The Bible tells us in Psalm 90 in verse number 9, for all our days are passed away in thy wrath. Notice what it says, we spend our years as a tale that is told.
point_summary: Human lives unfold like a story being told—Joseph, mid-pit, could not see the next chapter, just as Psalm 90:9 frames our years as a tale whose ending remains unknown to us.

context: The Bible says in verse number 10, the days of our years are threescore years and ten, for by reason of strength they be fourscore years, yet is their strength, labor, and sorrow for to soon cut off and we fly away.
point_summary: Human life passes like a tale being told — a framing used to show that Joseph, unlike the reader, could not yet see the full story arc of his own days.

context: So verse 12 says teach us to number our days that we may apply our hearts unto wisdom.
point_summary: Because Joseph could not see his story's end as we can, Psalm 90:12's call to number our days frames life as a tale unfolding under God's hand, even through unexpected turns.

context: ...the Bible tells us, it's an interesting verse of Scripture in verse number 25, it's speaking of the children of Israel...
point_summary: Complaining spreads to others because Israel's murmuring in the wilderness was not merely personal but contagious, infecting the community and drawing them away from trust in God.

context: We think about the simple here, and he talks about how the testimonies of the Lord is sure, making wise the simple.
point_summary: God's Word is perfect and converting — its testimony is sure, its statutes right, and its counsel more to be desired than gold, making it the sufficient guide the Laodicean church needed.

context: It tells us in verse eight, the statutes of the Lord are, what does it say, right.
point_summary: Unlike human perspective, which is flawed and shifting, God's statutes declared in Psalm 19:8 are always right—a fixed, authoritative counsel the Laodiceans desperately needed.

context: The fear of the Lord is clean, enduring forever. The judgments of the Lord are true and righteous altogether.
point_summary: God's judgments being "true and righteous altogether" establishes Scripture as the infallible standard of right counsel, contrasted with the flawed, self-directed perspective of every human opinion.

context: The Lord knoweth the thoughts of man that they are vanity.
point_summary: God's omniscience of human thoughts—that they are vanity—establishes the backdrop for why divine chastening is a blessing rather than a cause for despair.

context: Blessed is the man whom thou chastenest, O Lord, and teachest him out of thy law,
point_summary: Divine chastening is a mark of blessing, not curse — the psalmist declares the man whom God corrects and teaches from His law to be blessed.

context: that thou mayest give him rest from the days of adversity until the pit be digged for the wicked.
point_summary: Chastening from God is a mark of divine blessing, as Psalm 94:13 frames the one whom God corrects as "blessed," granting rest from adversity rather than abandonment.

context: The Bible tells us in Psalm 19, as it speaks about the law of the Lord being perfect and all that God's description is, he says, moreover, by them is thy servant warned and in keeping of them, there is great reward.
point_summary: Psalm 19's declaration that God's law carries great reward for those who keep it grounds the sermon's argument that obedience to God's commandments is not burdensome but beneficial, flowing from trust in his love.

context: In Psalm 119, in verse number 126, the Bible says this, it is time for thee, Lord, to work. For they have made void thy law.
point_summary: Amid cultural lawlessness, the psalmist's cry "It is time for Thee, Lord, to work" becomes a model prayer—anticipating God's intervention rather than dreading the difficulty that precedes it.

context: If you have your Bible with you this morning, Psalm 145, Psalm 145, and it's got 21 verses in this particular psalm, we're going to do our best to make it through the entirety of the psalm this morning.
point_summary: Psalm 145, the capstone text of a yearlong study on divine greatness, is presented as God's own self-revelation, with Scripture itself identified as the means by which He has disclosed His nature and the truth of salvation.

context: I find myself looking at verse number three and obviously understanding that the psalmist recognizes the greatness of God. But he makes this statement at the end of verse number three which is so, just a thought provoking statement when he says, and His greatness is unsearchable.
point_summary: God's greatness, though knowable through Scripture, is declared "unsearchable" in verse 3—meaning no matter how great any worshiper conceives Him to be, He surpasses that conception entirely.

context: We find that in Psalm 135 in verse number five, the psalmist declares with great confidence and certainty, I know that the Lord is great.
point_summary: Psalm 135:5's confident declaration "I know that the Lord is great" is cited to anchor the claim that God's greatness, though knowable, remains unsearchable—exceeding whatever measure any worshiper can conceive.

context: great is the Lord and greatly to be
point_summary: God's greatness, declared in verse 3 as unsearchable yet certain, is affirmed not as abstract doctrine but as a conviction the psalmist stakes his praise upon without reservation.

context: God is great and greatly to be praised.
point_summary: Though God's greatness surpasses full comprehension, the psalmist's broad declarative statement in verse 3 expresses settled, unshakeable confidence that God is great and worthy of praise.

context: Notice in verse number five, and we'll walk our way through the psalm...
point_summary: God's majesty, rendered in Psalm 145:5 as that which inspires awe and reverence, is identified here as the first specific detail supporting the psalmist's broad declaration that God is great.

context: great is the Lord and greatly to be praised, and his greatness is unsearchable
point_summary: God's unsearchable greatness, declared in verse 3, grounds the call to awe-filled worship, since understanding His revealed qualities and majesty is what makes Him worthy of reverence.

context: they shall abundantly utter the memory of thy great goodness and shall sing of thy righteousness
point_summary: God's great goodness, celebrated in verse 7 as worthy of abundant utterance, is an unchanging attribute independent of human circumstances, not contingent on how life feels at any given moment.

context: taste and see that the Lord is good
point_summary: Tasting and seeing that the Lord is good (Ps. 34:8) grounds the affirmation that God's goodness is absolute and unconditional, not determined by personal circumstances.

context: We find that just a few pages back in your, in the book of Psalms in Psalm 136, I'm not going to read it, but you'll notice that throughout Psalm 136 that every verse in Psalm 136 ends with this statement, for his mercy endureth forever.
point_summary: Psalm 136's repeated refrain—"his mercy endureth forever"—is cited as scriptural evidence that God's great mercy is an enduring, inexhaustible attribute he wants his people to deeply understand.

context: He's of great mercy is what the Psalmist says in Psalm 145 in verse number eight.
point_summary: God's "great mercy" in verse 8 is defined as his withholding what sinners truly deserve—eternal separation from God—contrasted with grace, which gives what is undeserved, both grounded in his inexhaustible long-suffering.

context: Notice as we continue in verse number nine, the Lord is good. Notice where it says to all.
point_summary: God's goodness extending "to all" in Psalm 145:9 is taken as evidence that God's love encompasses all of humanity, not a select few.

context: In verse number 11, they shall speak of the glory of thy kingdom and talk of thy power to make known to the sons of men his mighty acts and the glorious majesty of his kingdom.
point_summary: Psalm 145:11–13 grounds the declaration that God's kingdom is everlasting and his dominion endures through all generations, even as the heathen rage, because God omnipotent still rules and will establish his millennial kingdom on earth.

context: Thy kingdom is an everlasting kingdom, and thy dominion endureth throughout all generations.
point_summary: God's kingdom being everlasting and his dominion enduring throughout all generations grounds the assurance that, despite the raging of the heathen, God rules and will establish his millennial kingdom on earth.

context: We live in a time today where the heathen rage, Psalm 2.
point_summary: Despite the heathen's rage (Ps. 2), God's everlasting kingdom and omnipotent rule remain unchallenged, with Christ's millennial reign as the ultimate vindication of his dominion.

reference_text: the Lord upholdeth all that fall, and raiseth up all those that be bowed down
context: Notice as we continue in verse number 14, notice the Bible says, the Lord upholdeth all that fall, and raiseth up all those that be bowed down.
point_summary: God's sustaining and restoring character is drawn from verse 14's twin declarations that He upholds the fallen and raises the bowed down.

context: He restoreth my soul.
point_summary: God's restorative work, illustrated by the familiar phrase "He restoreth my soul" from Psalm 23, is cited as evidence that He is a restoring God who sustains those bowed down.

context: The eyes of all wait upon Thee, and Thou givest them their meat in due season.
point_summary: God's provision for every living thing is the theological point drawn from verse 15, with the preacher emphasizing that human providers are instruments, but God is the ultimate source of all sustenance.

reference_text: Thou openest Thine hand and satisfyest the desire of every living thing.
context: Thou openest Thine hand and satisfyest the desire of every living thing.
point_summary: God's open hand satisfies the desire of every living thing, establishing Him as the ultimate provider behind every human means of provision.

context: The Lord is righteous in all His ways.
point_summary: God's righteousness is total and without exception—"all His ways," not some or most—so that even painful or unexplained circumstances cannot legitimately call His goodness into question.

context: Remember what the psalmist says in Psalm 119, it was good for me to be afflicted. Really? Yeah. Because then I learned thy statutes.
point_summary: Affliction, rightly received, becomes a means of learning God's statutes — the psalmist's testimony that suffering was "good" grounds the sermon's claim that God's ways are righteous even when painful.

context: the Lord is nigh unto all them that call upon Him, to all that call upon Him in truth.
point_summary: God's nearness to those who call upon Him in truth is the theological ground for confidence in prayer, as the psalmist's conviction of God's greatness rests on the assurance that God hears every cry.

context: He will fulfill the desire of them that fear Him. He also will hear their cry and will save them.
point_summary: God's greatness is demonstrated in that He hears and fulfills the desires of those who fear Him when they pray, a truth the psalmist draws from personal experience throughout the Psalms.

context: Notice in verse number 20, the Lord preserveth all them that love Him, but all the wicked will He destroy.
point_summary: God's preservation of those who love Him is grounded in the believer's eternal security, secured not by personal merit but by faith in Christ's finished work at Calvary.

context: We see in the psalm, I mean, in Psalm 145, it's not exhaustive. Why is it not exhaustive? Well, remember, his greatness is unsearchable. David's providing us with a little glimpse.
point_summary: Psalm 145's repeated "I will" declarations reveal the psalmist's deliberate, purposed commitment to proclaiming God's greatness—not tentative effort, but resolved declaration.

context: Number two this morning, in Psalm 145, we find, number one, that the psalmist is convinced, he's confident of God's greatness.
point_summary: Psalm 145's repeated "I will" declarations reveal the psalmist's firm, purposed commitment to proclaiming God's greatness—not tentative effort, but resolved declaration.

context: I will dwell in the house of the Lord forever, Psalm 23 and verse number six
point_summary: David's vow to bless God's name "forever and ever" is grounded in the certainty of eternal dwelling in God's house, making present daily praise the beginning of a praise that never ends.

context: David said, my mouth shall speak the praise of the Lord, but notice, and let all flesh bless his holy name forever and ever.
point_summary: The shift from "saints" in verse 10 to "all flesh" in verse 21 signals that every created being—not only believers—will ultimately bless God's holy name, a truth confirmed by Philippians 2:9–10.

context: in verse number 10, it said saints.
point_summary: All believers — defined here as saints in the biblical sense — bear a present responsibility to bless God with their mouths, a duty contrasted with the future universal blessing of "all flesh" in verse 21.

context: they were saying the enemies, those that were rising against him, were saying there is no help for him in God.
point_summary: The enemies' taunt that "there is no help for him in God" sets the crisis David faces as real and severe, making his prayerful counter-declaration of God as shield all the more striking.

context: he says, oh Lord, he says, thou art a shield for me.
point_summary: David's prayer-response to his enemies' taunt ("there is no help for him in God") begins by rehearsing God's identity as his shield—the first such declaration in the Psalms.

context: I laid me down and slept; I awaked; for the Lord sustained me.
point_summary: Amid dire circumstances, David's act of lying down and sleeping testifies to a rest grounded in God's sustaining power—the theological climax the sermon identifies as the "rest" David experienced.

context: I will not be afraid of ten thousands of people, that have set themselves against me round about.
point_summary: Fearlessness before overwhelming opposition—"I will not be afraid of ten thousands"—is presented as the fruit of the rest and sustaining that God provided to David in his dire circumstances.

context: But thou, O Lord, art a shield for me, my glory and the lifter up of mine head.
point_summary: David's rehearsal of God's identity as shield, glory, and lifter of his head is presented as the theological foundation that enabled him to rest rather than continue worrying.

context: I cried unto the Lord with my voice and He heard me out of His holy hill, Selah.
point_summary: Confidence that God heard his cry (v. 4) freed David to stop worrying and actually sleep, demonstrating that true rest follows from leaving burdens with the Lord rather than merely bringing them to Him.

context: I laid me down and slept.
point_summary: David's act of lying down and sleeping amid overwhelming crisis is presented as the natural outcome of genuinely leaving one's burdens with God rather than continuing to worry after praying.

context: Do you not see a great peace in David in Psalm 3 and verse 5? Laid down and went to sleep.
point_summary: David's ability to lie down and sleep amid Absalom's threat illustrates the peace that surpasses understanding, the experiential fruit of bringing every care to God in prayer.

context: I laid me down and slept. And then it says this, I awaked. For the Lord sustained me.
point_summary: David's ability to lie down and sleep amid Absalom's real and pressing threat is presented as the peace that surpasses understanding, born of having prayed and entrusted the situation entirely to a God who sustains.

context: The Lord is my light and my salvation; whom shall I fear? the Lord is the strength of my life; of whom shall I be afraid?
point_summary: Knowing God as light, salvation, and strength—not merely as doctrine but as personal possession—is the ground on which the believer's repeated resolve not to fear rests.

context: When the wicked, even mine enemies and my foes, came upon me to eat up my flesh, they stumbled and fell.
point_summary: When enemies came to devour him, the psalmist declared fearlessness because his confidence rested in knowing who God is — a pattern the sermon holds up as the believer's proper response to threat.

context: Though an host should encamp against me, my heart shall not fear: though war should rise against me, in this will I be confident.
point_summary: Even when surrounded by enemies and threatened by war, the psalmist's heart refuses fear because his singular desire is to dwell with the Lord, grounding confidence in God's presence rather than circumstances.

context: One thing have I desired of the Lord, that will I seek after; that I may dwell in the house of the Lord all the days of my life, to behold the beauty of the Lord, and to inquire in his temple.
point_summary: Dwelling in God's presence and beholding His beauty is the singular desire that grounds the psalmist's repeated, confident refusal to fear any enemy or threat.

context: Every day, he says in verse number five, rest my words, all their thoughts are against me for evil, they gather themselves together, they hide themselves, they mark my steps when they wait for my soul.
point_summary: Even when enemies conspire daily against the believer, the psalmist's confidence rests not in circumstances but in the settled knowledge that God is for him.

context: Verse 8, thou tellest my wanderings, put thou my tears into thy bottle, are they not in thy book when I cry unto thee?
point_summary: God's meticulous awareness of every tear and every wandering step assures the believer that no suffering goes unnoticed or unrecorded before him.

context: Notice in verse number 9, when I cry unto thee, then shall mine enemies turn back, this I know. Notice this last phrase of verse number 9, for God is for me.
point_summary: In the midst of enemies, the psalmist's confident declaration "this I know, for God is for me" grounds assurance not in circumstances but in the settled knowledge of whose side God is on.

context: Notice in Psalm 118. In Psalm 118, in verse number 6, notice it says, the Lord is on my side. I will not fear. What can man do unto me?
point_summary: Confidence against human threat rests on the certainty that the Lord is on one's side, so that whatever man does is bounded by what God permits.

context: I will lift up my eyes unto the hills from whence cometh my help.
point_summary: Redirecting the mind away from one's trial and toward God—illustrated by Psalm 121:1's upward look—is presented as the path to the perfect peace Isaiah 26:3 promises.

context: my help cometh from the Lord which made heaven and earth.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: the truth of Psalm 3. There was a real situation, the reality is given to us in verses 1 and 2.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: the truth of Psalm 3. There was a real situation, the reality is given to us in verses 1 and 2.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: In Psalm 78, I love how the psalmist references this...
point_summary: God's sovereign choice of David—taken from tending sheep to shepherding Israel—illustrates that He selects and assigns His workmanship according to His own purpose, not human expectation.

context: he chose David, also his servant, and took him from the sheepfolds
point_summary: God's sovereign choice of David from obscure shepherding work, as the psalmist frames it, illustrates that God had a specific, individual plan for David's life.

context: from following the ewes, great with young, he brought him to feed Jacob, his people, and Israel, his inheritance
point_summary: God's sovereign choice of David from the sheepfold illustrates that He had a specific, individual plan for David's life long before David's greatness was recognized by anyone else.

context: so he fed them according to the integrity of his heart, and guided them by the skillfulness of his hands
point_summary: God's sovereign choice of David from the sheepfold, as the psalmist frames it, illustrates that God had a specific, individual plan for David's life long before his kingship was recognized by anyone else.

context: but murmured in their tents, and hearken not unto the voice of the Lord
point_summary: Murmuring in the tent—the private dwelling—corrupted the next generation, illustrating how a home's atmosphere either reinforces or undermines the teaching of God's Word.

context: The Lord is my shepherd. What does He say? I shall not want.
point_summary: Psalm 23:1's declaration that the Lord is shepherd grounds the call to contentment: because He is a good shepherd who provides, His people have no legitimate cause to want.

context: why do the heathen rage
point_summary: Psalm 2's depiction of earthly kings raging against the Lord is invoked to illustrate that the world system only appears permanent, while God's promise assures it will pass away.

context: the kings of the earth, they set themselves against the Lord
point_summary: Psalm 2's depiction of earth's kings setting themselves against the Lord is invoked to illustrate that the world system's apparent permanence is an illusion, since God promises it will pass away.

context: You think about the truth of Psalm 23 and you do not have to take the time to turn there, but it's an amazing truth where the psalmist ends that psalm by saying surely goodness and mercy shall follow me all the days of my life and I will dwell in the house
point_summary: The confidence of Psalm 23's closing promise—dwelling in the Lord's house forever—rests entirely on its opening declaration that the Lord is my shepherd.

context: the first statement of the 23rd Psalm. He cannot have the confidence that he will dwell in the house of the Lord forever if first the Lord is not my shepherd.
point_summary: Psalm 23:1 grounds the confidence of verse 6—only because the Lord is my shepherd can the psalmist declare with certainty that he will dwell in the house of the Lord forever.

context: the truth of the last statement of the 23rd Psalm is directly related to the first statement of the 23rd Psalm. He cannot have the confidence that he will dwell in the house of the Lord forever
point_summary: Eternal assurance of dwelling in God's house forever (Ps. 23:6) rests entirely on the prior confession of the first verse — "the Lord is my shepherd."

context: He that dwelleth in the secret place of the Most High shall abide under the shadow of the Almighty.
point_summary: Abiding under the shadow of the Almighty (Ps. 91:1) is set in direct contrast to Israel's tragic choice to seek refuge in the inferior shadow of Egypt and Pharaoh.

context: I will say to the Lord, He is my refuge and my fortress. My God, in Him will I trust.
point_summary: Psalm 91:2's declaration of the Lord as refuge and fortress is set against Israel's tragic choice to seek shelter in Egypt's shadow rather than the Almighty's.

context: The psalmist says in Psalm 121, we used it as a theme verse a couple of years ago. I will lift up mine eyes into the hills from whence cometh my help.
point_summary: Contrasted with Israel's condemned flight to Egypt for military aid, Psalm 121:1–2 is invoked to establish that the believer's help comes from the Lord alone, not from earthly powers.

context: My help cometh from the Lord, which made heaven and earth.
point_summary: God's people have no need to seek help from earthly powers, because their help comes from the Lord who made heaven and earth, not from men or horses.

context: Help, Lord. That's how it begins.
point_summary: In times of crisis, the simplest cry—"Help, Lord"—is presented as a sufficient and fitting prayer, affirming that God is the one to whom believers run when all other resources fail.

context: This book, the Bible says in Psalm 119,
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: the Bible says the heavens declare the glory of God
point_summary: General revelation stirs an innate awareness of God in those who have never heard the gospel, yet leaves them without the saving truth only a sent preacher can supply.

context: thou art a priest after the order of Melchizedek
point_summary: Because Christ is from the tribe of Judah rather than Levi, His priesthood cannot rest on Mosaic law but is validated solely by the Melchizedekian order prophesied in Psalm 110:4.

context: And the Psalmist said, I know that the Lord is great and yet without contradiction also declared that His greatness is unsearchable.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

reference_text: the fruit of the womb is his reward. Children are an heritage of the Lord.
context: the Bible says that the fruit of the womb is his reward. Children are an heritage of the Lord.
point_summary: Children are described not as a burden but as a heritage and reward from the Lord, countering a cultural tendency to view them as obstacles to personal ambition.

context: I know that as we look at Psalm 119, and there's so many references in that portion of Scripture that are so impactful for the believer
point_summary: Psalm 119 is cited as evidence that immersion in God's Word shapes a believer's thinking and speech so that the mouth becomes an instrument of strengthening others with God's own words.

context: The psalm ends with this verse and the psalmist says, let the words of my mouth and the meditation of my heart be acceptable in thy sight, O Lord, my strength and my redeemer.
point_summary: Psalm 19:14's closing petition—that words and heart-meditations be acceptable to God—is cited as the standard requiring believers to think carefully and deliberately before speaking to those who are hurting.

context: as the psalmist prayed in Psalm 19, let the words of my mouth and the meditation of my heart be acceptable in thy sight
point_summary: Psalm 19:14 is invoked as a model prayer for believers to offer, asking that both words and heart-meditations meet God's standard of acceptability in speech toward the suffering.

context: Verse number 16 says this, the Lord is king forever and ever. The Bible says the heathen are perished out of his land.
point_summary: God's eternal kingship is not subject to human election or approval — Christ reigns from his throne by rightful possession, not by vote, now and forever.
Christ's eternal kingship over all nations—affirmed in Psalm 10:16—stands independent of any human electoral process, demanding that Scripture's authority shape how believers engage civic life.
God's eternal kingship—"the Lord sitteth King forever"—is set alongside Psalm 10:16 and Revelation to establish that Christ's reign is not subject to electoral outcomes.

Christ's eternal, uncontested kingship—declared in the simple affirmation "the Lord reigneth"—is set alongside Psalm 10:16 and Revelation 19 as grounds for rejoicing that transcends any earthly election.

context: the wicked shall be turned into hell and all the nations that forget God
point_summary: Nations that forget God face divine judgment — a universal warning, not limited to Israel, underscoring that America stands ripe for judgment under a righteous God who loves righteousness.

context: I will praise thee for I am fearfully and wonderfully made. Marvelous are thy works, and that my soul knoweth right well.
point_summary: Psalm 139:14–16 grounds the sanctity of human life in God's authorship of it, establishing that abortion is a biblical issue, not merely a political one.

context: my substance was not hid from thee when I was made in secret and curiously wrought in the lowest parts of the earth.
point_summary: God's complete knowledge of a person's formation in the womb, cited alongside Psalm 139:14 and 16, grounds the sanctity of life as a biblical rather than merely political issue.

context: thine eyes did see my substance, yet being unperfect. And in my book all my members were written, which in continuance were fashioned, when as yet there was none of them.
point_summary: God's foreknowledge of each person's members before birth, recorded in his book, grounds the sanctity of human life as a biblical rather than merely political issue.

context: For promotion cometh neither from the east nor from the west nor from the south.
point_summary: Promotion and political power come not from human processes or earthly directions but from God alone, who sovereignly deposes and installs rulers according to His purposes.

context: But God is the judge. Notice this. He putteth down one and setteth up another.
point_summary: God alone determines the rise and fall of rulers—"he putteth down one and setteth up another"—so debates about electoral fairness must reckon with the sovereign Judge who stands above every earthly process.

context: Blessed, in Psalm 2, in verse number 12, blessed are all they that put their trust in Him.
point_summary: Psalm 2:12's beatitude — "blessed are all they that put their trust in Him" — grounds the call to place confidence in Christ as King rather than in electoral outcomes or political institutions.

context: Psalm 20, in verse number 7, some trust in chariots and some in horses, but we will remember the name of the Lord our God.
point_summary: Psalm 20:7 is cited to contrast misplaced trust in earthly powers with the believer's call to remember and rely on the name of the Lord alone.

context: I'm going to rejoice. Psalm 5 and verse number 11, but
point_summary: Rejoicing is grounded not in electoral outcomes but in trust in God alone, as Psalm 5:11 calls those who trust Him to shout for joy because He defends them.

reference_text: open Thou mine eyes that I may behold wondrous things out of Thy law
context: As I open my Bible each morning, I say, Lord, open Thou mine eyes that I may behold wondrous things out of Thy law.
point_summary: Psalm 119:18 is cited as a personal prayer for divine illumination and conviction when opening Scripture, illustrating that God must open eyes for His Word to teach and transform.

context: The psalmist in Psalm 119 says, I will not forget.
point_summary: The psalmist's resolve "I will not forget" in Psalm 119 illustrates the discipline required to retain divine truth, underscoring why believers must remain consistently in Scripture to avoid spiritual forgetfulness.

context: I think Mark you prayed it this morning from Psalm 139, search us. Show us if there be any wicked way.
point_summary: Psalm 139's prayer — "search us, show us if there be any wicked way" — is invoked as the honest posture required for genuine self-examination before taking the Lord's Supper.

context: blessed art thou, O Lord, teach me thy statutes
point_summary: The psalmist's repeated plea "teach me thy statutes" in verse 12 establishes the foundational desire for a teachable spirit that frames the entire sermon series on learning through affliction.

context: Blessed art thou, O Lord, teach me thy statutes.
point_summary: Psalm 119:12's plea — "teach me thy statutes" — is the opening prayer that verse 71 answers, showing affliction as God's chosen means of fulfilling that very request.

context: It is good for me that I have been afflicted, that I might learn thy statutes.
point_summary: Affliction, though unwelcome, is declared by the psalmist to be good precisely because it served as the means by which he learned God's statutes — the very thing he had been praying to be taught.

context: It is good for me that I have been afflicted; that I might learn thy statutes.
point_summary: Affliction, the psalmist testifies, is good precisely because God uses it as the classroom through which His statutes are learned—connecting the repeated plea "teach me thy statutes" to its divinely appointed means.

context: it is good for me that I have been afflicted. That's a perspective that we need to adopt in our lives.
point_summary: Affliction is not merely permitted by God but purposefully designed as a classroom through which believers learn His statutes—a perspective the psalmist declares as good, not grievous.

context: Before I was afflicted, notice what he says, I went astray. But then notice the end of verse 67, But now have I kept thy word.
point_summary: Affliction functioned as the turning point in the psalmist's life: before it he went astray, but afterward he kept God's word, showing that God uses suffering to redirect wandering hearts back to obedience.

context: It is good for me that I have been afflicted, that I might learn thy statutes.
point_summary: Affliction is God's appointed classroom for teaching his statutes, and the psalmist recognizes it as an answered prayer—the very means by which God corrected his straying and produced obedience to God's word.

reference_text: it is good for me that I've been afflicted, that I might learn thy statutes
context: you think about the psalmist, and he says, it is good for me that I've been afflicted, that I might learn thy statutes.
point_summary: Affliction, as the psalmist declares in Psalm 119:71, is purposeful—a truth Paul's own thorn-in-the-flesh experience confirms, showing suffering as divinely appointed rather than meaningless.

context: I've learned just as the psalmist said that it was good for me to be afflicted because then I learned thy statutes
point_summary: Affliction functions as a divinely appointed classroom where lessons unavailable elsewhere are learned, as confirmed by both the psalmist's testimony and the witness of Luther and Spurgeon.

context: All men are liars
point_summary: All men are liars — a truth the law exposes, illustrating that the law's function is condemnation, not salvation.

context: Psalm 16, and his presence is fullness of joy.
point_summary: Time spent in God's presence produces visible joy and transformation, as Psalm 16:11's promise of "fullness of joy" in His presence is seen on Moses' shining face.

context: Notice in Psalm 27 and in verse number 11, and really this evening we're using this as a springboard.
point_summary: Psalm 27:11's petition "Teach me Thy way, O Lord" is presented as a model recorded request, illustrating that believers should come to God with specific, directed desires to know His path and receive His guidance.

context: in fact, in Psalm 25 and verse number 4, he uses that phraseology that God would teach him, Thy paths.
point_summary: Psalm 25:4's parallel petition — "show me Thy ways, teach me Thy paths" — reinforces that the psalmist's recorded requests express dependence on God for direction and leadership in life.

context: The psalmist says in Psalm 119 a number of times, we'll just look at one of the references, but there's a number of times in Psalm 119, six times actually, that the psalmist says, teach me Thy statutes.
point_summary: Six times in Psalm 119, the psalmist explicitly asks God to "teach me thy statutes," demonstrating that the Word-saturated psalm frames divine instruction as something to be actively, repeatedly requested.

context: I have declared my ways and thou heardest me, teach me thy statutes.
point_summary: Psalm 119:26's repeated petition "teach me thy statutes" exemplifies the kind of recorded scriptural request to God for instruction in His Word that the sermon holds up as a model for believers today.

context: Blessed art thou, O LORD: teach me thy statutes.
point_summary: Psalm 119:12 is cited as one of six instances in that psalm where the psalmist petitions God to "teach me thy statutes," illustrating the recurring biblical pattern of asking God to impart understanding of His Word.

context: Open thou mine eyes, that I may behold wondrous things out of thy law.
point_summary: Psalm 119:18 is cited as a recorded prayer for God to open the eyes of the believer to perceive wondrous things in His Word, illustrating the kind of request one ought to bring to God about Scripture.

context: Teach me good judgment and knowledge: for I have believed thy commandments.
point_summary: Psalm 119:66's request for "good judgment and knowledge" is presented as a model prayer grounded in belief in God's commandments, parallel to Solomon's request for wisdom.

context: teach me good judgment and knowledge, for I have believed thy commandments
point_summary: Psalm 119:66 presents the psalmist's request for good judgment and knowledge as a model prayer, paralleled with Solomon's request for wisdom, showing that desiring divinely taught discernment is itself a mark of faith.

context: the Word of God tells us in verse number 10 that there's another recorded request here
point_summary: Knowing God's will is insufficient without doing it — David's prayer in Psalm 143:10, "teach me to do thy will," distinguishes mere knowledge from obedient application.

context: teach me to do thy will. For thou art my God, thy spirit is good, lead me into the land of uprightness.
point_summary: David's prayer in Psalm 143:10 — "teach me to do thy will" — distinguishes mere knowledge of God's will from the active doing of it, pressing the need to apply, not merely possess, divine instruction.

context: In Psalm 90 and verse number 12, this is a psalm that is attributed to Moses, and the Bible tells us this.
point_summary: Attributed to Moses, Psalm 90:12 is cited as a recorded prayer-request that God would teach us to number our days, connecting awareness of life's brevity to the pursuit of wisdom.

context: so teach us what? Teach us to number our days. Teach us to number our days that we may apply our hearts unto wisdom.
point_summary: Numbering one's days—recognizing life's brevity as a vapor—is the scriptural basis for asking God to teach us to apply our hearts unto wisdom.

context: His greatness is unsearchable, according to Psalm 145.
point_summary: God's greatness is declared unsearchable in Psalm 145, meaning finite human minds can only scratch the surface of comprehending the infinite God, warranting daily prayer for deeper knowledge of Him.

context: The Lord hath sworn and will not repent, thou art a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek.
point_summary: Psalm 110:4's sworn, irrevocable declaration—"Thou art a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek"—is cited as a messianic prophecy that, like Noah's prophecy, progressively unfolds its meaning as redemptive history advances.

context: behold, how good and how pleasant it is for brethren to dwell together in unity. Psalm 133, in verse number one.
point_summary: Psalm 133:1 is cited as the biblical foundation establishing that unity among God's people is not merely desirable but is God's own declared plan for his people.

context: I want you to turn over to the book of Psalms, if you will, and look at Psalm 106.
point_summary: Psalm 106:34–38 is read as a historical case study illustrating what happens when God's people ignore the warning of 1 Corinthians 15:33—mingling with surrounding nations led Israel from cultural compromise to idolatry and child sacrifice.

context: They did not destroy the nations concerning whom the Lord commanded them
point_summary: Israel's failure to maintain separation from the Canaanite nations—being "mingled among the heathen"—serves as the historical illustration of how unchosen fellowship with the ungodly leads progressively to adopting their practices, idolatry, and divine judgment.

context: but were mingled among the heathen, and learned their works
point_summary: Israel's failure to maintain separation from the nations—described as being "mingled among the heathen"—is presented as the historical illustration of how corrupt associations lead to spiritual corruption and divine judgment.

context: and they served their idols, which were a snare unto them
point_summary: Israel's failure to maintain separation from the nations led to learning their works, serving their idols, and ultimately experiencing God's wrath — a historical illustration of evil communications corrupting good manners.

context: Yea, they sacrificed their sons and their daughters unto devils
point_summary: Israel's failure to maintain separation from the nations led progressively from mingling with the heathen, to learning their works, to serving their idols, to sacrificing their children — illustrating the corrupting trajectory warned against in 1 Corinthians 15:33.

context: and shed innocent blood, even the blood of their sons and of their daughters
point_summary: Israel's failure to maintain separation from the nations led progressively from mingling with the heathen, to learning their works, to idolatry, to shedding innocent blood — illustrating the corrupting trajectory warned against in 1 Corinthians 15:33.

context: Thus were they defiled with their own works, and went a-whoring with their own inventions
point_summary: Israel's failure to maintain separation from the nations—mingling with the heathen, learning their works, and ultimately being defiled—illustrates the corrupting progression warned against in 1 Corinthians 15:33.

context: Therefore was the wrath of the Lord kindled against His people
point_summary: Israel's failure to maintain separation from the nations—mingling with the heathen, learning their works, and serving their idols—illustrates the progressive corruption warned against in 1 Corinthians 15:33, culminating in God's wrath against His own people.

context: mingled among the heathen, and learned their works
point_summary: Israel's mingling with the heathen illustrates how ungodly fellowship progresses—from association, to learning worldly works, to outright idolatry—in a sequence the people never anticipated when they began that path.

context: and they served their idols
point_summary: Serving idols was not Israel's initial intention, but close fellowship with the heathen led progressively from mingling to learning their works to full idolatrous worship.

context: they sacrificed their sons and their daughters unto devils
point_summary: The progressive corruption of Israel — from mingling with the heathen, to learning their works, to serving their idols, to sacrificing their own children to devils — illustrates how ungodly fellowship leads to unthinkable spiritual ruin.

context: shed innocent blood, even the blood of their sons and of their daughters, whom they sacrificed unto the idols of Canaan
point_summary: The progressive corruption of Israel — from mingling with the heathen, to learning their works, to serving their idols, to sacrificing their own children — illustrates the devastating endpoint of ungodly fellowship left unchecked.

context: they didn't obey God in destroying the inhabitants
point_summary: Israel's failure to destroy the inhabitants as God commanded (v. 34) set in motion a downward spiral—mingling, learning pagan works, serving idols, and ultimately sacrificing their own children.

context: but we see in v. 35 where they mingled among the heathen.
point_summary: Mingling with the heathen was not Israel's intended destination but the first step on a slippery slope that ended in child sacrifice and divine wrath.

reference_text: they sacrificed their sons and their daughters unto devils
context: and now we find that the Bible says that they sacrificed their sons and their daughters unto devils.
point_summary: The tragic endpoint of Israel's gradual compromise with pagan neighbors was child sacrifice to devils, illustrating the devastating destination of a "slippery slope" that began with mere mingling among the heathen.

context: Notice v. 39, thus, were they defiled. They were defiled with their own works and went to whoring with their own inventions.
point_summary: Gradual compromise with surrounding culture leads to full defilement — Israel's "whoring with their own inventions" in v. 39 is presented as the inevitable destination of the slippery slope begun in v. 35.

context: And then we get to v. 40 and the Bible says, therefore, as a result of those few verses that we've just looked at, and we find what has happened and what has transpired in this terrible path, this slippery slope, if you will, that it says, therefore, was the wrath of the Lord kindled against His people, insomuch that He abhorred His own inheritance.
point_summary: Psalm 106:40's "therefore" marks the culmination of a progressive moral descent—from mingling with the heathen to child sacrifice—resulting in God's wrath kindled against and abhorrence of His own people.

context: Blessed is the man that walketh not in the counsel of the ungodly, nor standeth in the way of sinners, nor sitteth in the seat of the scornful.
point_summary: Psalm 1:1's descending progression—walking, standing, sitting—illustrates that spiritual compromise deepens incrementally, so avoiding the final seat of the scornful requires refusing the first step of ungodly counsel.

context: in the counsel of the ungodly nor standeth in the way of sinners, nor sitteth in the seat of the scornful
point_summary: Psalm 1:1's three-fold progression — walking, standing, sitting — illustrates that spiritual compromise advances in stages, so that avoiding the final seat of the scornful requires refusing the first step of ungodly counsel.

context: They believed not his word.
point_summary: Disbelief in God's word—"they believed not his word" (Ps. 106:24)—is identified as the root cause that preceded Israel's mingling with the heathen and the corruption that followed.

context: how that they were mingled among the heathen, that they learned their work, served their idols, sacrificed their sons and their daughters.
point_summary: Israel's tragic descent into mingling with the heathen, learning their works, and serving their idols (Ps. 106:34–40) is traced directly to their prior failure to believe God's word (v. 24), illustrating that disregard for Scripture precedes moral corruption.

context: And so the Bible tells us in Psalm 106 verse 24 before you ever even get to those things that they did not, they believed not his word.
point_summary: Disbelief in God's word is identified as the root cause preceding Israel's moral collapse into idolatry and defilement, warning that dismissing Scripture's commands about corrupt company follows the same pattern.

context: Notice what the psalmist says in Psalm 1 and verse 2. But his delight is in the law of the Lord. And in his law doth he meditate day and night.
point_summary: Delighting in God's Word through daily meditation—contrasted with Israel's unbelief in Psalm 106—is presented as the path to fruitfulness and the safeguard against moral corruption.

context: We don't want those results that we read of in Psalm 106
point_summary: Unbelief in God's word, cited from Psalm 106:24, is identified as the root failure that leaves people vulnerable to deception and its consequences.

context: nor do we want what the Bible says about the ungodly or not so at the end of Psalm 1
point_summary: Delighting in God's Word day and night—not merely reading it—is presented as the practical remedy against deception, producing stability and fruitfulness in the believer's life.

context: As we look at the example of your people as it's recorded in Psalm 106, it's tragic, it's unfortunate.
point_summary: Israel's gradual mingling with the heathen in Psalm 106 serves as a tragic warning that incremental ungodly associations led to learning their works, idolatry, and divine wrath—an end no one anticipated at the start.

context: Great peace have they which love thy law, and nothing shall offend them.
point_summary: Great peace belonging to those who love God's law — cited here as assurance that believers can experience divine, unshakeable peace amid the world's turmoil and the Antichrist's coming counterfeit peace.

context: trust not in oppression
point_summary: Wealth may legitimately increase through God's blessing, but Psalm 62:10 warns against setting one's heart upon it — the danger lies not in riches themselves but in misplaced affection.

context: Psalm 139, turn there, and just stop my message while I probably use one of these verses
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: O Lord, Thou hast searched me and known me. Thou hast searched me and known me. Thou knowest my down-sitting and mine uprising.
point_summary: God's exhaustive, personal knowledge of every individual — his sitting, rising, thoughts, and ways — is affirmed here as the foundational claim of Psalm 139 before the sermon's main message begins.

context: Thou understandest my thought afar off.
point_summary: God's omniscience extends to every thought, path, and way—He knows everything about a person without exception, even what no human companion can fully know.

context: Thou compassest my path and my lying down, and art acquainted with all my ways.
point_summary: God's exhaustive knowledge of every person—every path, every lying down, every way—means nothing about any individual is hidden from Him.

context: God, you're acquainted with all my ways. You know everything there is to know about me
point_summary: God's intimate acquaintance with every detail of a person's life—their routines, burdens, and ways—makes Him the only sufficient refuge when circumstances are beyond human control.

context: for there is not a word in my tongue, but lo, O Lord, thou knowest it altogether
point_summary: God's exhaustive knowledge of every word before it is spoken grounds the assurance that He fully understands burdens too overwhelming for human words to express.

context: thou hast beset me behind and before, and laid thine hand upon me
point_summary: God's complete surrounding of a person — behind, before, and with his hand upon them — is offered as the basis for bringing overwhelming burdens directly to him in prayer.

context: Such knowledge is too wonderful for me, it is too high, I cannot attain unto it
point_summary: God's exhaustive, personal knowledge of a believer—too vast to comprehend—becomes the very ground of desperate, honest prayer when human resources are exhausted.

context: whither shall I go from thy spirit? Or whither shall I flee from thy presence?
point_summary: God's omnipresence—that there is nowhere one can flee from His Spirit—is the announced preaching theme for the evening, grounded in the rhetorical questions of this verse.

context: If I ascend up into heaven, thou art there. If I make my bed in hell, behold, thou art there
point_summary: God's inescapable presence—whether in heaven or hell—is cited as the foundation for bringing overwhelming, uncontrollable burdens directly to Him, since no situation lies beyond His reach or knowledge.

context: If I take the wings of the morning and dwell in the uttermost parts of the sea
point_summary: God's inescapable presence—reaching to heaven, Sheol, and the uttermost sea—is presented as the unavoidable reality that frames a believer's cry for help when circumstances are utterly beyond personal control.

context: even there shall thy hand lead me, and thy right hand shall hold me
point_summary: God's guiding hand reaches even to the uttermost parts of the sea, meaning there is no circumstance of overwhelming personal crisis where His leading and holding are absent.

context: If I say, Surely the darkness shall cover me, even the night shall be light about me
point_summary: Even the darkness cannot hide a person from God, who sees through night as through day, making concealment from His presence impossible.

context: yea, the darkness hideth not from thee, but the night shineth as the day, the darkness as the light are both alike to thee.
point_summary: Even total darkness cannot conceal a person from God, who perceives night and day with equal clarity—a truth the preacher anchors in his own experience of helplessness before an uncontrollable situation.

context: For thou hast possessed my reins, thou hast covered me in my mother's womb.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Well, I will praise thee, for I am fearfully and wonderfully made. Precious are thy works, and that my soul knoweth right well.
point_summary: God's exhaustive, personal knowledge of every person — from formation in the womb to every unspoken word — is the ground on which the psalmist, and the preacher in a moment of helplessness, rests confident praise.

context: My substance was not hid from thee. When I was made in secret, and curiously wrought in the lowest parts of the earth,
point_summary: God's complete knowledge of a person extends even to their hidden formation before birth, as the psalmist declares his substance was not hidden from God when made in secret.

context: thine eyes did see my substance yet being unperfect, and in thy book all my members were written, which in continuance were fashioned, when as yet there was none of them.
point_summary: God's foreknowledge of every person is so complete that even before birth, all one's days were already written in His book, a truth the sermon frames as the foundation for trusting Him in circumstances beyond human control.

context: Verse 17 says, How precious also are thy thoughts unto me, O God! How great is the sum of them!
point_summary: God's thoughts toward his people are precious and innumerable—outnumbering the sand—a reality the psalmist connects to the assurance of God's constant, all-encompassing presence and knowledge.

context: If I should count them, they are more in number than the sand. When I awake, I am still with thee.
point_summary: God's thoughts toward his people are more numerous than the sand, and waking each morning means finding oneself still in his presence—a comfort the preacher anchors in personal helplessness before an all-knowing God.

context: Surely thou wilt slay the wicked, O God! Depart from me therefore, ye bloody men!
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: For they speak against thee wickedly, and thine enemies take thy name in vain.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Do not I hate them, O Lord, that hate thee? And am not I grieved with those that rise up against thee?
point_summary: Hatred of God's enemies (vv. 21–22) serves as the foil that drives the psalmist to turn the lens inward, surrendering himself to divine examination of his own heart in verse 23.

context: I hate them with a perfect hatred. I count them mine enemies.
point_summary: Psalm 139:22's declaration of perfect hatred toward God's enemies serves as the foil that drives the psalmist to self-examination in verse 23, turning outward judgment inward in total surrender to God's scrutiny.

context: But then there's a transition in verse 23. He said, Now, Lord, search me. Search me, O God, and know my heart. Try me and know my thoughts,
point_summary: Turning from condemning the wicked around him, the psalmist surrenders full self-examination to God, inviting divine scrutiny of his own heart rather than resting in outward comparison with others.

context: and see if there be any wicked way in me, and lead me in the way everlasting.
point_summary: Surrendering full control to God, the psalmist invites divine scrutiny of his own heart rather than dwelling on the wickedness of others, asking to be led in the everlasting way.

context: Whither shall I go from thy spirit? Or whither shall I flee from thy presence?
point_summary: God's omnipresence means there is nowhere to flee from His presence, making escape from Him impossible for those running outside His will.

context: let them praise the name of the Lord, for His name alone is excellent, His glory is above the earth and heaven
point_summary: God's names collectively reveal His excellent character, and Psalm 148:13 is cited to establish that His name alone is excellent and worthy of the praise His people offer as they study those names.

context: Before the mountains were brought forth, or ever thou hadst formed the earth and the world, even from everlasting to everlasting, thou art God.
point_summary: Psalm 90:2 is cited to affirm that God exists from everlasting to everlasting, having no beginning and no end, as part of the divine name El Olam, the Everlasting God.

context: Great is the Lord, and greatly to be praised.
point_summary: God's greatness, declared in Psalm 48:1, is cited as the scriptural basis for praising Him, reinforcing that knowing Him through His Word produces awe and willing submission to His guidance.

context: For this God is our God forever and ever. He will be our guide even unto death.
point_summary: God's incomparable greatness, studied through Scripture, moves the believer to embrace Him personally as guide for life's entire journey, even unto death.

context: Bible tells us that, speaking of God's word in Psalm 19, that by these, by God's precepts that thy servant is warned and in keeping of them there is great reward.
point_summary: Psalm 19's declaration that God's servant is warned by His precepts and finds great reward in keeping them underscores that divine counsel—like any warning—profits nothing unless heeded.

context: O God thou knowest my foolishness and my sins are
point_summary: God's omniscience renders all attempts to conceal sin futile, as Psalm 69:5's declaration—"my sins are not hid from thee"—confirms what David himself learned through bitter experience.

context: David under the inspiration of the Holy Spirit of God would write this Psalm, he says, oh God, thou knowest my foolishness and my sins are not hid from thee.
point_summary: David's confession in Psalm 69—"my sins are not hid from thee"—is cited as Spirit-inspired testimony that no sin, however elaborately concealed, is hidden from God.

context: Moses, speaking of the fact that three score and ten, if by reason of strength, four score, 70 or 80 years, you look, you see that in Psalm 90.
point_summary: Psalm 90's reference to a human lifespan of seventy or eighty years is cited as evidence of the post-flood decline in longevity traced through the biblical genealogies from Noah to Moses.

context: David and he speaks in Psalm 34 about how the Lord delivered him out of all of his troubles
point_summary: Even amid life's compounding troubles, God's repeated deliverances—as David celebrated in Psalm 34—build the believer's confidence that God remains faithful through every successive trial.

context: when my heart is overwhelmed, what did the psalmist say in Psalm 61, lead me to the rock that is higher than I
point_summary: When the heart is overwhelmed by insurmountable problems, Psalm 61:2's cry — "lead me to the rock that is higher than I" — points to God as the faithful refuge that daily immersion in His Word prepares believers to run to.

reference_text: Be still and know that I am God
context: Be still and know that I am God, and He has promises, and He will never, ever fail on any promise that He's ever given us, amen?
point_summary: Amid famine and difficulty, the command to "be still and know that I am God" grounds the call to keep eyes fixed on God, trusting His promises, presence, and provision rather than yielding to despair.

context: David said in Psalm 13, how long, O Lord, will Thou forget me, forever?
point_summary: Psalm 13:1's cry — "How long, O Lord, wilt Thou forget me, forever?" — illustrates the temptation in difficulty to feel abandoned by God, countered here by the assurance that He never forgets His people.

context: Make haste, O God, to deliver me. Make haste to help me, O Lord.
point_summary: David's urgent double cry — "make haste" — in a single verse reflects the psalmist's desperate need for God's deliverance and help, whose timing differs from our own.

context: I will lift up mine eyes into the hills from whence cometh my help.
point_summary: Psalm 121:1–2 is briefly quoted to affirm that the God David urgently calls upon in Psalm 70 is the same creator God who is the source of all help.

context: My help cometh from the Lord, which made heaven and earth.
point_summary: Help comes from the Lord who made heaven and earth — the Creator's identity as maker grounds confidence that he is both able to deliver and able to help.

context: make haste, O God, to deliver me. Make haste to help me, O Lord.
point_summary: David's urgent double cry of "make haste" in a single verse reflects the psalmist's desperate need for God's deliverance and help, set against the recognition that God's timetable differs from our own.

context: Let them be ashamed and confounded that seek after my soul. Let them be turned backward and put to confusion that desire my hurt.
point_summary: Verse 2 reveals the urgency behind David's prayer in verse 1, showing that enemies actively seeking his soul and desiring his hurt drove him to cry out with such desperate haste.

context: Let them be turned back for a reward of their shame that say, aha, aha.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: make haste unto me, O God. Thou art my help and my deliverer. O Lord, make no tarrying.
point_summary: Psalm 70:1's cry "make haste, O God, to deliver me" is read as David's urgent prayer for deliverance from those actively seeking to destroy him.

context: that those that love thy salvation, that we are to say continually, let God be magnified.
point_summary: Those who love God's salvation — whether David's temporal deliverance or the believer's eternal rescue from sin's penalty — are called to say continually, "Let God be magnified."

context: in Psalm 70, and thinking about those that would love the salvation, the deliverance, that temporal reference that is being made in Psalm 70, that they are to say continually, let God be magnified.
point_summary: David's call in Psalm 70 for those who love God's salvation to say continually "let God be magnified" is applied to believers saved by Christ's blood, who have even greater reason to declare God's greatness.

context: The psalmist says, great is the Lord, and greatly to be praised in Psalm 48 and verse number one.
point_summary: God's greatness is declared in Scripture itself, as the psalmist's own words—"great is the Lord, and greatly to be praised"—ground the sermon's call to magnify Him.

context: The psalmist says this in Psalm 135, I know that the Lord is great.
point_summary: Psalm 135's declaration "I know that the Lord is great" grounds the sermon's call to magnify God—affirming that His greatness is a revealed, knowable reality Scripture itself proclaims.

context: through the text that we have here in Psalm 70 this morning, that we're to let God be magnified
point_summary: Psalm 70's call to say "continually, let God be magnified" is expounded as a standing resolve for the redeemed to declare and exalt God's greatness before a generation that does not know it.

context: Notice you're in Psalm 70, but look back in Psalm 69 in verse number 30. Notice what the psalmist says here. Again, another psalm that's attributed to David. It says in Psalm 69 in verse number 30, I will praise the name of God with a song
point_summary: The psalmist's opening words "I will" in Psalm 69:30 are read as the language of settled determination—a resolved, fixed purpose to praise and magnify God with song and thanksgiving.

context: Oh, magnify the Lord with me and let us exalt His name together.
point_summary: David's call in Psalm 34:3 to magnify the Lord "together" grounds the sermon's distinction between individual and corporate responsibility to declare God's greatness.

context: 'The humble shall hear thereof and be glad.'
point_summary: Magnifying God makes Him known to others, so that the humble who hear of His greatness are made glad.

context: 'I will extol thee, my God, exalt, to magnify. 'O King, and I will bless thy name forever and ever.'
point_summary: Psalm 145:1 is cited to illustrate the psalmist's personal, daily resolve to extol and magnify God, presented as the model determination every believer should share.

context: as it's revealed to us even in Psalm 78
point_summary: Psalm 78 is referenced as a backdrop illustrating the duty of God's people to make His greatness known across generations, though its specific content is not yet expounded in this segment.

context: Remember back in Psalm 70 in verse number 5, He said, I am poor and needy.
point_summary: Magnifying God keeps the believer humble, because the posture of declaring His greatness—like the psalmist's confession "I am poor and needy"—counters the Laodicean pride of self-sufficiency.

context: God speaks to Moses about the threescore and ten and so on and so forth, if by reason of strength, fourscore
point_summary: The decreasing lifespans after the flood, culminating in Moses' "threescore and ten," provide the backdrop against which Abraham's 175 years stands as a notably "good old age" fulfilling God's promise.

reference_text: Cast thy burden upon the Lord and he shall sustain thee
context: Cast thy burden upon the Lord and he shall sustain thee.
point_summary: Casting burdens on the Lord in prayer is contrasted with Jacob's continued self-reliance, illustrating the human tendency to pray yet still withhold full trust from God.

context: search me, as the psalmist says in Psalm 139. Search me, Lord.
point_summary: Psalm 139's "search me" prayer is held up as the honest posture believers should bring to self-examination—the opposite of Rachel's and Achan's concealment from those who might expose what they knew was hidden.

context: Turn, if you will, to Psalm 69. In Psalm 69, it's a psalm that's ascribed to David. I want you to consider the truth of verse number 5, Psalm 69 and verse number 5. Notice what the Word of God says.
point_summary: God knows every hidden sin without exception — a truth the sermon draws from David's confession that his foolishness and sins are not concealed from God, however successfully they may be hidden from others.

reference_text: he hath daily loadeth me with benefits
context: we could think and very honestly agree with what the psalmist says when he said that he hath daily loadeth me with benefits
point_summary: Daily loading with benefits is cited from the Psalms to affirm that God has already been abundantly providing for believers, setting the context for praying "give us day by day our daily bread."

context: Psalm 23, a precious passage of scripture, the Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want.
point_summary: Psalm 23:1 grounds the petition for daily bread in the character of God as the good shepherd who leads and cares for his sheep, making him the true provider behind every earthly source of supply.

context: I have been young and now am old, yet have I not seen the righteous forsaken nor his seed begging bread.
point_summary: David's personal testimony that he had never witnessed the righteous forsaken or reduced to begging bread is cited as evidence that God faithfully provides, warning believers against taking that provision for granted through rote, mindless prayer.

context: The testimony of David in Psalm 37 and verse 25
point_summary: David's testimony in Psalm 37:25 is invoked as a witness to God's faithful provision, grounding the call to remain regularly mindful that God is our provider and to praise Him accordingly.

context: Think about these verses. Turn to the book of Psalms, if you will, Psalm 27.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Wait on the Lord, be of good courage, and he shall strengthen thine heart. Wait, I say, on the Lord
point_summary: Waiting on the Lord requires active courage and trust, as Psalm 27:14 is cited alongside parallel psalms to establish that God's perfect timing demands patient, expectant faith from his people.

context: Our soul waiteth for the Lord. He is our help and our shield.
point_summary: God's people are called to wait on the Lord because He is their help and shield—a posture of active trust while anticipating His perfect timing.

context: Truly, my soul waiteth upon God. From him cometh my salvation.
point_summary: God's people are called to active, expectant waiting—trusting that salvation, defense, and ultimate expectation come from Him alone while His perfect timing unfolds.

context: My soul, wait thou only upon God.
point_summary: Waiting on God is grounded in expectation directed solely toward Him, with the soul's anticipation fixed on God as the one from whom all fulfillment comes.

context: the heathen, they rage according to Psalm chapter number 2. But you know what God's doing? He's sitting on his throne in Psalm 2.
point_summary: Psalm 2's portrait of God enthroned while the heathen rage is cited to contrast worldly chaos with divine sovereignty, grounding the believer's perseverance in God's untroubled, reigning position.

context: the truth of the psalmist in Psalm 23, a familiar passage of Scripture that we often refer to to be a source of great encouragement to us and strength to us, that the Word of God says the Lord is my shepherd.
point_summary: The security of eternal life is illustrated by Psalm 23:1's declaration "the Lord is my shepherd," showing that the believer's relationship with God is constant, not probationary.

context: And his presence, the psalmist said in Psalm 1611, is fullness of joy.
point_summary: Fullness of joy is found in God's presence, grounding the sermon's claim that fellowship and obedient living restore the joy sin steals away.

context: for I know that the Lord is great, that our Lord is above all gods
point_summary: Psalm 135:5 grounds the sermon's "phase one" theme—that every believer must move from inherited assumption to personal, substantiated conviction that the Lord is great above all gods.

context: the psalmist makes that second statement that is so impactful
point_summary: Paired with Psalm 135:5's call to *know* God's greatness, Psalm 145:6 supplies the complementary imperative: the one who knows must also *declare* that greatness outwardly.

reference_text: This is the day the Lord hath made; we will rejoice and be glad in it.
context: The psalmist said this. This is the day the Lord hath made. I will rejoice and be glad in it.
point_summary: Rejoicing is a purposeful, determined choice—not a reaction to favorable circumstances—because God himself is the sufficient reason to be glad in every day he has made.

context: As we're gonna think about this statement and I mentioned by way of introduction where the psalmist says in Psalm 135 in verse number five, for I know that the Lord is great.
point_summary: Psalm 135:5 is paired with Jethro's declaration to show that God's people should be able to voice a settled, personal conviction of God's greatness, unshakeable by any outside persuasion.

context: The fool hath said in his heart, there is no God.
point_summary: Belief shapes behavior: the fool's denial of God in his heart explains his foolish living, just as genuine belief in God's greatness must visibly align one's actions with one's declarations.

context: In Psalm 78, the Bible talks about they limited the Holy One of Israel.
point_summary: Israel's unbelief is identified as the limiting factor in God's redemptive purposes, citing Psalm 78's declaration that they "limited the Holy One of Israel" as the reason mighty works were withheld.

context: Psalm 90 is a prayer of Moses. We don't have time to look at the entirety of the Psalm this evening, but I want you to begin looking with me in verse number nine. He says, for all our days are passed away in thy wrath.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: We spend our years as a tale that is told.
point_summary: Moses' prayer that human life passes "as a tale that is told" is applied as a framework for examining how each day writes an entry into one's personal story before God.

context: The days of our years are three score years and 10. And if by reason of strength, they be four score years.
point_summary: Human life is likened to "a tale that is told," using Moses' words in Psalm 90:10 to prompt reflection on the ongoing narrative each person writes through daily choices and relationships.

context: Who knoweth the power of thine anger even according to thy fear. So is thy wrath.
point_summary: God's wrath frames the brevity of human life, and Moses' image of years spent "as a tale that is told" is applied to prompt reflection on what each person's life story is actually recording.

context: So teach us to number our days that we may apply our hearts unto wisdom.
point_summary: Numbering one's days rightly—recognizing life as a brief, unfolding story—should move believers to apply their hearts to wisdom rather than to carnal rivalry.

context: He is our refuge and strength, a very present help in trouble.
point_summary: Because God is refuge, strength, and ever-present help in trouble, his people need not fear any catastrophe—a confidence the psalm reinforces with its repeated "Selah" and closing refrain that the Lord of hosts is with us.

context: therefore, will not we fear, though the earth be removed, and though the mountains be carried into the midst of the sea,
point_summary: Because God is refuge, strength, and ever-present help in trouble, his people need not fear even catastrophic upheaval—a confidence the psalm reinforces with its repeated "Selah" refrain calling for deep meditation on that truth.

context: though the waters thereof roar and be troubled, though the mountains shake with the swelling thereof? Selah.
point_summary: Because God is refuge, strength, and ever-present help, his people need not fear even catastrophic upheaval—a confidence the psalm reinforces with its repeated "Selah," calling the reader to meditate on that assurance.

context: the Lord of hosts, notice this, is with us. The God of Jacob is our refuge. Selah.
point_summary: The repeated "Selah" refrain of Psalm 46—"the Lord of hosts is with us; the God of Jacob is our refuge"—grounds fearlessness in God's abiding presence, confirmed by His promise in Hebrews never to leave or forsake His people.

context: be still and know that I am God. I will be exalted among the heathen. I will be exalted in the earth.
point_summary: Stillness before God—"be still and know that I am God"—is grounded in the repeated assurance that the Lord of hosts is present with His people and will not forsake them.

context: the Lord of hosts is with us. The God of Jacob is our refuge. Selah.
point_summary: God's repeated assurance — "the Lord of hosts is with us" — grounds fearlessness in the knowledge of who He is: refuge, strength, and ever-present help.

context: David says this... I want to read to you what David would say in Psalm 69 and verse 20. He says that, every reproach hath broken my heart, I am full of heaviness, and I looked for some to take pity, but there was none, and for comforters, but I found none.
point_summary: David's lament that he sought comforters and found none serves as a warning that no local congregation should leave its faint-hearted members without someone to come alongside them.

reference_text: restore unto me the joy of thy salvation
context: Do you remember what David said in Psalm 51, restore unto me the joy of thy salvation?
point_summary: Unconfessed sin robs a believer of joy without forfeiting salvation, as David's cry in Psalm 51 to have joy "restored" shows he needed restoration to fellowship, not regeneration.

context: let the words of my mouth and the meditation of my heart be acceptable in thy sight, O Lord, my strength and my Redeemer.
point_summary: Secret sins cannot be hidden from God, who hears every word and knows every thought, making the psalmist's prayer for acceptable words and meditations a response to living under God's total awareness.

context: Wherewithal shall a young man cleanse his way? By taking heed thereto according to thy word.
point_summary: Memorizing and hiding God's Word in the heart—cited from Psalm 119:9 and 11—is presented as a practical means of resisting the devil so that he will flee.

context: Thy word have I hid in mine heart
point_summary: Hiding God's Word in the heart, drawn from Psalm 119:11, is presented as a practical act of resistance that equips believers to repel the devil's temptations.

context: Remember, we've mentioned on a couple of occasions the truth of Psalm 135 coupled with the truth of Psalm 145, where the psalmist declares, I know that the Lord is great.
point_summary: Psalm 135's declaration that God is great, paired with Psalm 145's affirmation that His greatness is unsearchable, illustrates that knowing God's greatness and fully comprehending it are not contradictory but complementary truths.

context: But then he says the greatness of God is unsearchable.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Well, according to Psalm 19, the Bible speaks of itself in saying that by them thy servant is warned.
point_summary: Psalm 19's self-testimony that God's word warns his servant grounds the pastoral duty to protect the flock by measuring all teaching against Scripture.

context: thinking about the truth of Psalm 90, that psalm has been ascribed to Moses
point_summary: Psalm 90, ascribed to Moses, teaches that life passes swiftly "as a tale that is told," making the numbering of our days essential to gaining a heart of wisdom.

context: Yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil. Why? For thou art with me. Thy rod and thy staff, they comfort me.
point_summary: Even in the valley of the shadow of death, God's presence is the source of comfort—a comfort the sermon applies to the believer's awareness that the Lord accompanies them everywhere they go.

reference_text: Whither can I flee from his presence?
context: Whither can I flee from his presence? What's the answer? I can't. I can't. I can't.
point_summary: God's omnipresence, from which no one can flee, is presented as the defining reality that shaped Joseph's righteous conduct even in pagan Egypt, in contrast to Judah's behavior in Genesis 38.

context: Great is the Lord, and greatly to be praised
point_summary: Psalm 48:1's declaration that the Lord is "great and greatly to be praised" is cited as a doxological anchor, grounding praise in God's nature and character rather than in circumstance alone.

context: For this God is our God forever and ever. He will be our guide even unto death.
point_summary: Psalm 48:14's declaration that "this God is our God forever and ever" grounds the assurance that the God of the universe is personally and individually committed to each believer, even unto death.

context: 'They that sow in tears shall reap in joy.'
point_summary: Faithful gospel witness, framed through the image of sowing in tears, demands genuine emotional burden for the lost—not mere duty—before the promised harvest of joy can come.

context: 'He that goeth forth and weepeth, bearing precious seed, shall doubtless come again with rejoicing, bringing his sheaves with him.'
point_summary: Faithful gospel witness, framed through Psalm 126:6, requires genuine emotional burden—weeping over lost souls—as the condition that precedes the promised harvest of rejoicing.

context: The psalmist in Psalm 119 speaks about the wickedness that he observes and the tears that are response.
