point_summary: Grief over observed wickedness, expressed in tears, is presented as the psalmist's model for how the believer's heart should be moved by sin in the world around him.

context: if you read about the confession of David in Psalms 51 in Psalm 51 you read a brokenhearted man that receives forgiveness and restoration
point_summary: Psalm 51 is cited as David's confession after his sin with Bathsheba, illustrating that yielding to temptation costs joy, yet a merciful God grants forgiveness and restoration to the brokenhearted.

context: The psalmist cries out in Psalm 19, in verse number 10, speaking about the law of the Lord, which is perfect. He says, more to be desired they than gold, yea, than much fine gold, sweeter also than the honey in the honeycomb.
point_summary: Scripture's own testimony that God's Word is more desirable than gold and sweeter than honey stands as a rebuke to the church's widespread neglect of daily Bible reading.

context: The psalmist says this in Psalm 119, in verse 162, what a great verse of scripture. He says, I rejoice at thy word. And here's the description, the way in which he's rejoicing, as one that findeth great spoil.
point_summary: Amid alarming statistics on biblical neglect, the psalmist's joy over God's word—likened to finding great spoil—stands as the standard against which the church's spiritual poverty is measured.

context: The Bible says this in Psalm chapter number 1, blessed is the man that walketh not in the counsel of the ungodly, nor standeth in the way of sinners, nor sitteth in the seat of the scornful.
point_summary: Psalm 1:1's threefold progression—walking, standing, sitting—frames Lot's descent into Sodom as a warning that God's people can and must resist conforming to a corrupt world.

context: Look at Psalm 133 and verse number one. Psalm 133 and verse number one.
point_summary: Unity among God's people is declared both good and pleasant, establishing the divine ideal against which the Corinthian church's divisions and contentions stand as a tragic failure.

context: Behold, how good and how pleasant it is for brethren to dwell together in unity.
point_summary: Unity among believers is described as both good and pleasant in Psalm 133:1, establishing that division and contention fall short of God's clear desire and intent for relationships.

context: It is a good thing. It is a pleasant thing is what the psalmist said in Psalm 133, for brethren to dwell together in unity.
point_summary: Unity among believers is declared by the psalmist to be both good and pleasant, framing the sermon's practical goal of resolving conflict so that brethren may dwell together.

reference_text: The psalmist said, I will set no wicked thing before mine eyes.
context: The psalmist said, I will set no wicked thing before mine eyes.
point_summary: The psalmist's resolve — "I will set no wicked thing before mine eyes" — is applied as a standard for guarding the thought life against harmful input through reading, watching, and listening.

context: I'm thankful that the Bible tells us that his mercy endureth forever.
point_summary: God's mercy endures forever, a truth invoked here to ground Paul's prayer for Timothy—that divine compassion sustains those in distress, including pastors facing hardship in ministry.

reference_text: doorkeeper, is what the book of Psalms says
context: when you're a doorkeeper, is what the book of Psalms says, in the house of the Lord.
point_summary: Even the humblest acts of church service—cutting grass, vacuuming floors, keeping the door—are honored by the Psalms' own language as a privilege before the King of kings.

context: this is the day the Lord hath made. We will rejoice and be glad in it.
point_summary: Each day is a gift from God to be received with rejoicing, grounding gratitude for physical life in the recognition that God is its author and giver.

reference_text: He knows our frame. He knows that we're but dust.
context: He knows our frame. He knows that we're but dust. He knows that we're frail and he comforts us.
point_summary: God's intimate knowledge of human frailty—that he knows our frame and that we are dust—grounds the assurance that his comfort meets believers precisely where they are in suffering.

context: In the Lord put I my trust
point_summary: Trusting God rather than oneself demands a deliberate, active decision—the word "put" in Psalm 11:1 eliminating all passivity from the act of faith.

context: To just be still and know that you are God.
point_summary: Deliberate, protected time in God's presence—being still and knowing He is God—is what produces a visible witness that others perceive as divine accompaniment.

context: Psalms, you don't have to turn there, but in Psalm 19, in verse number 11, as the psalmist
point_summary: Scripture itself declares that God's Word serves to warn His servant, a function the sermon ties directly to Paul's stated ministry of warning every man.

context: moreover by them, speaking of the words of God, the law of God, is thy servant warned, and in keeping of them there is great reward.
point_summary: Scripture itself serves as the instrument of divine warning, as Psalm 19:11 is cited to ground Paul's ministry of warning every man in the God-given purpose of His Word to reveal what pleases and displeases Him.

context: Psalm 121 that the Lord would help us to lift up our eyes into the hills from whence cometh our help
point_summary: When the king confesses he cannot help and only God can, Psalm 121:1 is invoked to contrast human helplessness with the assurance that our help comes from the Lord who made heaven and earth.

context: Knowing that our help cometh from the Lord which made heaven and earth
point_summary: When the king confesses he cannot help, Psalm 121:2 is invoked to contrast royal helplessness with the assurance that true help comes from the Lord who made heaven and earth.

context: he is a very present help in the time of trouble
point_summary: God is described as "a very present help in the time of trouble," contrasted with the helpless king who could offer nothing, underscoring that true help belongs to the Lord alone.

context: ...Maybe like we see in the Psalms, in Psalm 12, Lord help, help Lord...
point_summary: Psalm 12:1's brief cry "Help, Lord" is offered as a model of the instantaneous, wordless-but-real prayer Nehemiah likely prayed in the split second before answering the king.

context: the Lord is my shepherd
point_summary: David's declaration "the Lord is my shepherd" is cited to confirm the believer's identity as one of Christ's sheep, grounding the sermon's point about eternal security in John 10:27–29.

context: In Psalm 84, notice in verse number 10, the Bible says, for a day in thy court is better than a thousand. I had rather be a doorkeeper in the house of my God than to dwell in the tents of wickedness.
point_summary: Even the humblest act of service—being a doorkeeper in God's house—is a privilege and delight surpassing anything the world offers, because serving God is the result of salvation, not the means of it.

context: The psalmist says here in Psalm 84, what he would rather do, be a doorkeeper in the house of my God.
point_summary: Even the humblest role in God's service — illustrated by the psalmist's preference to be a doorkeeper in God's house over dwelling in wickedness — is a privilege and delight, not a burden.

context: I want you to see what the psalmist says in Psalm 100 in verse number two.
point_summary: Serving the Lord is to be done with gladness because it is a privilege—a perspective reinforced by Psalm 100:2's command to "serve the Lord with gladness."

reference_text: restore unto me the joy of thy salvation
context: That's why David says, restore unto me the joy of thy salvation.
point_summary: Sin functions as a joy thief, which is why David's cry in Psalm 51:12 to have the joy of salvation restored presupposes that sin had already stripped it away.

context: in his presence is fullness of joy
point_summary: God's promise of his presence with Jacob on the journey to Egypt is grounded in the truth that, as the psalmist declares, fullness of joy is found in that presence.

context: A larger place here in terms of the book of Psalms, if you will, Psalm 73. I want to look at a quick reference here in Psalm 73 as I try to package together these first two thoughts from Genesis 46, promises for the journey and protection for the journey.
point_summary: God's continual presence, guiding hand, and ultimate reception into glory—drawn from Psalm 73:23–25—are offered as the scriptural anchor uniting the sermon's twin themes of divine promises and protection for the journey.

context: The Bible says in Psalm 73, verse 23, nevertheless, I am continually with thee. Thou hast holden me by my right hand.
point_summary: God's continual presence, guiding hand, and ultimate reception to glory are held together in Psalm 73:23 as the scriptural anchor for the twin themes of divine promise and protection on life's journey.

context: Thou shalt guide me with thy counsel, and afterward receive me to glory.
point_summary: God's guidance and protection on life's journey—illustrated in Jacob's family traveling to Egypt—are grounded in the psalmist's confidence that God holds, guides, and ultimately receives his own to glory.

context: Verse 25, notice what the psalmist says, Whom have I in heaven but thee? There is none upon earth that I desire beside thee.
point_summary: God alone is the soul's sufficient treasure and strength, as the psalmist's declaration "whom have I in heaven but thee" anchors the sermon's call to gratitude for divine presence and protection on life's journey.

context: My flesh and my heart faileth, but God is the strength of my heart and my portion forever.
point_summary: God is declared the strength of the heart and portion forever, anchoring the sermon's twin themes of divine promise and protection for the journey.

context: For lo, they that are far from thee shall perish. Thou hast destroyed all them that go a-whoring from thee.
point_summary: Those who remain far from God perish, while drawing near to God in trust is declared the believer's good—framing the journey's protection as inseparable from nearness to God.

context: But it is good for me to draw near to God. I've put my trust in the Lord God that I may declare all thy works.
point_summary: Drawing near to God is declared the believer's good, grounding both divine promises and divine protection as the twin provisions sustaining the journey of faith.

context: the interesting thing in Psalm 19 and in verses 7 through 10 describes the Bible, the law of the Lord is perfect
point_summary: Psalm 19:7–11 is cited to establish that Scripture's perfection serves a warning function, and that heeding those warnings is in the believer's best interest.

context: And then you come to verse number 11, and the Bible says that by them, moreover by them is thy servant warned.
point_summary: Obedience to Scripture's warnings is presented as being in the believer's best interest, with Psalm 19:11's phrase "by them is thy servant warned" framing the Word of God as God's living voice of caution.

context: Sing unto God, sing praises to His name. Extol Him that writeth upon the heavens by His name, JAH, and rejoice before Him.
point_summary: "JAH" in Psalm 68:4 is identified as an abbreviated derivative of Jehovah, the proper personal name of God, equivalent to Yahweh, meaning the self-existent one.

context: The Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want.
point_summary: Jehovah Ra, the compound name rendered "the Lord is my shepherd" in Psalm 23:1, reveals God as the divine shepherd who fully provides for His sheep, leaving them in want of nothing.

context: The Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want
point_summary: Psalm 23:1 reveals the compound name Jehovah-Ra, meaning the Lord is my shepherd, a title David—himself a shepherd—would have understood as declaring God's personal care and provision for His people.

reference_text: the Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want
context: Jehovah-Ra, that the Lord is my shepherd, and that means he cares for me, he takes care of my needs, he provides for me, he says, I shall not want
point_summary: David's firsthand experience as a shepherd who protected his flock informs the personal declaration "the Lord is my shepherd," revealing that Jehovah-Ra actively cares for, provides for, and protects his people.

context: the Lord is my shepherd
point_summary: David's shepherd background illuminates the personal, peace-giving nature of Jehovah-Ra: the Lord as *my* shepherd who provides, protects, and leads sheep who need not know the way themselves.

reference_text: let the redeemed of the Lord say so
context: The Bible says, let the redeemed of the Lord say so
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

reference_text: let everything that hath breath, praise the Lord
context: The Bible says, let everything that hath breath, praise the Lord, praise ye the Lord
point_summary: Every breathing creature is called to praise, grounding the sermon's Thanksgiving theme in the universal obligation of worship voiced in this verse.

context: memorizing Psalm 100, and what a blessing to hide the Word of God in your heart
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: why do the heathen rage and the people imagine a vain thing?
point_summary: Psalm 2:1's depiction of earthly kings raging against the Lord and His anointed is presented as prophetically alive today, underscoring Christ's supreme rank as King of kings.

context: and cast away their cords from us. Listen, we're living in a time where there's so much prophetic nature of this particular portion of Scripture. The kings of the earth set themselves, the rulers take counsel together against the Lord and against His anointed
point_summary: Psalm 2:3's depiction of earth's rulers conspiring against the Lord is read as prophetic confirmation that Christ's sovereignty as King of kings cannot be overthrown by any human counsel or plan.

context: but He, verse 4, that sitteth in the heavens shall laugh. The Lord shall have them in derision.
point_summary: God's laughter at the raging kings of the earth (Ps. 2:4) demonstrates that no earthly ruler can conquer, defeat, or alter His plan, confirming His rank as King of kings and Lord of lords.

context: Yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil. Why? For Thou art with Me. Thy rod, Thy staff, they comfort Me.
point_summary: Even in the valley of the shadow of death, the Shepherd's presence with His sheep is the ground of fearlessness and comfort.

context: Whither shall I go from Thy Spirit? For whither shall I flee from Thy presence?
point_summary: Psalm 139:7's rhetorical question — "Whither shall I flee from Thy presence?" — is cited as scriptural proof that God's omnipresence makes escape from Him impossible, grounding the believer's assurance that He is always with them.

context: as the psalmist said in Psalm 33, I stand in awe of you
point_summary: Psalm 33's declaration "I stand in awe of you" is invoked as the fitting posture of worship before a God whose greatness, though knowable, remains ultimately unsearchable.

context: You remember the wonderful 23rd Psalm? He restoreth my soul.
point_summary: Psalm 23's declaration that God "restoreth my soul" is cited as scriptural grounding for the principle that church discipline's purpose is restoration, not excommunication.

context: Psalm 147 and verse number 5, notice here what the psalmist says...
point_summary: God's infinite greatness is displayed in His numbering and naming every star, a detail the sermon connects to His transcendence and yet His desire to be personally known by humanity.

reference_text: He telleth the number of the stars; he calleth them all by their names.
context: And here our God says that He telleth the number of the stars, He calleth them all by their names.
point_summary: God's act of numbering and naming every star establishes His infinite understanding, which grounds the sermon's call to accept the Trinity by faith rather than demand full rational comprehension.

reference_text: Great is our Lord, and of great power: his understanding is infinite.
context: Look at verse 5, great is our Lord and of great power, His understanding is infinite.
point_summary: God's infinite understanding, cited from Psalm 147:5, establishes why the Trinity cannot be fully explained by human reason and must be accepted by faith from Scripture.

context: The psalmist said that we are to give thanks at the remembrance of His holiness.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Thy throne, O God, is forever and ever. A scepter of righteousness is the scepter of thy kingdom.
point_summary: Hebrews 1:8–9's quotation of Psalm 45:6 is presented as the Father's own address to the Son, establishing that the Father himself calls the Son "God."

context: Thou hast loved righteousness and hated iniquity. Therefore God, even thy God, hath anointed the oil of gladness above thy fellows.
point_summary: Psalm 45:7, quoted in Hebrews 1:8–9, is presented as the Father's own words addressing the Son as "God," demonstrating that both Father and Son equally bear the divine title.

context: the heavens declare the glory of God, and the firmament showeth His handiwork
point_summary: Creation's declaration that "the heavens declare the glory of God" is drawn as a parallel to the Trinity, since the universe consists of space, time, and matter—three distinct elements that together form one inseparable whole.

context: as we say as the psalmist does in Psalm 139, God search me oh God. Search me oh God. And show me if there be any wicked way in my life.
point_summary: God's work of personal conviction—bringing suppressed sin to the forefront—is grounded in the psalmist's invitation for divine self-examination: "Search me, O God, and show me if there be any wicked way in me."

context: in Psalm 37 and verse number 30, with regard to this thought of whether it's helpful. In Psalm 37 and verse number 30, the psalmist says this, speaking of the righteous man, that the mouth of the righteous speaketh wisdom and his tongue talketh of judgment.
point_summary: The righteous man's wisdom-speaking mouth is rooted in God's law dwelling in his heart, so that Scripture-saturated living produces Scripture-saturated speech.

context: It's interesting because verse number 31 gives us the key as to why he speaks wisdom and he talks of judgment, and that is because the law of his God is in his heart. None of his steps shall slide.
point_summary: A heart saturated with God's law is the root cause of wise and righteous speech, so that Scripture-filled living naturally produces Scripture-shaped words.

context: And we're there in Psalm 37, look over in chapter 35, and notice in verse number 28, Psalm 35 and verse number 28, the Bible says,
point_summary: Tongue-speech that declares God's righteousness and praise all day long is held up as the model of inspiring words, contrasted implicitly with complaining speech.

context: Psalm 77 and verse number 12 says it this way, I will meditate also of all thy work and talk of all thy doings.
point_summary: Meditating on and speaking of God's works and doings is presented as an inspiring, encouraging use of the tongue that stirs others toward praise.

context: Let the words of my mouth and the meditation of my heart be acceptable in thy sight, O Lord, my strength and my redeemer.
point_summary: Words and heart-intentions together must be acceptable to God, framing speech as an act of worship directed toward the Lord as strength and redeemer.

context: that the words of our mouth, the meditation of our heart would be acceptable, O Lord, in thy sight
point_summary: Psalm 19:14 is presented as the governing motto for the sermon, calling believers to make acceptable words and heart-meditations before God the conscious standard of daily life.

context: the heavens declare the glory of God, the firmament showeth forth his handiwork
point_summary: General revelation through creation declares God's existence, but Scripture alone reveals his attributes, character, and saving love in specific, knowable detail.

context: the Lord is my shepherd
point_summary: The personal possessive "my" in "the Lord is my shepherd" distinguishes individual saving relationship from general knowledge of Christ as shepherd, illustrating the blessedness of personal redemption.

context: In Psalm 16 and 11, the Bible says that in thy presence is fullness of joy.
point_summary: Psalm 16:11's declaration that fullness of joy is found in God's presence is cited as the scriptural basis for joy being a direct product of fellowship with God, sustaining believers even through hardship.

context: The Bible says in the book of Psalms that with thee there is forgiveness.
point_summary: Forgiveness originates with God alone — "with thee there is forgiveness" (Ps. 130:4) — and is available to guilty humanity only through the blood of Jesus Christ.

context: Oh, How Love I Thy Law
point_summary: Psalm 119:97's declaration of love for God's law introduces a sermon centered on the remarkable privilege of possessing and freely opening Scripture, reflected in the congregation's ordinary habit of turning to a Bible.

context: take your Bible and turn to Psalm 119
point_summary: Psalm 119's near-universal focus on God's word across its 176 verses grounds a call to recognize the extraordinary privilege of possessing Scripture in an age of abundant access.

context: The psalmist here in Psalm 119, he's speaking so much about the Bible, and he comes to verse 97, and he says, oh, how love I thy law, it, thy law, is my meditation all the day.
point_summary: The psalmist's declaration of all-day meditation on God's law is held up as a model for believers to carry Scripture on their hearts and minds throughout the entire day.

context: the words of the Lord are pure words. As silver tried in a furnace of earth purified seven times
point_summary: God's word is uniquely pure—like silver refined seven times—a purity only an eternal God could promise and preserve across every generation.

context: I hate and abhor lying, but thy law do I love.
point_summary: Hatred of lying and love of God's law are set in direct contrast, presenting devotion to Scripture as inseparable from a rejection of falsehood and false teaching.

context: great peace have they which love thy law, and nothing shall offend them.
point_summary: Great peace is the promised reward for those who love God's law, because love for the law drives one into the Word, into time with God, and into deeper knowledge of Him.

context: consider how I love thy precepts. Quicken me, O Lord, according to thy loving kindness.
point_summary: Loving God's word moves beyond personal declaration to a prayer inviting God himself to observe and consider the reality of that love.

context: the result in verse 165
point_summary: Those who love God's law are promised great peace precisely because that love draws them into the Word, into time with God, and into deeper knowledge of Him.

context: In verse 167, just a few verses down, he says, my soul hath kept thy testimonies
point_summary: Loving God's word is not merely declared to others but offered to God himself as evidence, as the psalmist's exceedingly love for the testimonies in verse 167 belongs to a pattern of affections God is invited to consider.

context: he makes the declaration that he loves God's word
point_summary: Loving God's law is not merely a declaration to others but a claim laid before God himself, as the psalmist moves from proclaiming his love in verse 97 to inviting divine scrutiny of it in verse 159.

context: in Psalm 119, verse 140, where he says that thy word is very pure
point_summary: The purity of God's Word — that it is perfect and without error — is given as a foundational reason to love Scripture, grounding the psalmist's affection in the trustworthy, inspired character of the Bible itself.

context: in Psalm 19, in verse number seven, the psalmist makes this declaration with regard to God's word. The law of the Lord is perfect
point_summary: The perfection of God's law (Ps. 19:7) grounds the sermon's case for loving Scripture, with the preacher noting he has never found an error in the Bible despite challenges from critics.

context: forever, O Lord, Thy word is settled in heaven
point_summary: God's eternal preservation of His Word is grounded in Psalm 119:89's declaration that His Word is forever settled in heaven, assuring believers that the Bible they hold is the very preserved Word of God.

context: Thy word is true from the beginning and every one of Thy righteous judgments endureth forever
point_summary: God's word is not partially but entirely true and enduring — "every one" of His righteous judgments endures forever — a totality only God could guarantee, underscoring the doctrine of preservation.

context: wherewithal shall a young man cleanse his way by taking heed thereto according to thy Word
point_summary: Memorizing Scripture is not merely a children's exercise but a discipline for all believers, enabling them to wield God's Word as a weapon against temptation as Christ himself demonstrated.

context: Thy Word have I hid in mine heart that I might not sin against thee
point_summary: Memorizing Scripture is not merely a childhood exercise but a lifelong discipline that equips believers to resist temptation, as Christ himself answered Satan with "it is written."

context: The Psalmist said in Psalm 119, it is my meditation. Oh, how love I thy law. It is my meditation all the day.
point_summary: Loving God's law as the psalmist did—meditating on it all the day—produces a mind so saturated with Scripture that it shapes both thought and behavior instinctively.

context: oh, how love I thy law
point_summary: Genuine love for God's Word naturally overflows in conversation, so that a believer who meditates on it daily need not fear being asked what Scripture is teaching them.

context: As the heart panteth after the water brooks, so panteth my soul after thee, O God.
point_summary: Earthly possessions and pleasures deteriorate, making the psalmist's image of a soul panting after God the model for the spiritual thirst that only God can satisfy.

context: My soul thirsteth for God, for the living God. When shall I come and appear before God?
point_summary: Earthly possessions and pleasures deteriorate, making the psalmist's soul-thirst for the living God the model for the spiritual pursuit that alone brings true satisfaction.

context: with the declaration of the psalmist in Psalm 19 in verse number one, where the Bible says, the heavens declare the glory of God and the firmament showeth his handiwork.
point_summary: The heavens' innumerable stars, cited from Psalm 19:1, illustrate that creation declares God's glory precisely through a magnitude no human mind or scientific estimate can fully comprehend.

context: The Bible begins in verse number one, really our text will be verses three through six, but let me read verse number one as we get into it. The Bible says, praise ye the Lord, for it is good to sing praises unto our God, for it is pleasant, and praise is comely.
point_summary: God created the stars not to be worshipped but to display His power and infinite wisdom, drawing mankind to worship the Creator instead.

context: The Lord doth build up Jerusalem, He gathereth together the outcast of Israel
point_summary: God created the stars not for human worship but to reveal His great power and infinite wisdom, directing mankind to worship the Creator rather than the creation.

context: He healeth the broken and hardened, bindeth up their wounds
point_summary: God created the stars not to be worshipped but to display His great power and infinite wisdom, drawing mankind to worship the Creator instead.

context: He telleth the number of the stars, He calleth them all by their names
point_summary: God's naming of every star — amid an estimated number too vast for human comprehension — is presented as a proclamation of His infinite power and wisdom, designed to draw worship to the Creator rather than the creation.

context: God knows exactly how many there are and He calls them all by their name. That's phenomenal to me. Now this great, powerful proclamation of Psalm 147 in verse number four is followed up in verse number five by revealing to us and helping us understand what God is seeking to communicate to us as His creation with this reference to the stars.
point_summary: God's personal knowledge of every star — numbering an estimated 200 billion trillion and naming each one — is presented as overwhelming evidence of His greatness.

context: He knows exactly the number of stars and He has given all of them a name
point_summary: God's naming of every star among the estimated 200 billion trillion serves as the basis for declaring His greatness, great power, and infinite understanding in Psalm 147:5.

context: great is our Lord, notice, and of great power. Yes, we mentioned last week the power of God. And the psalmist continues and says, His understanding is infinite.
point_summary: God's ability to name every one of the estimated 200 billion trillion stars demonstrates that He is great, of great power, and of infinite understanding, as Psalm 147:5 declares.

context: He healeth the broken in heart.
point_summary: The same God whose power spans the cosmos and who names every star personally promises not that His people will be spared a broken heart, but that He heals it.

context: He healeth the broken in heart
point_summary: The God whose power numbers and names every star is the same God who heals broken hearts, with the text stressing that healing—not immunity from heartbreak—is the promise given to His people.

context: He telleth the number of the stars; he calleth them all by their names
point_summary: God's intimate knowledge of every star by name and number is the very basis for trusting that He also knows and heals the broken heart of every individual.

context: Hear my cry, O God, attend unto my prayer.
point_summary: When the heart is overwhelmed, the psalmist models transparent, unashamed prayer—crying out to God to be led to the Rock that is higher than oneself rather than pretending to have everything under control.

context: From the end of the earth will I cry unto Thee. Notice what he says, when my heart is overwhelmed, lead me to the rock that is higher than I.
point_summary: When the heart is overwhelmed, Psalm 61:2 is cited to show that the believer's response is not self-sufficiency but crying out to God as the Rock higher than oneself.

context: that we find that the psalmist tells us here in Psalm 147 in verse number 3, he healeth the broken in heart.
point_summary: God's healing of the broken heart (Ps. 147:3) is presented as the comfort available to overwhelmed believers, contrasted with the hopelessness of facing life's difficulties without Christ.

reference_text: He healeth the broken in heart, and bindeth up their wounds.
context: He healeth the broken in heart. He bindeth up their wounds.
point_summary: God's power to name and number the stars is invoked as the measure of his ability to bind up wounds, arguing that no human hurt exceeds the healing reach of the Creator.

reference_text: The Lord lifteth up the meek: he casteth the wicked down to the ground.
context: Notice in verse number six, the Lord lifteth up the meek.
point_summary: God's power to name and number the stars is invoked as the measure of his ability to lift up and restore the meek who are cast down.

context: He calls them all by their names. He knows their number.
point_summary: God's intimate knowledge of every star by name and number, cited from Psalm 147:4, anchors the surrounding promises that this same God heals broken hearts and binds up wounds.

context: Understand with me that the Bible tells us in Psalm 90, take a moment and turn there if you will, Psalm 90, it's important for us to understand these
point_summary: God's perspective on time differs fundamentally from man's, as Psalm 90 establishes that a thousand years in His sight are as a single day — grounding the claim that "shortly" in Revelation describes swiftness of sequence, not nearness of date.

context: For a thousand years in thy sight are but as yesterday when it is passed, and as a watch in the night.
point_summary: God's perspective on time differs radically from man's, as a thousand years registers to Him only as yesterday — establishing that "shortly" in Revelation speaks to manner and swiftness, not human chronology.

context: The days of our years are threescore years and ten; and if by reason of strength they be fourscore years, yet is their strength labour and sorrow; for it is soon cut off, and we fly away.
point_summary: God's perception of time differs radically from man's, as a thousand years in His sight passes like yesterday, contextualizing why "shortly" in Revelation speaks to manner rather than human chronology.

context: for a thousand years in thy sight are but as yesterday when it is passed
point_summary: Psalm 90:4's declaration that a thousand years are as yesterday before God establishes that divine "shortly" operates on God's timescale, not man's, grounding the imminence language of Revelation 1:1.

context: Because the entrance of thy word giveth light.
point_summary: The entrance of God's Word giving light (Ps. 119:130) is cited as the reason believers are urged to read Scripture — because the Word benefits the hearer's own spiritual walk, not the preacher's.

context: as the psalmist said in Psalm 42 is the heart panteth after the water brooks so panteth my heart after thee Oh God
point_summary: Hungering and thirsting after God—as the psalmist's deer pants for water—is presented as the defining mark of Epaphras's walk and the standard for every believer's desire toward Christ.

context: blessed are they that keep his testimonies and that seek him, how? With the whole heart.
point_summary: Wholehearted seeking of God and keeping His testimonies are presented as the very posture that guards against the heart-deception Moses warns about in prosperity.

context: with my whole heart, have I sought they? Oh, let me not wander from thy commandments.
point_summary: Wholehearted seeking of God, paired with hiding His Word in the heart, is presented as the safeguard against the spiritual wandering and deception that prosperity can produce.

context: Thy word have I hid where? In mine heart. Why? For I might not sin against thee.
point_summary: Hiding God's word in the heart—cited alongside Psalm 119:2 and 10—is presented as the safeguard against the deception and spiritual wandering that prosperity and cultural pressure can produce.

context: revive us again that thy people may rejoice in thee
point_summary: Revival is urgently needed first within Bible-believing churches, where God's people must pray, confess sin, and repent before genuine rejoicing in Him can follow.

context: 'Examine me, O Lord, and prove me. Try my reins and my heart.'
point_summary: Self-examination before the Lord's Table is grounded in Scripture's repeated call to honest introspection, with Psalm 26:2 cited as one of several biblical examples of inviting God to search and prove the heart.

context: 'Stand in awe and sin not. Commune with your own heart upon your bed and be still.' Selah.
point_summary: Psalm 4:4's call to commune with one's heart and be still is cited as a biblical warrant for the self-examination required before approaching the Lord's Table worthily.

context: 'I call to remembrance my song in the night. I commune with my own heart, and my spirit may diligent search.'
point_summary: Communing with one's own heart and making a diligent search, as in Psalm 77:6, exemplifies the intense self-examination Scripture repeatedly calls believers to practice before approaching the Lord's Table.

context: 'I thought on my ways and turned my feet unto thy testimonies.'
point_summary: Self-examination that leads to repentance: the psalmist's deliberate reflection on his own ways resulted in a decisive turning of his feet back toward God's testimonies.

context: Turn to the book of Psalms, if you will, in Psalm chapter number 12.
point_summary: God's promise to preserve His pure Word (Ps. 12:6) is presented as the foundational promise undergirding all other divine promises, since the reliability of every subsequent promise depends on the integrity of the book that contains them.

context: 'The words of the Lord are pure words. As silver tried in a furnace of earth, purified seven times. Thou shalt keep them, O Lord. Thou shalt preserve them from this generation forever.'
point_summary: God's promise in Psalm 12:6–7 to preserve His pure, tried words forever grounds the believer's confidence in every other promise Scripture contains.

context: Notice, if you will, in Psalm 119 in verse number 89, the Bible tells us, of course, you're familiar with this chapter in Psalm 119 dealing with the Word of God and all the wonderful characteristics and the ways in which the psalmist is relating to the Word of God and its place in his life. And here in Psalm 119 in verse 89, the Bible says, forever, O Lord, thy Word is settled in heaven.
point_summary: God's Word is eternally settled in heaven, grounding the sermon's assurance that divine preservation of Scripture gives believers full confidence in its promises.

context: He owns the cattle on a thousand hills.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

reference_text: This is the day which the Lord hath made
context: This is the day which the Lord hath made. I will rejoice and be glad in
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: He restoreth my soul is what the psalmist says in Psalm 23.
point_summary: God's restorative purpose in pursuing a fallen Peter is grounded in the psalmist's declaration that "he restoreth my soul," identifying the Lord as a God of repeated restoration and second chances.

context: He speaks about the sins being as far removed as the east is from the west. That's in Psalm 103 and verse number 12. And it's a great truth, and you understand that if you travel east, you can just keep on, there's no point in which east and west are really divisible one from the other. We find that He did not say as far as the north is from the south, but as far as the east is from the west. In Psalm 103 and verse 12, as far as the east is from the west, so far hath He removed our transgressions from us.
point_summary: The infinite, unmeasurable removal of sin described in Psalm 103:12 — east perpetually separated from west with no convergence point — illustrates how completely God has removed the believer's transgressions through redemption.

context: I want to draw your attention to verse number 2 of Psalm 150. We can read the entirety of the psalm and see the call to praise and how important it is that we praise the Lord. But I want you to notice what the psalmist says in verse number 2, praise Him for His mighty acts.
point_summary: Psalm 150:2 distinguishes two grounds for praise — God's mighty acts (what He does) and His excellent greatness (who He is) — grounding the evening's study of God's moral attributes.

context: The psalmist says this, take your Bibles and turn to Psalm 145, if you will, and Psalm 145, and I want you to notice what the psalmist says with regard to the righteousness of God and making this declaration.
point_summary: Psalm 145:17 is cited to ground the claim that God's righteousness is the outward expression of His holiness—He is just and lawful, always doing what is right in every action.

context: The psalmist says, and you can jot it down in Psalm 119 in verse number 142, the psalmist declares, thy righteousness is an everlasting righteousness, and thy law is the truth.
point_summary: God's righteousness is everlasting—not temporary or conditional—and this eternal quality stands in contrast to humanity's total unrighteousness, making Christ's imputed righteousness the only basis for approaching God.

context: We find in Psalm 86 and verse number 15, the Bible says this, but thou, O Lord, art a God full of compassion and gracious, long-suffering, and plenteous in mercy and truth.
point_summary: God's compassion, as drawn from Psalm 86:15, is defined as a "mixed passion" — a compound of love and sorrow — seen most visibly in Christ being repeatedly "moved with compassion" throughout the Gospels.

context: In Psalm 111, we find that in verse number four, the Bible says, he hath made his wonderful works to be remembered. The Lord is gracious and full of compassion.
point_summary: Psalm 111:4's declaration that "the Lord is gracious" grounds the definition of grace as God giving sinners what they do not deserve, supremely expressed in salvation through Christ's atoning death.
Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

Psalm 116:5 is cited to show that grace, righteousness, and mercy are united in a single verse, illustrating God's mercy as withholding deserved judgment in contrast to grace as giving undeserved blessing.
Psalm 116:5 clusters grace, righteousness, and mercy together in a single verse, illustrating that God's mercy—not giving us what we deserve—is inseparable from His other attributes.

context: Gracious is the Lord and righteous, yea, our God is merciful.
point_summary: Psalm 116:5 clusters grace, righteousness, and mercy together in a single verse to establish that God's mercy—defined as withholding deserved judgment—is inseparable from His other perfections.

context: our God is plenteous in mercy.
point_summary: God's mercy — defined as withholding deserved judgment — is declared "plenteous" in Psalm 86:5, a quality the sermon connects to the 41 occurrences of "His mercy endureth forever" throughout Scripture.

context: Remember, we started this evening in Psalm 150 when the psalmist said, praise Him for His mighty acts, praise Him according to His excellent greatness.
point_summary: Psalm 150's call to praise God for His mighty acts and excellent greatness is cited as the fitting response to studying His seven attributes—His holiness, righteousness, faithfulness, love, compassion, grace, and mercy.

context: the Psalm ends in verse number 14 when it says, this God is our God forever and ever. He will be our guide even unto death.
point_summary: God's immutability and faithfulness ground the believer's confidence that He remains "our God forever and ever," guiding His people even through death, which fuels both rejoicing and daily perseverance through adversity.
Psalm 85:6 is presented as a model prayer for revival, with its petition—"Wilt thou not revive us again"—framing the entire sermon's call to preparation through prayer.

context: And here in the Psalms, in Psalm 85 and verse number 6, the psalmist says this, "'Wilt thou not revive us again, that thy people may rejoice in thee?' That thy people may rejoice in thee."
point_summary: Revival, as the psalmist's cry in Psalm 85:6 shows, is something only God can send, and its fruit is a restored rejoicing in Him—since rejoicing is the outworking of joy.

context: ...as the psalmist did in Psalm 139, search me, O God...
point_summary: Self-examination, modeled by the Laodicean church's failure to align self-perception with God's assessment, leads the believer to the psalmist's posture of inviting divine scrutiny: "Search me, O God."

context: ...you're in Psalm 85, so just turn several pages over to Psalm 139...
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: search me, O God, and know my heart. Try me and know my thoughts.
point_summary: Inviting God to search the heart, as the psalmist does, is presented as the necessary act of self-examination that precedes revival readiness, because human self-inspection is insufficient and self-deception is real.

context: and see if there be any wicked way in me, and lead me in the way everlasting.
point_summary: Inviting God to search for any wicked way, as the psalmist does, is presented as the posture of genuine self-examination that precedes revival-ready humility.

context: ...blessed is he whose transgression is forgiven, whose sin is covered.
point_summary: The blessedness declared in Psalm 32:1 is grounded not in concealment but in genuine forgiveness — sins covered by the blood of Christ through confession, as the psalmist models in verse five.

context: ...I acknowledged my sin unto thee, and mine iniquity have I not hid.
point_summary: Confession of sin — not hiding iniquity but openly acknowledging it before God — is presented as the pathway to the forgiveness God promises in response.

context: for I acknowledge my transgressions, my sin is ever before me.
point_summary: Genuine confession, modeled in David's acknowledgment that his sin is ever before him, means calling sin what God's Word calls it—neither covering nor excusing it—and directing that confession to God alone.

context: against thee, thee only have I sinned, and done this evil in thy sight, that thou mightest be justified when thou speakest, and be clear when thou judgest.
point_summary: David's confession in Psalm 51:4—"against thee, thee only have I sinned"—frames genuine repentance as directed solely toward God, not requiring human mediation, as the sinner acknowledges wrongdoing on God's own terms.

context: purge me with hyssop, and I shall be clean. Wash me, and I shall be whiter than snow.
point_summary: David's cry in Psalm 51:7 — "purge me with hyssop, and I shall be clean" — expresses the desire not merely for partial cleansing but for complete purity before God through honest confession.

context: create in me a clean heart, O God, and renew in me a right, excuse me, renew a right spirit within me.
point_summary: David's prayer in Psalm 51:10 is presented as the culmination of genuine repentance—a cry not merely for partial cleansing but for complete renewal of heart before God.

context: as the heart panteth after the water broke, so panteth my heart after thee, O God
point_summary: Genuine communion with God requires intentional pursuit, illustrated by the deer's desperate thirst as a picture of the soul that hungers to be in God's presence above competing distractions.

context: We read it in Psalm 32 about acknowledging my sin and not hiding it, confessing it, and having forgiveness.
point_summary: Selah in Psalm 32—marking the confession and forgiveness of sin—calls believers to stop, meditate, and let that truth fully sink in rather than rushing past it.

context: The psalmist said, will thou not revive us again that thy people may rejoice in thee?
point_summary: Revival-readiness is framed as an active, daily posture of seeking God's face, so that when He moves, His people are positioned to rejoice in Him.

context: David said restore unto me the joy of thy salvation. In that same Psalm 51 that we looked at a little bit this morning.
point_summary: Restoration of joy in salvation, drawn from Psalm 51:12, is identified as the fruit revival produces in the believer's heart.

context: ...not just here in Psalm 3, but many times as you read throughout the book of Psalms...

point_summary: Psalm 3:1 is read as a real, historical crisis David faced, illustrating that opposition and rising enemies are a genuine experience believers must learn to respond to rightly.

context: when they say of me that there is no help for him in God
point_summary: When enemies multiply and declare there is no help for him in God, the believer's response is not despair but confidence that God is greater than all opposition.

context: what a great proclamation in Psalm 121 in verses one and two when the psalmist says, I will lift up mine eyes unto the hills from whence cometh my help.
point_summary: The psalmist's declaration that his help comes from "the Lord, which made heaven and earth" establishes the foundational link between God's identity as Creator and his role as the believer's personal helper.

point_summary: The psalmist's declaration that help comes from "the Lord, which made heaven and earth" grounds personal reliance on God directly in his identity as Creator.

context: We see in Psalm 48, verse 1 and 2, the verses that are familiar to us, the verses that we sing for our song, our theme song for this year
point_summary: God's greatness declared in Psalm 48:1–2 is not merely a doxology but a personal promise, as the same psalm concludes that this great God is "our God forever and ever" and our guide unto death.

context: We see in Psalm 48, verse 1 and 2, the verses that are familiar to us, the verses that we sing for our song, our theme song for this year
point_summary: Psalm 48's declaration of God's greatness finds its completion in the closing affirmation that this great God is "our God forever and ever," a personal, enduring helper unto death.

context: lead me, O Lord, in thy righteousness because of mine enemies. Notice what he says, make thy way straight before my face.
point_summary: God's loving guidance of his children is the theological ground of Psalm 5:8, where David's prayer—"lead me, O Lord… make thy way straight before my face"—illustrates the believer's dependence on divine direction amid enemies.

context: I'm mindful of what the psalmist will say in Psalm 27 in verse number 11 where the Bible says,
point_summary: God's guidance is sought through prayer because he loves his people and knows what they do not—making the path plain and straight before them.

context: Notice what the psalmist says in Psalm 27.
point_summary: Psalm 27:11's petition — "teach me thy way, O Lord, and lead me in a plain path" — is cited as the believer's prayer for clear divine guidance and direction from God who loves his children.

context: But notice in verse number 23, the Bible says, nevertheless, I am continually with thee. Thou hast holden me by my right hand...thou shalt guide me with thy counsel, and afterward, receive me to glory.
point_summary: God's guiding hand—pictured as a parent holding a child's hand—leads believers through life by His counsel and ultimately receives them into glory.

context: thy word is a lamp unto my feet and a light unto my path
point_summary: God's word is identified as the source of divine counsel and guidance, with Psalm 119:105 confirming that Scripture illuminates the path God leads his people to walk.

context: giveth to the beast his food and to the young ravens which cry. It's of interest to me that in Psalm 147 and verse number 9, that God reminds us that he provides for the beast and for the young ravens.
point_summary: God's provision for ravens in Psalm 147:9 underscores the irony that the same God who feeds wild ravens commanded those very creatures to feed his servant Elijah.

context: And then the psalmist would say this in Psalm 42, in verses 1 and 2, the song that they just sang, As the heart panteth after the water brooks, so panteth my soul after thee, O God.
point_summary: Longing for God himself—not for what he gives—is the soul's truest thirst, as the psalmist's panting hart illustrates the singular desire for God alone.

context: My soul thirsteth for God, for the living God. When shall I come and appear before God?
point_summary: Longing for God himself—not for what he gives—is the soul's true thirst, as the psalmist's panting after the living God illustrates the singular desire for Christ alone.

context: The psalmist said in Psalm 78, excuse me, Psalm 73 in verse number 28, notice what the psalmist says here in this portion of scripture with regard to drawing near unto the Lord.
