point_summary: Drawing near to God is declared good by the psalmist, a truth the sermon connects directly to Abraham's act of approaching the Lord in intercession.

context: ...The Bible tells us in Psalm chapter number three, we're reminded that the Lord is not only my helper and my shield, but he is the lifter up of mine head...
point_summary: Even when no human encourager is present, God himself lifts the discouraged believer's head, as David's experience in Psalm 3 illustrates amid his own great adversity.

context: Notice if you will, Psalm 113, we'll look at two Psalms, verses in the book of Psalms. Look at first with me at Psalm 113. God is the giver of life.
point_summary: God is the giver, sustainer, and author of life—truths invoked from Psalm 113 to frame children as blessing rather than burden and to elevate eternal life above physical existence.

context: He maketh the barren woman to keep her house, and to be a joyful mother of children, praise ye the Lord.
point_summary: God's sovereign power to transform barrenness into joyful motherhood is cited as the scriptural backdrop for the Shunammite woman's blessing of receiving a child.

context: a verse of Scripture that's familiar to many of us
point_summary: Children are identified as a divine heritage and reward from God, grounding the Shunammite woman's blessing within the broader biblical truth that God alone grants the gift of children.

context: Lo, children are an heritage of the Lord, and the fruit of the womb is His reward.
point_summary: Children are described as a heritage and reward from God, lent to parents for a season to be invested in, influenced, instructed, and aimed—like arrows—toward the Lord.

context: As arrows are in the hand of a mighty man, so are children of the youth.
point_summary: Children, likened to arrows, fly in the direction they are aimed—underscoring parental responsibility to guide and direct them toward God.

context: Happy is the man that hath his quiver full of them. They shall not be ashamed, but they shall speak with the enemies in the gate.
point_summary: Children are described as arrows that fly in the direction they are aimed, illustrating the parent's responsibility to guide and direct them toward the Lord.

context: Psalm 46 and in fact, if you study the entirety of Psalm 46, it's amazing what the Psalmist says but he speaks about in verse number one how God is a very present help in the time of trouble
point_summary: God's presence as a very present help in trouble (Ps. 46:1) grounds the assurance that Paul's hardships, and ours, are met not with abandonment but with divine assistance.

context: as the verses continue in Psalm 46 in verse two and three, the catastrophic events that the Psalmist references in that Psalm
point_summary: Even amid catastrophic circumstances, God remains a very present help in trouble — the assurance grounding Paul's endurance through hardship.

context: as the verses continue in Psalm 46 in verse two and three, the catastrophic events that the Psalmist references in that Psalm
point_summary: Even amid catastrophic circumstances, God remains a very present help in trouble — the assurance Psalm 46 provides as backdrop to Paul's own hardships in 2 Corinthians 4.

context: He quotes back to the psalmist in Psalm 116.
point_summary: Paul's quotation of Psalm 116 ("I believed, therefore have I spoken") grounds the conviction that genuine faith in received truth compels its proclamation.

context: the psalmist says, it was good for me to be afflicted
point_summary: Affliction, though painful, is the very means by which God teaches his people his statutes—a truth the psalmist embraced and Paul's own suffering-filled ministry confirms.

context: Go back to Psalm 51 if you will. We visited Psalm 51 this morning, I told you we'd be back tonight. In Psalm 51, it's the psalm of repentance penned by David after the prophet Nathan.
point_summary: Repentance toward God must be grounded not in personal merit but in His mercy and lovingkindness, the only basis on which transgressions can be blotted out.

context: God, according to Thy loving kindness, according unto the multitude of Thy tender mercies, blot out my transgressions.
point_summary: Psalm 51:1 opens David's psalm of repentance—written after Nathan's confrontation—as the model for approaching God on the basis of His lovingkindness and tender mercies when sin has stripped away joy.

context: Wash me throughly from mine iniquity, cleanse me from my sin,
point_summary: Psalm 51:2's plea to be washed from iniquity frames personal repentance as the necessary path to restored joy, connecting David's cry directly to Joel's portrait of joy withhered away.

context: for I acknowledge my transgressions. My sin is ever before me
point_summary: Psalm 51:3's confession — "my sin is ever before me" — grounds the sermon's move from national calamity to personal repentance, showing that restored joy requires first acknowledging one's own transgressions before God.

context: against Thee. Thee only have I sinned and done this evil in Thy sight, that Thou mightest be justified when Thou speakest and be clear when Thou judgest.
point_summary: Sin's theft of joy is confirmed by David's cry in Psalm 51:12 to have it *restored*—implying that unconfessed sin had already stripped him of the joy of salvation.

context: Behold, I was shaped in an iniquity, and in sin did my mother conceive me.
point_summary: Psalm 51:5, read as part of David's full confession, grounds the sermon's call to personal repentance by showing that sin acknowledged before God alone is the path back to restored joy.

context: Behold, Thou desirest truth in the inward parts, and in the hidden part Thou shalt make me to know wisdom.
point_summary: Psalm 51:6's placement within David's psalm of repentance frames the loss of joy described in Joel 1 as rooted in unconfessed sin, with restoration requiring honest acknowledgment before God.

context: Purge me with hyssop, and I shall be clean. Wash me now, excuse me, wash me, and I shall be whiter than snow.
point_summary: Psalm 51:7's cry to be purged and washed connects the restoration of joy (v. 12) to genuine repentance, mirroring Joel's call for Israel to turn from sin back to God.

context: It says this, as we continue in verse number 8, that the bones which Thou hast broken may rejoice.
point_summary: Psalm 51:8's plea that "the bones which Thou hast broken may rejoice" connects David's broken condition to the missing joy of Joel 1:16, framing restored joy as the goal of genuine repentance.

context: Hide Thy face from my sins and blot out all mine iniquities.
point_summary: Psalm 51:9's placement within David's penitential prayer frames the sermon's point that sin's consequence is lost joy, not lost salvation — David pleads for joy's restoration, not salvation's restoration.

context: Make in me a clean heart, O God, and renew a right spirit within me.
point_summary: Psalm 51:12 is read alongside Joel 1 to show that lost joy is a spiritual condition requiring restoration, not that salvation itself is forfeited.

context: Cast me not away from Thy presence, and take not Thy Holy Spirit from me.
point_summary: Psalm 51:12 is cited to show that a believer can lose not salvation itself but the joy of it, requiring restoration through repentance rather than regeneration.

context: Notice verse number 12, restore unto me the joy of Thy salvation, and uphold me with Thy free spirit.
point_summary: Psalm 51:12 is cited to show that salvation itself is not lost in sin's aftermath, but its joy can be—and must be restored through repentance and God's renewing work.

context: In his presence, Psalm 16, is fullness of joy, and we understand that he gives us joy.
point_summary: Psalm 16:11's declaration that fullness of joy is found in God's presence grounds the sermon's point that salvation itself guarantees joy, even when that joy must be restored through repentance.

context: David doesn't pray in Psalm 51, 12, restore me my salvation. No. You're saved, you're saved. You're kept by the power of God. But he says, restore unto me the joy of Thy salvation.
point_summary: Psalm 51:12 is cited to show that sin—not loss of salvation—strips away joy, and that God's restorative grace can return what unconfessed sin has taken.

context: It says, down my lips and my mouth shall show forth thy praise.
point_summary: God's desire for a broken, contrite heart over outward sacrifice is the prerequisite that makes any offering acceptable, as David's prayer in Psalm 51 makes plain.

context: for thou desirest not sacrifice, else would I give it. Thou delightest not in burnt offering.
point_summary: God desires the heart behind the offering more than the offering itself, so that sacrifices without a right heart carry no weight before Him.

context: He says the sacrifices of God in verse 17 are a broken spirit, a broken and a contrite heart. Oh God, thou wilt not despise.
point_summary: God values a broken and contrite heart above outward religious offerings, and without that inward sacrifice, even God-ordained rituals fail to please Him.

reference_text: restore unto me the joy of thy salvation
context: And David says, restore unto me the joy of thy salvation.
point_summary: David's plea in Psalm 51:12 is used to establish that God is a restoring God, and that lost joy is traced specifically to sin as its cause.

context: He says, he, speaking of the Lord being our shepherd, he restoreth my soul
point_summary: Restoration as the shepherd's work toward wandering souls grounds the sermon's insistence that church discipline's true aim is reconciliation, not excommunication.

context: The righteous showeth mercy and giveth.
point_summary: Righteous generosity flows from received grace—the righteous show mercy and give because God has first been merciful and given to them.

context: Notice in Psalm 68, specifically look at verse number 19. The Bible says, blessed be the Lord who daily loadeth us with benefits, even the God of our salvation, Selah.
point_summary: Daily, God does not merely sprinkle blessings but "loadeth us with benefits," a picture of overflowing, undeserved abundance that calls for pausing in grateful meditation.

context: The Bible says in Psalm 100, in verse number one, 'Make a joyful noise unto the Lord, all ye lands.'
point_summary: Serving God is framed as an extraordinary privilege, with Psalm 100's call to "serve the Lord with gladness" underscoring that joyful service flows from recognizing He made us and we belong to Him.

context: 'Serve the Lord with gladness. 'Come before His presence with singing.'
point_summary: Serving God is a gladness-filled privilege, not a drudgery, because understanding who He is and what He has done transforms service into joy.

context: Notice verse three, 'Know ye that the Lord, He is God. 'It is He that hath made us, and not we ourselves. 'We are His people, and the sheep of His pasture.
point_summary: Belonging wholly to God as His people and His making grounds the call to serve Him with gladness rather than drudgery.

context: 'Enter into His gates with thanksgiving, 'and into His courts with praise. 'Be thankful unto Him, and bless His name.
point_summary: Entering God's gates with thanksgiving (v. 4) frames the privilege of serving Him—because knowing who He is and what He has done makes service a joy, never a drudgery.

context: For the Lord is good. His mercy is everlasting. His truth endureth to all generations.
point_summary: God's goodness, everlasting mercy, and enduring truth (v. 5) ground the call to serve Him with gladness rather than drudgery, since knowing who He is transforms service into joy.

context: I will lift up mine eyes into the hills from whence cometh my help.
point_summary: Because God is the ultimate source of help (Ps. 121:1–2), believers are privileged to reflect that same helping character toward others, as Phoebe exemplified.

context: My help cometh from the Lord which made heaven and earth.
point_summary: Because God himself is the ultimate helper whose aid comes from the Lord who made heaven and earth, believers are privileged to reflect that same helping character toward others.

context: why did the heathen rage and the people imagine vain things?
point_summary: The early church's prayer quotes Psalm 2:1 to frame the opposition of Herod, Pilate, Gentiles, and Israel against Jesus as the fulfillment of God's sovereignly determined counsel.

context: The kings of the earth stood up and the rulers were gathered together against the Lord and against his Christ
point_summary: The early church cited Psalm 2:2 as prophetic fulfillment, identifying Herod, Pilate, the Gentiles, and Israel as the rulers gathered against the Lord and His Christ.

context: I will lift up mine eyes into the hills from whence cometh my help. My help cometh from the Lord which made heaven and earth.
point_summary: Lifting eyes to the hills in Psalm 121:1 anchors the disciples' prayer in the recognition that their help comes from the Lord who made heaven and earth — the same Creator God who spoke all things into existence.

context: With thee, the psalmist said, there is forgiveness.
point_summary: Forgiveness is available with God for those who know His will but fail to do it, and confession of that failure as sin is the prescribed response.

context: the Bible tells us in the book of Psalms in the 19th chapter, that by them speaking of God's word and how thankful I am for the word is thy servant warned
point_summary: Psalm 19 is cited to establish that God's Word serves as a warning to His servants, with the phrase "by them is thy servant warned" grounding the sermon's claim that Scripture provides necessary cautions for life.

context: The psalmist said in Psalm 48, Great is the Lord, and greatly to be praised.
point_summary: God's greatness, as declared in Psalm 48, grounds the call to trust Him fully—because this great God is also "our God," He is worthy of belief over human understanding.

context: the Prophecy of a greater priest and then as we went through chapter number 5 we find that the writer presents the Lord Jesus Christ as The and proves that he is the fulfillment of that prophecy in Psalm 110
point_summary: Psalm 110:4's declaration of a priest "after the order of Melchizedek" is treated as an accepted messianic prophecy, previewed in Hebrews 5 and now fully expounded in Hebrews 7 as fulfilled in Jesus Christ.

context: the Messianic Psalm of Psalm 110
point_summary: Psalm 110's declaration that Christ is "a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek" is identified as a Messianic prophecy whose meaning only becomes fully clear when Hebrews unpacks it alongside Genesis 14.

context: Psalm 110 in verse number 4, it's mentioned twice in chapter number 5, it's given at the end of chapter number 6, it's mentioned twice again here in chapter number 7. And what is that prophecy of Psalm 110 in verse number 4? We read it in verse 17 of chapter 7, and at the end of verse number 21, thou art a priest, what does it say, forever after the order of Melchizedek.
point_summary: Psalm 110:4's declaration that Christ is a priest "forever after the order of Melchizedek" grounds his superiority over the Levitical priesthood in the power of an endless, unchangeable life.

context: He is that Messiah that was spoken of in Psalm 110 that he would come and he would be a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek.
point_summary: Psalm 110:4's prophecy of a priest "forever after the order of Melchizedek" is shown to find its fulfillment in Jesus Christ, whose eternal priesthood surpasses the insufficient Aaronic and Levitical orders.

context: I will lift up mine eyes unto the hills from whence cometh my help
point_summary: Lifting one's eyes to the hills frames a prayer of confident dependence, trusting that the God who created heaven and earth can protect his people through any dangerous journey.

context: my help cometh from the Lord which made heaven and earth
point_summary: God's creative power over heaven and earth is the ground of confidence that no journey or danger exceeds his ability to protect his people.

context: the Bible says that of itself that it's a lamp unto our feet that it guides us and it leads us
point_summary: Scripture's profitability as a lamp guiding believers' feet (Ps. 119:105) is cited as one characteristic among several—precious, powerful, preserved, profitable, prophetical—that together establish the Word's authority before the sermon addresses Colossians 3:4.

context: Oh Lord thy word is settled in heaven
point_summary: God's word is eternally settled in heaven and therefore beyond the reach of any government or law that would silence or outlaw it, making it an unchanging reason for continual rejoicing.

context: the truth of Psalm 150 where we are to praise God
point_summary: Psalm 150's call to praise God not only for what He does but for who He is grounds thanksgiving in God's unchanging character rather than in shifting circumstances.

reference_text: if I regard iniquity in my heart, the Lord will not hear me
context: He says this, if I regard iniquity in my heart, the Lord will not hear me.
point_summary: Cherished iniquity in the heart hinders a believer's fellowship with God, though it does not forfeit salvation, as Psalm 66:18 is cited to establish.

reference_text: the Psalms said that no bones would be broken
point_summary: The Psalms' prophecy that no bones of Christ would be broken was fulfilled at the crucifixion, confirming the Bible's precise accuracy in predicting the details of his death.

context: Search me, O God, and know my heart. Try me and know my thoughts
point_summary: Psalm 139:23–24 is presented as an invitation for God to search and expose hidden sin in the heart, paired with a call to extended corporate prayer.

context: and see if there be any wicked way in me, and lead me in the way everlasting
point_summary: Psalm 139:24's petition — "lead me in the way everlasting" — is introduced alongside its companion verse as an invitation for God to search, try, and cleanse the heart, framed by the congregation's sung response in the hymn "Cleanse Me."

context: Search me, O God, and know my heart. Try me, and know my thoughts
point_summary: Psalm 139:23's prayer "Search me, O God" is presented as a deeply personal plea, with David's use of first-person pronouns underscoring that genuine self-examination before God must be individually owned.

context: see if there be any wicked way in me, and lead me in the way everlasting
point_summary: Psalm 139:24's petition — "search me, know my heart" — is presented as a deeply personal plea, with David's use of first-person pronouns underscoring that genuine self-examination before God is an individual, not corporate, act.

context: Search me, O God, and know my heart: try me, and know my thoughts:
point_summary: Because the human heart is deceitful and capable of self-deception, David's plea for God to search him acknowledges that only the Lord can truly know and examine one's heart and thoughts.

context: David is saying, God, I'm interested in you searching my heart, I'm interested in you knowing my heart and trying me and knowing my thoughts.
point_summary: David's plea in Psalm 139:23 — "search me, O God, know my heart" — is framed as a call to personal, self-directed examination rather than deflecting God's scrutiny onto others.

context: search me, know my heart, try me, know my thoughts
point_summary: The personal pronouns of Psalm 139:23—"search me," "know my heart," "try me"—underscore that spiritual self-examination before God is an individual, not comparative, responsibility.

context: search me, know my heart, try me, know my thoughts
point_summary: The personal pronouns of Psalm 139:23–24 ("search me," "know my heart," "try me") underscore that self-examination before God is an individual, not comparative, responsibility.

context: O Lord, thou hast searched me and known me.
point_summary: David's opening declaration that God has already searched and known him establishes divine omniscience as the theological foundation that makes his closing petition—"Search me, O God"—a passionate, personal desire rather than mere acknowledgment.

context: Search me, O God, and know my heart. Try me and know my thoughts.
point_summary: David's closing plea in Psalm 139:23 is marked by both personal specificity—"search me," "know my heart," "try me"—and passionate desire, not merely intellectual acknowledgment of God's omniscience.

context: Search me, O God, and know my heart. Try me and know my thoughts
point_summary: Praying "search me, O God" requires personal humility—turning attention away from others' sins and asking God to expose whatever hinders one's own walk with Him.

context: see if there be any wicked way in me and lead me in the way everlasting
point_summary: Praying "search me, O God" requires personal humility—asking God to expose hidden sin in one's own life rather than measuring oneself against others' worse failures.

context: will say, as the psalmist said here in Psalm 139, God, I'm the one that needs you to work in my life.
point_summary: Psalm 139's closing petition calls each believer to a personal, humble posture—asking God to search and work in one's own life rather than focusing on others—as the only true preparation for corporate revival.

context: In His presence, the psalmist said, is fullness of joy.
point_summary: Knowing that God's presence holds fullness of joy, believers who have been granted access through Christ have every reason to draw near rather than forfeit the privilege of fellowship and prayer.

context: Psalm 48 says, great is the Lord and greatly to be praised.
point_summary: God's greatness, affirmed in Psalm 48:1, is introduced as scriptural warrant for a structured study of the divine attributes, both natural and moral.

context: Notice in Psalm 135, in verse number 1, the Bible says, Praise ye the Lord, praise ye the name of the Lord.
point_summary: Psalm 135:1 is cited as a gateway into verses 3–5, where the psalmist's declaration "the Lord is great" anchors the evening's study of God's natural attributes as the first movement in a two-part series on divine greatness.

context: For the Lord hath chosen Jacob unto Himself in Israel for His peculiar treasure.
point_summary: God's greatness, being unsearchable, is meant to be declared from one generation to the next as an act of praise and worship.

context: For I know that the Lord is great, and that our Lord is above all gods.
point_summary: Against the backdrop of Old Testament idolatry, the psalmist's personal declaration — "I know that the Lord is great" — introduces a multi-week examination of God's attributes as incomparably superior to all false gods.

context: I will extol Thee, my God, O King, and I will bless Thy name forever and ever.
point_summary: God's unsearchable greatness, declared in Psalm 145, anchors a multi-week study of the divine attributes, with each generation called to pass that declaration on to the next.

context: Every day will I bless Thee, and I will praise Thy name forever and ever.
point_summary: Daily praise of God's name, as voiced in Psalm 145:2, frames the sermon's call to examine God's attributes so that each generation might declare His unsearchable greatness.

context: Great is the Lord, and greatly to be praised, and His greatness is unsearchable.
point_summary: God's greatness, declared unsearchable in Psalm 145:3, serves as the scriptural foundation for a multi-week examination of His natural and moral attributes.

context: one generation shall praise Thy works to another, and shall declare Thy mighty acts
point_summary: Each generation's responsibility to declare God's mighty works to the next reflects a divinely established principle of generational influence, the failure of which leaves succeeding generations ignorant of God's greatness.

context: I will speak of the glorious honor of Thy majesty and of Thy wondrous works
point_summary: Psalm 145:5's declaration that one generation shall praise God's works to another grounds the principle that each generation bears responsibility to pass on the knowledge of God's greatness to the next.

context: and men shall speak of the might of Thy terrible acts. Notice the last phrase of verse number 6, and I will declare Thy greatness
point_summary: The closing phrase of Psalm 145:6, "I will declare Thy greatness," anchors the sermon's call for each generation to faithfully transmit knowledge of God's greatness to the next.

context: Oh Lord thou hast searched me and known me.
point_summary: God's exhaustive knowledge of every person is affirmed in Psalm 139:1, where the psalmist declares that the Lord has searched and known him, illustrating the omniscience Job confessed in Job 42.

context: Thou knowest my down sitting and mine uprising. Thou understandest my thought afar off.
point_summary: God's omniscience extends even to human thought, as Psalm 139:2 is cited alongside Job 42:2 to show that no thought can be withheld from Him.

context: Look at verse chapter 147 here in the book of Psalms and look at verses four and five.
point_summary: God's omniscience extends even to the vast, uncountable stars, each of which He has numbered and named, illustrating that nothing in creation lies beyond His knowledge.

context: wither shall I go from thy spirit or wither shall I flee from thy presence
point_summary: God's omnipresence, unlike human limitation, means there is no place—heaven, hell, or the uttermost sea—where one can flee from His presence or escape His guiding hand.

context: if I ascend up into heaven thou art there if I make my bed in hell behold thou art there
point_summary: God's omnipresence means there is no location—heaven, hell, or the uttermost sea—where one can flee from His presence or escape His guiding hand.

context: if I take the wings of the morning and dwell in the uttermost parts of the sea
point_summary: Psalm 139:9's image of taking "the wings of the morning" to the uttermost sea illustrates God's omnipresence—that no distance or location lies beyond His reach.

context: even there shall thy hand lead me and the right hand shall hold me
point_summary: God's omnipresence, illustrated in Psalm 139:7–12, means His hand leads and holds His people even in the uttermost parts of the sea, with no place beyond His presence.

context: if I say surely the darkness shall cover me even the night shall be light about me
point_summary: Darkness offers no concealment from God, for even the night is as light to him — illustrating that his omnipresence admits no hiding place from his presence.

context: yea the darkness hideth not from thee but the night shineth as the day the darkness and the light are both alike to thee
point_summary: Psalm 139:12 is read as part of a continuous passage (vv. 7–12) establishing that God's omnipresence is absolute — darkness and light are alike to Him, so no place or condition lies beyond His presence.

context: yea though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death I will fear no evil why for thou art with me
point_summary: God's omnipresence, illustrated by Psalm 23:4's "thou art with me," is the theological ground for the believer's fearlessness in life's darkest valleys, since God is equally present with every one of his children at all times.

context: Before the mountains were brought forth, or ever thou hadst formed the earth and the world, Then from everlasting to everlasting thou art God.
point_summary: Psalm 90:2's declaration that God exists "from everlasting to everlasting" is cited as scriptural proof that God is self-existent and eternal, having no creator and no beginning.

context: Psalm 48 starts out with a song that we sing, Psalm 48, 1 and 2, great is the Lord, right?
point_summary: Psalm 48:1's declaration that God is great and greatly to be praised grounds the call for every generation to personally affirm and declare his greatness to the next.

context: Psalm 48 starts out with a song that we sing, Psalm 48, 1 and 2, great is the Lord, right?
point_summary: Psalm 48's opening declaration — "Great is the LORD, and greatly to be praised" — grounds the sermon's call to stand in awe of God's revealed attributes and declare His greatness to the next generation.

context: But just as the psalmist said in Psalm 145, one generation has to declare his greatness to another generation
point_summary: Psalm 145 grounds the call for each generation to personally declare God's greatness, not merely acknowledge it, committing with the psalmist: "I will speak of thy greatness."

context: I've often said as I read Psalm 119, what a wonderful psalm it is, and you think about all that the psalmist says about the Word of God and how wonderful it is and how precious it is and so on and so forth
point_summary: Psalm 119's celebration of God's Word is invoked as the scriptural basis for the psalmist's goal—and the believer's calling—to think and act biblically through spiritual growth.

context: the Lord is in His holy temple. The Lord's throne is in heaven. His eyes behold, His eyelids try, the children of men
point_summary: God's throne being in heaven establishes His sovereign authority over all humanity, a truth the Old Testament saints understood and invoked as the foundation of their prayers.

context: not unto us, O Lord, not unto
point_summary: Glory belongs to God's name alone—not to humanity—a truth the sermon draws from Psalm 115:1 to contrast the sovereign Lord in heaven with the powerless idols addressed in the surrounding passage.

context: us, but unto thy name give glory for thy mercy and for thy truth's sake, wherefore should the heathen say, where is now their God?
point_summary: Psalm 115:1 anchors a contrast between the living God enthroned in heaven who alone deserves glory and the lifeless, man-made idols who can neither see, speak, nor act.

context: wherefore should the heathen say, where is now their God? But our God is in the heavens. He hath done whatsoever he hath pleased.
point_summary: God's heavenly dwelling is contrasted with the powerless, man-made idols of the heathen, establishing that Jehovah alone, enthroned in heaven, is worthy of worship and trust.

context: But our God is in the heavens. He hath done whatsoever he hath pleased.
point_summary: God's heavenly dwelling is contrasted with powerless man-made idols to establish that Jehovah alone, as Creator enthroned in heaven, is worthy of worship and trust.

context: Their idols are silver and gold, the work of men's hands.
point_summary: Psalm 115:4's description of idols as silver and gold, the mere work of men's hands, contrasts sharply with the living God who dwells in heaven and has done whatever He pleased.

context: They have mouths, but they speak not, eyes have they, but they see not.
point_summary: In contrast to mute, sightless idols fashioned by human hands, the living God is enthroned in heaven as Creator, worthy of worship precisely because He made us rather than we Him.

context: They have ears, but they hear not, noses have they, but they smell not.
point_summary: Psalm 115:6's declaration that idols "have ears, but they hear not" anchors a contrast between lifeless man-made gods and the living Jehovah who reigns in heaven and does whatsoever He pleases.

context: They have hands, but they handle not, feet have they, but they walk not, neither speak they through their throat.
point_summary: Psalm 115:7's reference to idol hands that "handle not" anchors a contrast between lifeless, man-made idols and the living Creator God enthroned in heaven, whom alone his creatures are to worship.

context: They that make them are like unto them, so is everyone that trusteth in them.
point_summary: Psalm 115:8 is cited within a contrast between the living God enthroned in heaven and lifeless idols, underscoring the absurdity of worshiping what human hands have made.

context: O Israel, trust thou in the Lord, he is their help and their shield.
point_summary: Psalm 115:9's call to trust in the Lord as Israel's help and shield grounds the sermon's claim that prayer is a privilege rooted in relationship with the omnipotent God who reigns in heaven.

context: O house of Aaron, trust in the Lord, he is their help and their shield.
point_summary: Those who trust in the Lord find in Him their help and shield—a contrast to the lifeless idols fashioned by human hands that leave their makers equally powerless.

context: Ye that fear the Lord, trust in the Lord, he is their help and their shield.
point_summary: Those who fear the Lord are called to trust him as their help and shield — a summons to confidence in the living God that stands in sharp contrast to the powerless idols described in the surrounding verses.

context: thou art my son, today have I begotten thee
point_summary: Psalm 2:7's declaration "Thou art my Son, today have I begotten thee" refers not to the incarnation but to the resurrection of Christ, as confirmed by Paul's preaching in Acts 13.

context: we come to Psalm 2 in verse number 7, that's referenced here in Hebrews 5 in verse number 5
point_summary: Acts 13:33's citation of Psalm 2:7 demonstrates that "this day have I begotten thee" refers to the resurrection, not the birth, and that Hebrews 5:5 applies this same prophecy messianically to Christ.

context: that other place is Psalm 110 in verse number 4
point_summary: Psalm 110:4 is identified as a messianic psalm—recognized as such even by Jewish religious leaders—and its application in Hebrews 5:6 to Christ confirms Jesus as the promised Messiah.

context: thou art a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek
point_summary: Psalm 110:4, recognized by Jewish leaders as a messianic psalm, is applied in Hebrews 5:6 to establish that Christ holds a priesthood after Melchizedek's order—distinct from the Levitical line he could not enter by tribe.

context: he gives them the prophecy from Psalm 2
point_summary: Psalm 2 is cited as messianic prophecy establishing that Jesus fulfills the role of a greater priest, distinct from the Levitical order, under the order of Melchizedek.

context: then from Psalm 110, referencing that Jesus is the fulfillment
point_summary: Psalm 110 is cited as the messianic prophecy establishing that Jesus fulfills a priesthood greater than the Levitical order—one after the pattern of Melchizedek, who uniquely held both priest and king offices.

context: the greatest one is Psalm 22 with regard to the crucifixion.
point_summary: Psalm 22, identified here as the greatest Old Testament prophecy of the crucifixion, undergirds the claim that Christ's Gethsemane prayer was not to escape the cross but to reach it, fulfilling that prophetic script.

context: Isn't that what Psalm 23 tells us?
point_summary: God's provision for his people through the shepherd—who leads, protects, and supplies every need—is illustrated in Jacob's family receiving both the best land and sustained nourishment in Egypt.

context: The Bible says that the entrance of thy words, it giveth light
point_summary: Scripture's own testimony that God's Word gives light undergirds the third element of Paul's ministry philosophy—teaching every man—as the antidote to the spiritual blindness the enemy promotes.

context: 'Lord, how are they increased that trouble me? 'Many are they that rise up against me,
point_summary: Psalm 3:1 establishes the historical reality of David's crisis — the rebellion of Absalom — as a genuine, unexaggerated threat of many rising against him.

context: 'many there be which say of my soul, 'there is no help for him in God, Selah.'
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: 'But thou, O Lord, art a shield for me.'
point_summary: Against the enemies' taunt that God would not help him, David's response in verse 3 was to affirm God as his shield, his glory, and the lifter of his head.

context: 'I understand, God, that you are a shield for me, 'my glory and the lifter up of mine head.'
point_summary: Against the enemies' taunt that God would not help him, David counters in verse 3 by confessing God as his shield, his glory, and the lifter of his head—modeling the response of faith in adversity.

context: 'I cried unto the Lord with my voice, 'and he heard me out of his holy hill, Selah.'
point_summary: David's cry to the Lord amid Absalom's revolt, and God's answer from His holy hill, is presented as the exemplary response to adversity — trusting God and casting burdens upon Him rather than despairing.

context: 'I laid me down and slept. 'I awaked, for the Lord sustained me.
point_summary: After pouring out his burden to God in prayer, David experienced a peace that enabled restful sleep amid mortal danger, illustrating that trust in God produces calm even when surrounded by enemies.

reference_text: I will not be afraid of 10,000s of people that have set themselves against me roundabout.
point_summary: Casting burdens on God and trusting his care produces the deep peace and rest David models in verse 6, where he lies down unafraid despite ten thousands arrayed against him.

reference_text: Cast thy burden upon the Lord, and he shall sustain thee.
point_summary: Casting burdens on the Lord—drawn from Psalm 55:22—is presented as the basis for the peace and rest David experiences amid trial.
Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: 'Arise, O Lord, save me, O my God. 'For thou hast smitten all mine enemies upon the cheekbone. 'Thou hast broken the teeth of the ungodly.
point_summary: Psalm 3:7 introduces the fourth and final division of the psalm—David's rejoicing—framing his cry "Arise, O Lord, save me" as an expression of praise rooted in confidence that God is in control.

context: 'Salvation belongeth unto the Lord. 'Thy blessing is upon thy people, Selah.
point_summary: Salvation belonging to the Lord is the theological ground for David's rejoicing, framing the entire concluding section of Psalm 3 as a response of praise rooted in God's sovereign ownership of deliverance.

context: this is the day the Lord hath made. We will what? Rejoice in it, right? And be glad.
point_summary: Believers have many reasons to rejoice because God has planned, purposed, and is in control of every day He has made.

context: Arise, O Lord, save me, O my God.
point_summary: David's cry "Arise, O Lord, save me" is read as an expression of full confidence in God's ability and power to deliver, confirming a trust that runs throughout the entire psalm.

context: for thou hast smitten all mine enemies upon the cheekbone. Thou hast broken the teeth of the ungodly.
point_summary: David's cry "arise, O Lord, save me, O my God" reveals both confidence in God's unlimited ability and complete dependence upon Him to act on the believer's behalf.

context: and I said, this is my infirmity, but I will remember the years of the right hand of the Most High.
point_summary: Remembering God's past works — as Psalm 77:10 models — is presented as the proper response to present trial, fueling rejoicing rather than despair.

reference_text: Thou hast smitten all mine enemies upon the cheekbone. Thou hast broken the teeth of the ungodly.
context: Thou hast smitten all mine enemies upon the cheekbone. Thou hast broken the teeth of the ungodly.
point_summary: Recalling God's past victories — as David does in crying "thou hast smitten mine enemies" — should produce both rejoicing and renewed trust that the same God can act in present circumstances.

reference_text: This is the day the Lord hath made. We will rejoice and be glad in it.
context: This is the day the Lord hath made. We will rejoice and be glad in it.
point_summary: Purposing to rejoice—as in "This is the day the LORD hath made"—redirects focus from the problem to God, who is greater than any situation faced.

context: David understands that, listen, salvation belongeth unto the Lord
point_summary: Salvation belonging to the Lord, illustrated through David's dependence on God from Goliath to Absalom's revolt, is meant to catalyze the believer's rejoicing rather than self-reliant strategizing.

reference_text: this is the day the Lord hath made
context: Because He says, this is the day the Lord hath made. I will rejoice and be glad in it.
point_summary: Rejoicing in the Lord even amid difficulty is commanded without exception, grounded in the declaration that this day is the Lord's making.

context: The Bible tells us that God was working on their behalf, that they called and troubled and I delivered thee in verse number seven. I answered thee in the secret place of thunder.
point_summary: Psalm 81:7 is cited to illustrate that God was actively working and answering on Israel's behalf, yet blessings were forfeited because the people refused to hearken to His voice.

context: I proved thee at the waters of Meribah, Sila.
point_summary: God's deliverance at Meribah illustrates His faithfulness to His people, set against the tragic forfeiture of promised blessings that followed Israel's persistent disobedience.

context: I am the Lord thy God, which brought thee out of the land of Egypt. Open thy mouth wide and I will fill it,
point_summary: God's invitation to open wide one's mouth and receive His filling stands as the unfulfilled promise forfeited by Israel's disobedience, illustrating how God's desired blessings go unreceived when His people refuse to hearken.

context: but my people would not hearken to my voice. And Israel would none of me.
point_summary: Israel's refusal to hearken to God's voice, cited from Psalm 81:11, illustrates how covenant blessings are forfeited through disobedience rather than divine unwillingness to give them.

context: so I gave them up unto their own hearts lust and they walked in their own councils.
point_summary: When Israel refused to hearken to God's voice, He gave them over to their own hearts' lusts — illustrating that disobedience forfeits the blessings God desired to pour out upon His people.

context: Oh, that my people had hearkened unto me and Israel had walked in my ways.
point_summary: God's longing cry in Psalm 81:13 — "Oh, that my people had hearkened unto me" — reveals that forfeited blessings grieve the heart of God and trace directly to Israel's disobedience.

context: I should soon have subdued their enemies and turn my hand against their adversaries.
point_summary: Obedience unlocks divine intervention: had Israel hearkened, God would have swiftly subdued their enemies — blessings forfeited through disobedience, not divine unwillingness.

context: the people of God limited the Holy One of Israel.
point_summary: Israel's disobedience "limited the Holy One of Israel," forfeiting the blessings God desired to pour out — a pattern the sermon traces throughout the Old Testament.

context: if I regard iniquity in my heart, the Lord will not hear me
point_summary: Cherished sin in the heart—"regarding iniquity"—disqualifies prayer from being heard, establishing the negative condition that makes obedience the necessary ground for confident, answered prayer.

context: fret not thyself because of evildoers, neither be thou envious against the workers of iniquity
point_summary: Psalm 37:1's command to "fret not" over evildoers frames the early church's prayer in Acts 4 as a model of trusting God rather than retaliating against threatening adversaries.

context: for they shall soon be cut down like the grass and wither as the green herb
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Trust in the Lord and do good, so shalt thou dwell in the land and verily thou shalt be fed
point_summary: Trusting in the Lord and doing good—not fretting over evildoers—is the prerequisite context for the promises of Psalm 37, which are often cited selectively and therefore misapplied.

context: delight thyself also in the Lord, and He shall give thee the desires of thine heart
point_summary: When a believer genuinely delights in the Lord, God's desires become that believer's desires, so that answered prayer flows from alignment with His will rather than personal want.

context: commit thy way unto the Lord, trust also in Him, and He shall bring it to pass
point_summary: Committing one's way to the Lord and trusting Him is inseparable from first delighting in Him, so that His desires become the believer's desires and He brings them to pass.

context: delight thyself also in the Lord, and He shall give thee the desires of thine heart
point_summary: Delighting in the Lord transforms the believer's desires into God's own desires, so that the promise of receiving heart's desires is fulfilled through alignment with His will, not apart from it.

context: Commit thy way unto the Lord, trust also in Him, and He shall bring it to pass
point_summary: Committing one's way to the Lord (v. 5) is inseparable from delighting in Him (v. 4), so that the desires God brings to pass are His own desires now shared by the surrendered believer.

reference_text: search me, O God, know me
context: as the psalmist said, search me, O God, know me.
point_summary: Honest self-examination before God—echoing the psalmist's "search me, O God"—is presented as the posture believers must take to identify and correct any area of disobedience hindering fellowship with Him.

context: help me, oh, Lord, my God. Oh, save me according to thy mercy.
point_summary: Praying for God's mercy so that onlookers will recognize "this is thy hand" (v. 27) frames Eleazar's rehearsal of answered prayer as the pattern for making God's work unmistakably visible.

context: that they may know that this is thy hand that thou, Lord, hast done it.
point_summary: Specific, God-glorifying answers to prayer are designed so that onlookers can recognize unmistakably that the outcome is divine, not human, in origin.

context: Be still and know that I am God
point_summary: Quieting oneself before God—"be still and know that I am God"—is presented as the posture of attentive listening through which a believer discerns God's will.

context: turn to the book of Psalms, if you will, in Psalm 86, I want you to see what is said here.
point_summary: Psalm 86:1's plea — "Bow down thine ear, O Lord" — establishes a reciprocal principle: we must first bow our ear to God's Word to know His will before we can rightly ask Him to bow His ear to our prayers.

context: 'Bow down thine ear, O Lord. 'Hear me, for I am poor and needy.'
point_summary: Psalm 86:1's plea — "Bow down thine ear, O Lord" — is set in reciprocal parallel with Proverbs' call to bow down our ear to God's word: only as we submit to His will can we pray with confidence that He hears.

context: The psalmist said in Psalm 121, I will lift up mine eyes unto the hills. From whence cometh my help?
point_summary: Lifting one's eyes to the hills in Psalm 121:1 grounds the assurance that God is our Helper—the reason fear of any earthly power is displaced by confidence in His presence.

context: My help cometh from the Lord which made heaven and earth.
point_summary: God's presence as Helper — drawn from Psalm 121:2 alongside Hebrews 13:5-6 — grounds the assurance that those who stand faithfully will not face consequences alone.

context: look, if you will, very quickly at Psalm 32 and notice what is said here, excuse me, Psalm 33.
point_summary: Psalm 33 is cited alongside Genesis 1 to confirm that God spoke creation into existence, with the repeated pattern of divine speech and fulfillment establishing the meaning of "worlds framed by the Word of God" in Hebrews 11:3.

context: rejoice in the Lord, O you righteous, for praise is comely for the upright
point_summary:
Psalm 33:1 is cited as an entry point into the passage, but the sermon's actual development focuses on verses 6–9 and their affirmation that God spoke creation into existence, directly paralleling Genesis 1.

Wait, I need to follow the rules strictly and output only one sentence.

Praise befitting the upright, introduced in verse 1, serves as the sermon's doorway into Psalm 33's broader testimony that God spoke creation into existence, echoing Genesis 1's repeated "God said."

context: He loveth righteousness and judgment. The earth is full of the goodness of the Lord
point_summary: Psalm 33:5–9 is read as a doxological confirmation of Genesis 1's repeated pattern—God spoke, and creation stood fast—establishing divine speech as the sole sufficient cause of all that exists.

context: By the word of the Lord were the heavens made. And all the host of them, by the breath of his mouth
point_summary: Creation's divine origin is confirmed by Psalm 33:6's declaration that the heavens were made by God's spoken word, echoing Genesis 1's repeated pattern of "God said" and "it was so."

context: he gathereth the waters of the sea together as in heap. He layeth up the depth in storehouses
point_summary: God's gathering of the waters into heaps (Ps. 33:7) is cited as part of Psalm 33's broader testimony that creation came into being solely by divine speech—"he spake and it was done."

context: Let all the earth fear the Lord. Let all the inhabitants of the world stand in awe of him
point_summary: Universal reverence is the fitting response to a God whose spoken word alone brought all creation into existence, as Psalm 33:8–9 confirms the Genesis pattern of "God said, and it was done."

context: for he spake and it was done. He commanded and it stood fast
point_summary: God's creative act required no process or effort — he spoke, and it was done; he commanded, and it stood fast.

context: He spake and it was done
point_summary: Psalm 33's declaration that God spoke and creation came into being is cited alongside Genesis 1 to affirm that the worlds were framed by the Word of God, evidencing divine design.

context: The heavens declare the glory of God. The firmament showeth forth his handiwork.
point_summary: Creation's glory declared in the heavens was David's testimony, cited here as one biblical witness among many that Scripture's authors consistently understood God as Creator.

context: I will lift up mine eyes into the hills from whence cometh my help.
point_summary: Psalm 121:1–2 is cited as evidence that the biblical authors consistently grounded their confidence in God's help in His identity as Creator of heaven and earth.

context: I will lift up mine eyes into the hills from whence cometh my help.
point_summary: The confession that help comes from the Lord who made heaven and earth grounds the psalmist's trust in God's power to help in the Creator's sovereign authority over all things.

context: that everything that hath breath, that we're to praise the Lord, praise ye the Lord.
point_summary: Psalm 150's call for all who have breath to praise God grounds the truth that believers always have reason to rejoice, because God's unchanging character never gives out.

context: great is the Lord. I mean, what a proclamation. What a way to just start the Psalm. Great is the Lord. And it says this, and greatly to be praised.
point_summary: Psalm 48:1's opening declaration, "Great is the Lord and greatly to be praised," is presented as a foundational proclamation calling believers to recognize God's greatness and respond with proportionate praise.

context: Notice in verse number one, the Bible says, praise ye the Lord. Praise ye the name of the Lord. Praise Him, O ye servants of the Lord.
point_summary: Psalm 135:1–5 is cited to reinforce that Scripture repeatedly and confidently declares God's greatness, calling His servants to praise Him precisely because He is great and above all gods.

context: Isn't that a wonderful statement of the psalmist in Psalm 135 in verse number five?
point_summary: The psalmist's confident declaration "I know that the Lord is great" — not merely a belief but a settled knowledge — is presented as a conviction that should practically shape prayer, witness, praise, and daily living.

context: And the psalmist in Psalm 135 said, I know that the Lord is great.
point_summary: Psalm 135's declaration "I know that the Lord is great" is cited as a confident, personal affirmation of divine greatness, paired with Moses's testimony that God had only begun to display it.

context: Great is the Lord, and greatly to be praised. And then it says this, His greatness is unsearchable
point_summary: God's greatness is declared unsearchable in Psalm 145:3, meaning no human articulation — however eloquent — can fully comprehend or exhaust how great He is.

context: This great God, as we look in Psalm 48, the end of the chapter in v. 14, after it goes through all of these statements with regard to our God being a refuge and so on and so forth, He tells us in v. 14, for this God is our God, forever and ever. He will be our guide even unto death.
point_summary: God's eternal covenant with His people — "this God is our God, forever and ever" — means He guides believers through every moment of life, even unto death.

context: Look at Psalm 145. I told you we'd be there in just a moment. Look at what the Bible says here in the first three verses. In Psalm 145, beginning in verse number one, notice what the psalmist says. I will extol thee.
point_summary: Psalm 145:1's "I will extol thee" is read as a determined, personal commitment to praise—grounded in the psalmist's understanding of God's greatness and extending into eternity.

context: He's going to speak about the greatness of God in verse number three, but you understand that he's saying
point_summary: God's greatness, declared in Psalm 145:3, grounds the psalmist's determined, forward-looking commitment to extol and bless his name—a praise that extends into eternity.

context: I will extol thee, my God, O King, and I will bless thy name forever and ever.
point_summary: The psalmist's "I will extol thee" in Psalm 145:1 reflects a determined, volitional commitment to praise God based on an understanding of His greatness, not on favorable circumstances.

context: For this God is our God for ever and ever: he will be our guide even unto death.
point_summary: God's identity as "my God" in Psalm 48:14 extends into eternity, making present praise a preparation for the unending worship believers will offer forever.

context: Every day will I bless thee; and I will praise thy name for ever and ever.
point_summary: Daily praise is framed as a determined, unconditional commitment rooted not in favorable circumstances but in the psalmist's settled understanding of God's greatness.

context: great is the Lord and greatly to be praised
point_summary: God's greatness, declared unsearchable in Psalm 145:3, grounds the call to proclaim who He is across generations rather than constructing competing human ideas about Him.

context: one generation shall praise thy works to another and shall declare thy mighty acts
point_summary: Each generation bears the responsibility to praise God's works and declare His mighty acts to the next, continuing a chain of testimony about who God is.

context: I will speak of the glorious honor of thy majesty and of thy wondrous works
point_summary: Psalm 145:5's declaration "I will speak of the glorious honor of thy majesty" is presented as a personal statement of commitment and determination to proclaim God's greatness to others.

context: Similar to what we read in Psalm 78, that generational influence
point_summary: Psalm 78's theme of generational transmission—passing knowledge of God's mighty acts to the next generation—parallels Psalm 145:4's call for one generation to declare His works to another.

context: Wilt thou not revive us again?
point_summary: Revival is God's sovereign work alone — the psalmist's cry "Wilt thou not revive us again?" is cited to establish that revival cannot be manufactured by human effort but must be granted by God.

context: It is time for thee, Lord, to work, for they have made void thy law.
point_summary: Desperation over the voiding of God's law becomes the ground for crying out, "It is time for thee, Lord, to work"—revival being wholly God's work, not something humans can manufacture.

context: create in me a clean heart, O God, and renew a right spirit within me
point_summary: David's prayer for a clean heart in Psalm 51:10 illustrates the sermon's call for complete spiritual cleansing rather than mere incremental improvement, distinguishing between being "cleaner" and truly clean.

context: purge me with hyssop and I shall be clean
point_summary: David's prayer in Psalm 51:7 — "purge me with hyssop and I shall be clean" — is cited as the foundation for his deeper plea in verse 10, illustrating that true revival requires complete cleansing, not merely becoming cleaner.

reference_text: Create in me a clean heart
context: Create in me a clean heart. Not just cleaner, but clean.
point_summary: Genuine revival requires not merely a cleaner heart but a completely clean one, as David's cry in Psalm 51:10 illustrates the difference between superficial moral improvement and true heart transformation before God.

reference_text: The sacrifices of God are a broken spirit
context: The sacrifices of God are a broken spirit, a broken and a contrite heart. Oh God, thou wilt not despise.
point_summary: Genuine brokenness before God—not merely grief over sin's consequences—is the contrite heart Psalm 51:17 describes as the sacrifice God will not despise.

reference_text: Against Thee and Thee only have I done this wickedness
context: Against Thee and Thee only have I done this wickedness. Have I sinned? I've done this evil in Thy sight.
point_summary: Brokenness over sin must be directed toward God himself as the one offended, not merely toward sin's consequences, as David's confession "against Thee and Thee only have I sinned" demonstrates.

context: Notice what the psalmist says, turn to Psalm chapter 42, if you will.
point_summary: Psalm 42 is introduced as an analogy illustrating the manner and intensity with which believers ought to seek and pursue God personally.

context: As the heart panteth after the water brooks, so panteth my soul after thee, O God.
point_summary: The deer's panting after water brooks serves as the controlling image for what wholehearted, desperate pursuit of God looks like in the believer's soul.

context: My soul thirsteth for God, for the living God. When shall I come and appear before God?
point_summary: Psalm 42:2's image of a thirsting soul is used to illustrate the intensity and desperation with which believers ought to pursue God, longing for communion with him as a parched animal craves water.

context: as the heart panteth after the water brooks, so panteth my soul after thee, O God
point_summary: The deer's desperate panting for water serves as the psalmist's image for an intense, consuming thirst for God that crowds out every distraction.

context: I agree with the psalmist in Psalm 119, 126, but the prophet says, well, there's some things that you need to do.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: the psalmist said he daily loatheth me with benefits
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: lift up our eyes unto the hills from whence cometh our help
point_summary: Lifting eyes away from life's circumstances toward the Lord — echoing the Psalmist's upward gaze — is presented as the posture of a people whose help comes from God alone.

context: our help cometh from the Lord which made heaven and earth
point_summary: Lifting eyes to the hills (Ps. 121:1–2) is cited as the thematic anchor for the prior year's preaching, with the assurance that help comes from the Lord who made heaven and earth.

context: if the foundations be destroyed. What can the righteous do?
point_summary: When earthly foundations seem to be crumbling, Psalm 11:3–4 is read as a paired question and answer: the righteous have recourse because the Lord remains enthroned in His holy temple, sovereign over all.

context: the Lord is in His holy temple the Lord's throne is in heaven his eyes behold his eyelids try The children of men
point_summary: God's sovereignty over earthly powers is grounded in Psalm 11:4's declaration that the Lord's throne is in heaven, from which his eyes observe and test all humanity.

context: thy throne. Oh God is forever and ever The scepter of thy kingdom is a right Scepter
point_summary: God's throne is eternal and his scepter righteous, establishing that no earthly power or opposition can displace him from sovereign rule.

context: God reigneth over the heathen God sitteth upon the throne of his holiness
point_summary: God's universal reign over the nations is affirmed in Psalm 47:8 as the psalmist declares that God sits upon the throne of his holiness, a truth marshaled here alongside other throne-texts to establish that no earthly power supersedes him.

context: we started out this morning by reading a few verses of Scripture from the book of Psalms and Psalm 34
point_summary: Praising God continually pleases Him because He is worthy of praise in every circumstance, as Psalm 34 establishes the foundation for the call in Hebrews 13:15 to offer the sacrifice of praise.

context: this is the day the Lord hath made, we will rejoice and be glad in it
point_summary: Rejoicing in each day, as commanded in Psalm 118:24, is framed as a deliberate decision rooted in continual remembrance of one's salvation, not in favorable circumstances.

context: the Bible says that every word of God is pure
point_summary: The purity of every individual word of God, as declared in Psalm 12:6, grounds the case that literal word-for-word translation is the only method consistent with verbal plenary inspiration.

context: I've enjoyed the study through Psalm 23
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: ...Maybe like we see in the Psalms, in Psalm 12, Lord help, help Lord...
point_summary: Psalm 12:1's compressed cry — "Lord, help" — illustrates that prayer without ceasing includes brief, spontaneous petitions offered in the midst of urgent circumstances, as Nehemiah did before answering the king.

reference_text: the steps of a good man are ordered by the Lord
context: The Bible tells us that the steps of a good man are ordered by the Lord.
point_summary: God's ordering of a good man's steps is cited as the biblical basis for the claim that every person, regardless of life stage, is dependent on divine direction rather than self-navigation.

context: the idols are described throughout the Scriptures, the Psalms, a couple of different Psalms deal with it, where they have ears but they hear not.
point_summary: Confidence in prayer rests on the contrast between deaf, powerless idols—who have ears but cannot hear—and the living God who genuinely hears his people.

context: you read about praise in Psalm 150
point_summary: Psalm 150 is cited to show that praise encompasses both adoration of who God is and thanksgiving for what he does, providing two distinct but related motivations for worship.

context: with you this evening, Psalm 62. We'll be looking at the entirety of the psalm.
point_summary: Psalm 62, ascribed to David, is introduced as a transparent, God-preserved word that will strengthen, encourage, comfort, instruct, and correct God's people through all 12 verses.

context: 'Truly, my soul waiteth upon God. 'From him cometh my salvation.
point_summary: Psalm 62:1 opens David's declaration that his soul waits upon God alone, establishing the psalm's central theme of exclusive trust in God as the sole source of salvation and stability.

context: 'He only is my rock and my salvation. 'He is my defense. 'I shall not be greatly moved.
point_summary: The near-identical repetition of verse 2 in verse 6 is highlighted as a deliberate, strengthening restatement—God alone as rock, salvation, and defense—with the notable intensification that "greatly moved" becomes simply "not be moved."

context: 'How long will ye imagine mischief against a man? 'Ye shall be slain, all of you, 'as a bowing wall shall ye be, 'and as a tottering fence.
