context: For he established a testimony in Jacob, and appointed a law in Israel, which he commanded our fathers, that they should make them known to their children:
point_summary: God's establishment of testimony and law in Israel carried the explicit command that fathers pass this knowledge to their children, ensuring the coming generation would know and declare it.

context: That the generation to come might know them, even the children which should be born; who should arise and declare them to their children:
point_summary: The phrase "the generation to come" appears twice in the psalm (vv. 4 and 6), marking the transmission of God's works and testimonies as the passage's central, repeated concern.

context: That they might set their hope in God, and not forget the works of God, but keep his commandments:
point_summary: Faithful transmission of God's works across generations is the intended outcome, so that children would set their hope in God and keep His commandments rather than repeat the stubborn rebellion of their fathers.

context: And might not be as their fathers, a stubborn and rebellious generation; a generation that set not their heart aright, and whose spirit was not stedfast with God.

context: The Bible says, we will not hide them. from their children.

context: And then you find in verse number five, at the end of the verse, it says that they should make them known to their children.

context: Those are some great verses of scripture in Psalm 145, verses three through six.

context: one generation shall praise thy works to another.

context: It's always been God's plan that his people influence the next generation.

context: Moses said this in Psalm 90 and verse number 2, before the mountains were brought forth or ever thou hadst formed the earth and the world, even from everlasting to everlasting, thou art God.

context: it's interesting as we take that truth and look at a verse of Scripture in the book of Psalms, if you will, in Psalm 107

context: for he spake and it was done. He commanded and it stood still.

context: 'The heavens declare the glory of God, the firmament showeth forth its handiwork.'

context: For He spake, and it was done. He commanded, and it stood fast.

context: Let all the earth fear the Lord: let all the inhabitants of the world stand in awe of him.

context: They that sow in tears shall reap in joy.

context: lift up our eyes into the hills from whence cometh our help
point_summary: Lifting eyes to the hills frames an "upward expectation" — a perspective that recognizes all help as coming from the Lord who made heaven and earth, not from human courage or faith.

context: our help cometh from the Lord, which made heaven and earth
point_summary: Psalm 121:2 grounds the call to an "upward expectation," linking the lifted eyes of the psalmist to trust in the Lord who made heaven and earth as the source of all help.

context: I know that you're there in Psalm 34
point_summary: Psalm 34 opens a sermon on praise by establishing that God's people, amid life's trials and triumphs, are called to be a people who praise Him, since He is worthy of worship.

context: I want to read to you the final psalm of the book of Psalms in Psalm 150
point_summary: Psalm 150, closing the entire Psalter, is presented as a fitting summary of the book's dominant theme: that God's people ought to praise Him regardless of life's trials and triumphs.

context: Praise ye the Lord praise God in his sanctuary Praise him in the firmament of his power
point_summary: Psalm 150 is read as a summative expression of the Psalter's dominant theme, establishing that praise of God is the fitting, universal response of all who have breath.

context: Praise him for his mighty acts praise him according to his excellent greatness
point_summary: Psalm 150:2's call to praise God "for his mighty acts" and "according to his excellent greatness" grounds the sermon's opening claim that God is wholly worthy of praise on account of his perfections and excellent works.

context: Praise him with the sound of the trumpet praise him with the saw tree and harp
point_summary: Psalm 150 is read as a sweeping summation of the Psalter's dominant theme, establishing that God's perfections and excellent works make Him universally worthy of praise.

context: Praise him with the timbrel and dance praise him with stringed instruments and organs
point_summary: Psalm 150 is read as a sweeping summation of the Psalter's dominant theme: that God's people are called to praise Him with every instrument and, ultimately, with every breath.

context: Praise him upon the loud cymbals praise him upon the high sounding cymbals
point_summary: Psalm 150:5, read as part of the psalm's climactic call for universal praise, illustrates that the whole of Psalm 150 summarizes the Psalter's profound and pervasive theme of praising God.

context: Let everything that hath breath praise the Lord praise Ye the Lord
point_summary: Psalm 150:6, as the climactic verse of the entire Psalter, establishes that every breathing creature is called to praise God, whose perfections and excellent works make Him universally worthy of that praise.

context: I will bless the Lord at all times His praise shall continually be in my mouth
point_summary: Praise is the fitting and continual response of God's people to him because he is perfect in every way and worthy of exaltation at all times.

context: My soul shall make her boast in the Lord. The humble shall hear thereof and be glad
point_summary: Boasting in the Lord—praising him continually for his perfections and excellent works—is the fitting, constant response of God's people to who he is.

context: Oh Magnify the Lord with me and let us exalt his name
point_summary: Magnifying God together in praise—as Psalm 34:3 calls believers to do—is presented as the fitting, collective response to a God who is perfect in every way and therefore wholly worthy of worship.

context: his work is honorable and glorious and His righteousness endureth forever
point_summary: God's works are honorable and glorious, and He has ensured they are remembered — a basis for praise grounded in both His perfections and His excellent deeds.

context: He hath made his wonderful works to be remembered
point_summary: God's wonderful works are preserved in Scripture so that his people may continually rehearse and recall them as grounds for praise.

context: let all the earth fear the Lord Let all the inhabitants of the world stand in all of him
point_summary: Awe before God flows naturally from meditating on His character and creative power, making Psalm 33:8's call to worldwide fear a prompt for daily, rehearsed praise.

context: for he spake and it was done he commanded and it stood still
point_summary: God's creative word in Psalm 33:9 — "he spake and it was done" — grounds the call to awe in verse 8, making creation the basis for the praise and worship that naturally flows from drawing near to God.

context: As we look here at Psalm 34, I want to just share a couple of thoughts with you this morning.
point_summary: Psalm 34's opening "I will bless the Lord at all times" reflects a deliberate, volitional resolve to praise—not passive feeling but purposeful commitment, echoed throughout the Psalms in repeated "I will" declarations.

context: I will bless the Lord at all times: his praise shall continually be in my mouth.
point_summary: Psalm 34:1's repeated "I will" language signals a deliberate, volitional resolve to praise God continually—not a passive response, but a purposeful commitment the psalmist makes.

context: My soul shall make her boast in the Lord: the humble shall hear thereof, and be glad.
point_summary: Boasting in the Lord is not self-promotion but humble, resolved praise, as the psalmist's repeated "I will" language throughout the Psalms signals deliberate, ongoing commitment to glorify God.

context: O magnify the Lord with me, and let us exalt his name together.
point_summary: Magnifying the Lord is a communal, resolved act — the psalmist's call in verse 3 illustrates that praise is not passive but a deliberate, shared commitment to exalt God's name together.

context: Let the redeemed of the Lord say so, whom he hath redeemed from the hand of the enemy;
point_summary: Psalm 107:2's call for the redeemed to "say so" is invoked as a warrant for vocal praise and personal testimony about what God has done.

context: O give thanks unto the Lord for he is good, for his mercy endureth forever.
point_summary: Psalm 107:1's call to give thanks is used to ground a broader exhortation that praising God should be a natural, resolved commitment of the believer, not something laboriously worked up.

context: O that men would praise the Lord for his goodness and for his wonderful works to the children of men.
point_summary: Psalm 107:8's repeated refrain—"O that men would praise the LORD for his goodness"—is cited as a fourfold call within the psalm grounding the sermon's appeal that praise should be the natural, resolved commitment of God's people.

context: but murmured in their tents and hearken not unto the voice of the Lord.
point_summary: Murmuring "within their tents" illustrates that Israel's complaining was private and habitual, contrasted with the psalmist's deliberate resolve to praise the Lord instead.

context: the Lord is my shepherd
point_summary: David's declaration "The Lord is my shepherd" is cited as evidence of his closeness with God and offered as a truth to fill the mind, supporting a call to resolved, wholehearted praise.

context: in his presence is fullness of joy
point_summary: Wholehearted resolve to praise God—not passive feeling but deliberate, purposed decision—is set against Israel's pattern of murmuring, with Psalm 16:11's "fullness of joy in his presence" grounding why such resolve is warranted.

context: I will praise thee, O Lord, with my whole heart; I will shew forth all thy marvellous works.
point_summary: Wholehearted praise—not dutiful or performative—is the resolve David models in Psalm 9:1, offering a pattern of intentional, total-hearted thanksgiving each believer needs.

context: Let the redeemed of the Lord say so.
point_summary: Psalm 107:2's call for the redeemed to declare what God has done grounds an appeal for wholehearted, testimony-driven praise rather than reluctant or dutiful acknowledgment.

context: the truth of Psalm 68, that he, God, hath daily loatheth us with benefits.
point_summary: Psalm 68's declaration that God daily loads his people with benefits grounds the call to wholehearted, overflowing praise—so much so that time itself runs short when counting his blessings.

context: Psalm 69, in verse number 30. In Psalm 69, in verse number 30, the psalmist says, I will praise the name of God with a psalm and will magnify him with thanksgiving.
point_summary: Praising God with song and thanksgiving is a deliberate, personal resolve—the psalmist's declaration that worship through music magnifies God himself.

context: I will praise thee, O Lord my God, with all my heart, and I will glorify thy name forevermore.
point_summary: Wholehearted, perpetual praise and glorifying God's name are presented as the fitting response to answered prayer and God's attentiveness to his people.

context: I will praise thee, notice this, for thou hast heard me and art become my salvation.
point_summary: Praise is the fitting response when God has heard prayer, as the psalmist's vow—"I will praise thee, for thou hast heard me"—ties answered prayer directly to the obligation of thanksgiving.

context: I will praise thee in Psalm 139 and verse number 14, why? For I am wonderfully made.
point_summary: The personal knowledge that God is one's own Creator grounds the psalmist's resolve to praise Him, linking creaturely dependence to worshipful devotion.

context: praise thee for I am fearfully and wonderfully made, marvelous are thy works and that my soul knoweth right well.
point_summary: The psalmist's declaration "I am fearfully and wonderfully made" grounds praise in the truth that God is personal Creator, making worship a fitting response to his intimate, crafting knowledge of each person.

context: I will bless the Lord at all times and his praise shall continually be in my mouth
point_summary: Psalm 34:1's "at all times" is read as a call not merely to resolve praise but to practice it regularly and without interruption, paralleled with 1 Thessalonians 5's "in everything give thanks."

context: In Psalm 108 in verse number 3 the psalmist says this, I will praise thee O Lord among the people and I will sing praises unto thee among the nations.
point_summary: Private praise of God, when cultivated consistently, naturally overflows into public declaration among others, as the psalmist models by vowing to sing praises among the peoples and nations.

context: if we're mindful of what the psalmist said in Psalm 34, when he said that he was going to praise the Lord, bless the Lord, it says at all times.
point_summary: Psalm 34:1's declaration to bless the Lord "at all times" sets an unconditional standard for praise that extends beyond favorable circumstances into seasons of adversity.

reference_text: Taste and see that the Lord is good.
context: Taste and see that the Lord is good.
point_summary: Tasting and seeing that the Lord is good means recognizing His goodness not as the absence of trouble, but as His faithfulness demonstrated through every storm.

context: Psalm 103 says not only bless his holy name but all that is within me bless his holy name.
point_summary: Wholehearted, passionate praise—"all that is within me"—is the standard Psalm 103:1 sets for God's people, contrasted with the unthankfulness that marks the perilous times.

context: notice what he says here in Psalm 100 and verse number four, enter into his gates, how?
point_summary: Entering God's presence is properly characterized by thanksgiving and praise, contrasting the unthankful spirit Scripture warns will mark perilous times.

context: Enter into his gates with thanksgiving, and into his courts with praise: be thankful unto him, and bless his name.
point_summary: Entering God's presence with thanksgiving and praise, as Psalm 100:4 commands, should characterize both corporate worship and personal prayer time, beginning with blessing God for His goodness.

context: Praise ye the Lord. Praise God in his sanctuary. Praise him in the firmament of his power.
point_summary: Psalm 150's repeated command to praise—spanning sanctuary, instruments, and every breathing creature—is read in full to underscore that the Christian's life, including prayer, should be saturated with praise to God.

context: Praise him for his mighty acts. Praise him according to his excellent greatness.
point_summary: Psalm 150:2 is read in full to underscore that praise—repeated throughout every verse—should saturate the believer's prayer life and corporate worship as God's own design.

context: Praise him with the sound of the trumpet. Praise him with the psalter and harp.
point_summary: Psalm 150's repeated command to praise—across sanctuary, instruments, and all breathing creatures—is cited to show that praise should saturate the believer's prayer life and corporate worship.

context: Praise him with the timbrel and dance. Praise him with stringed instruments and organs.
point_summary: Psalm 150's repeated command to praise—across instruments, settings, and all breathing creatures—is cited to show that praise should saturate the believer's prayer life and corporate worship.

context: Praise him upon the loud cymbals. Praise him upon the high-sounding cymbals.
point_summary: Psalm 150's repeated command to praise—across instruments, settings, and all breathing creatures—is cited to show that praise should saturate the believer's prayer life and corporate worship.

context: Let everything that hath breath praise the Lord. Praise ye the Lord.
point_summary: Psalm 150's repeated command to praise—culminating in "let everything that hath breath praise the Lord"—grounds the call for believers to characterize both personal prayer and corporate worship with active, wholehearted praise.

context: Notice when he says in verse 2, praise him for his mighty acts. Praise him according to his excellent greatness.
point_summary: Psalm 150:2 grounds the call to praise not only in God's acts but in his very being—his holiness, sovereignty, omnipotence, and other attributes that make him worthy of worship in himself.

reference_text: a very present help in the time of trouble
context: he's my helper. He's a very present help in the time of trouble.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: in the book of Psalms in Psalm chapter number 2
point_summary: Psalm 2's depiction of nations raging against God in vain finds its parallel in Babel's humanistic rebellion, with God's laughter from heaven underscoring that all power belongs to Him alone.

context: Psalm chapter number 2 the Bible says why do the heathen rage? And the people imagine a vain thing
point_summary: Psalm 2:1's depiction of nations raging and imagining vain things is applied to humanity's God-excluding, self-exalting rebellion, which God meets not with alarm but with laughter and derision.

context: the kings of the earth set themselves and the rulers take counsel together against the Lord and against his anointed saying
point_summary: Psalm 2's depiction of nations raging against God in vain is applied to the Babel builders' humanistic rebellion, showing that God, enthroned in heaven, laughs at every plan devised without Him.

context: Let us break their bands asunder and cast away their cords from us.
point_summary: Human rebellion against God is ultimately laughable to the enthroned Lord, whose sovereign derision and wrath render all godless, humanistic opposition vain and powerless.

context: He that sitteth in the heavens shall laugh: the Lord shall have them in derision.
point_summary: God's sovereign laughter at human rebellion in Psalm 2:4 illustrates that all opposition to the Almighty—from Babel to the final revolt in Revelation 20—is ultimately laughable before him.

context: Then shall he speak unto them in his wrath, and vex them in his sore displeasure.
point_summary: God's spoken word of wrath against those who rebel in humanistic self-exaltation is so decisive that their opposition is rendered laughable before the enthroned Lord.

context: I preached a message not long ago, just a couple of Sundays ago. And as I preached from Psalms, and I really was dealing with the truth of really pour out your heart before him in Psalm 62.
point_summary: Psalm 62's call to pour out your heart before God underscores that Christ alone fully knows what his people are facing, even when others cannot or should not.

context: Psalm 62 this morning, Psalm 62, I'll be looking at the entirety of the psalm.
point_summary: Psalm 62 is introduced as a model of transparency before God, with David's practice of pouring out his heart serving as an encouragement and pattern for believers in their own daily walk.

context: Truly, my soul waiteth upon God. From Him cometh my salvation.
point_summary: Psalm 62:1's repeated phrase "He only" is highlighted as a key word marking God alone as the soul's rock, salvation, and defense.

context: He only is my rock and my salvation. He is my defense. I shall not be greatly moved.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Trust in Him at all times, ye people. Pour out your heart before Him. God is a refuge for us, Selah.
point_summary: Psalm 62:8's command to "pour out your heart before Him" is presented as the sermon's governing theme, grounding the entire message in the call to transparent, trust-filled prayer before God as refuge.

reference_text: truly my soul waiteth upon God, from Him cometh my salvation
context: And the psalmist says, truly my soul waiteth upon God, from Him cometh my salvation.
point_summary: Waiting on God in the storm is the hardest part, yet Psalm 62:1 grounds that waiting in confident trust that deliverance comes from Him alone.

context: how long will you imagine mischief against a man you shall be slain all of you as a bowing wall shall you be in as a tottering fence
point_summary: Verse 3's imagery of a bowing wall and tottering fence portrays David's enemies as fundamentally unstable and lacking strength, contrasting their threatening mischief with the solid foundation David finds in God.

context: it is time for the Lord to work for they have made void thy law
point_summary: Cited alongside Psalm 62:5's call to wait on God alone, Psalm 119:126's cry "it is time for the Lord to work" grounds the expectation that God will act on behalf of those who wait.

context: Trust God. Pour out your heart before Him... God is a refuge for us. Selah.
point_summary: Pouring out one's heart before God is grounded in the closing declaration of verse 8—"God is a refuge for us"—which the psalmist offers as the explanatory reason why believers should trust Him at all times.

context: trust in Him at all times in verse 8
point_summary: Trusting God at all times is grounded in His being a refuge—a shelter and protection—available equally to all His children, in contrast to the vanity of trusting in man or riches.

context: Surely men of low degree are vanity and men of high degree are a lie
point_summary: Human beings of every social rank — low and high alike — are collectively lighter than vanity on the scales, making mankind an utterly unreliable foundation for trust compared to God.

context: Trust not in oppression and become not vain in robbery
point_summary: Wealth and human status are explicitly named as false objects of trust, contrasted with the call to trust God at all times, because riches and men of every rank are ultimately weightless vanity.

context: from the end of the earth will I cry unto thee
point_summary: When the heart is overwhelmed, the right response is to cry out to God, trusting that He leads to a rock higher than ourselves — moving, as the psalmist did, from sorrow to praise.

context: when my heart is overwhelmed. Lead me to the rock that is higher than I.
point_summary: When the heart is overwhelmed, Psalm 61:2 shows David crying out to God and asking to be led to a rock higher than himself — modeling the movement from overwhelm to praise.

context: He says in verse number one of that psalm, I can hear my cry
point_summary: Psalm 61:1 is cited as one of several psalms where the writer moves from overwhelming distress to praise, illustrating the pattern of pouring out one's heart before God.

context: he says in verse number eight, so will I sing praise unto thy name
point_summary: Psalm 61:8 illustrates the movement from an overwhelmed heart to praise, showing that honest prayer before God leads to singing.

context: how long wilt thou forget me, O Lord? Forever?
point_summary: Psalm 13 illustrates the movement from overwhelming grief to praise, showing that honest lament before God does not end in despair but in singing.

context: I will sing unto the Lord because he hath dealt bountifully with me in verse six
point_summary: Psalm 13 illustrates the movement from overwhelming sorrow to singing, as David shifts from "how long wilt thou forget me" to declaring God has dealt bountifully with him.

context: He is a very present help in the time of trouble
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: the Lord is my shepherd
point_summary: Gentile believers, grafted into covenant blessing through Calvary, may personally claim "the LORD is my shepherd" as recipients of new-covenant salvation distinct from Israel's national promises.

context: These things, right, we unto you, that your joy may be full in His presence, His fullness of joy, according to the psalmist.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: we sing for our theme song this year from Psalm 121, I will lift up mine eyes into the hills, from whence cometh my help
point_summary: Psalm 121:1–2, cited as the congregation's theme song, anchors the sermon's affirmation that the Lord who made heaven and earth is the Creator, corroborating the Genesis record against those who deny it.

context: Look over to the book of Psalms, if you will, Psalm 29. I want to reference just a couple of verses of Scripture here in the book of Psalms to understand and lay the groundwork for the fact that God indeed is worthy of our worship. In Psalm 29, in verse 2, the Bible says this.
point_summary: Worship is owed to God because His name carries inherent worth—the English word itself encoding the concept of "worth-ship"—making reverent, holy adoration the fitting response to who He is.

context: Give unto the Lord, O ye mighty, give unto the Lord glory and strength.
point_summary: Psalm 29:1's command to "give unto the LORD glory and strength" grounds the claim that God is inherently worthy of worship—a worthiness embedded in the very English etymology of the word "worth-ship."

context: oh, worship the Lord and the beauty of holiness. Fear before Him all the earth.
point_summary: Worship offered to God must be rendered in the beauty of holiness, with reverent fear, because He alone is worthy — a worthiness embedded in the very English word "worship-ship."

context: He's after the order of Melchizedek and obvious fulfillment of Psalm 110 and verse number 4, that messianic prophecy
point_summary: Psalm 110:4 is identified as a messianic prophecy finding its proper fulfillment in Jesus Christ, establishing His priesthood after the order of Melchizedek as superior to the Levitical order.

reference_text: if I regard iniquity in my heart, the Lord will not hear me
context: ...The psalmist said, if I regard iniquity in my heart, the Lord will not hear me...
point_summary: Harboring unconfessed sin hinders a believer's fellowship with God, as the psalmist's warning that God will not hear one who regards iniquity in his heart confirms.

context: Remember David as he prayed, as he made confession in Psalm 51, he says, return unto me the joy of my salvation.
point_summary: Psalm 51:12's cry to "restore the joy of my salvation" illustrates that unconfessed sin disrupts daily fellowship with God, which the indwelling Holy Spirit convicts believers to address through confession.

context: psalmist talks about this in Psalm 139, search me. Amen? Show me if there be any wicked way in me.
point_summary: Psalm 139's petition—"search me… show me if there be any wicked way in me"—is invoked as the believer's posture of desiring complete cleansing, not merely incremental moral improvement.

reference_text: In His presence is fullness of joy
context: In His presence is fullness of joy
point_summary: Psalms is cited briefly to affirm that in God's presence is fullness of joy, grounding the call to holy living in the sweetness of unbroken fellowship with the Father.

context: I will lift up mine eyes unto the hills. From whence cometh my help?
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: My help cometh from the Lord, remember, which made heaven and earth.
point_summary: Cited as assurance that no problem exceeds God's power, grounding the believer's upward gaze in the One who made heaven and earth.

reference_text: restore unto me the joy of thy salvation
context: That we might have, as David said, restore unto me the joy of thy salvation.
point_summary: Unconfessed sin breaks fellowship with God, and David's cry in Psalm 51 — "restore unto me the joy of thy salvation" — is held up as the believer's path back to that clean heart and sweet communion.

context: For thou, O God, hast proved us. Thou hast tried us as silver is tried.
point_summary: God sovereignly ordains trials for His people, as Psalm 66:10 confirms that it is He who proves and tries believers as silver is tried.

context: as God says in Psalm 12, of God as silver tried in a furnace seven times
point_summary: Gold tried in a furnace seven times in Psalm 12:6 illustrates the refining purity that God works in believers through trial, removing impurities just as fire purifies silver.

context: I will lift up mine eyes into the hills, from whence cometh my help?
point_summary: Help comes from the Lord who made heaven and earth — cited from Psalm 121:1–2 to affirm that God is not only able to help His people but personally available to do so.

context: My help cometh from the Lord, which made heaven and earth.
point_summary: Help comes from the Lord who made heaven and earth — the Creator God is personally available as Helper, grounding the Ebenezer memorial's declaration that "hitherto hath the Lord helped us."

reference_text: The heavens declare the glory of God
context: The Bible tells us that the heavens declare the glory of God.
point_summary: While creation reveals that a God exists, Scripture alone discloses His attributes — making the Bible indispensable for knowing God truly.

context: And we find that as we come to Psalm 54, just as the cases with many of the Psalms, you find that there is a particular event that is the background or the setting for that particular Psalm.
point_summary: Reading the Psalms daily—five a day through the month—both encourages and challenges the believer, and Psalm 54 is grounded in a specific historical event: David's experience at Keilah in 1 Samuel 23.

context: David says, save me, O God, by thy name, and judge me by thy strength.
point_summary: David's urgent cry — "save me, O God, by thy name" — is grounded in the desperate historical circumstances of 1 Samuel 23, where God's name and strength are the sole basis of appeal for deliverance.

context: Hear my prayer, O God, give ear to the words of my mouth.
point_summary: David's urgent plea — "Hear my prayer, O God" — is grounded in the desperate circumstances of 1 Samuel 23, where being completely surrounded gave his cry for deliverance a measure of urgency.

context: lift up our eyes under the hills from whence cometh our help
point_summary: Psalm 121:1 is invoked to affirm that God is both available and able to deliver, with "our help cometh from the Lord" grounding David's urgent cry for salvation in divine power over all creation.

context: a very present help in the time of trouble
point_summary: God's identity as "a very present help in the time of trouble" is invoked to affirm that he is not merely available but able to deliver, grounding David's urgent prayer in Psalm 54 in divine power.

context: God is mine helper. The Lord is with them that uphold my soul.
point_summary: Amid genuine military accomplishment, David's declaration "God is mine helper" reflects his recognition that deliverance belonged to God, not to his own skill or strength.

context: for he hath delivered me out of all trouble and mine eye hath seen his desire upon mine enemies
point_summary: Recalling past deliverances, as David does in verse 7, is presented as a beneficial practice that produces worship and builds expectation that God, still on his throne, will act again.

context: he shall reward evil unto mine enemies
point_summary: Verse 5 is briefly contrasted with verse 7 to show that David's confidence in God's judgment on enemies is grounded in remembered past deliverance, not merely future hope.

context: And we find that as we enter into this thought from Psalm 54 and noticing what the psalmist says, I will praise thy name
point_summary: Recalling past deliverances—as David does in verse 7—fuels purposeful, intentional praise, because rehearsing what God has done produces both worship and expectation of future faithfulness.

context: I will bless the Lord at all times, his praise shall continually be in my mouth.
point_summary: Psalm 34:1's declaration "I will bless the Lord at all times" illustrates that praise must be a deliberate, intentional choice, not a natural default, since human beings instinctively default to complaint rather than worship.

context: Psalm 108, beginning in verse number one, the Bible says this, Psalm 108 and verse number one, oh God, my heart is fixed. I will sing and give praise even with my glory.
point_summary: Purposing the heart in praise—declaring "my heart is fixed"—is presented as the believer's deliberate, settled commitment to worship rather than complaint, grounded in God's mercy and truth.

context: Awake, psaltery and harp. I myself will awake early.
point_summary: Purposing oneself to rise early and praise—"I myself will awake early"—illustrates that genuine, Spirit-filled praise is a deliberate, fixed commitment, not a passive default.

context: I will praise thee, oh Lord, among the people and I will sing praises unto thee among the nations.
point_summary: Purposeful, fixed-hearted praise among the peoples flows from the psalmist's settled conviction that God is merciful and his truth reaches to the clouds.

context: Why, notice what he says in verse number four. For thy mercy is great above the heavens and thy truth reacheth under the clouds.
point_summary: God's surpassing mercy and truth reaching to the clouds are the stated reasons the psalmist's heart is fixed and his praise is purposeful rather than defaulting to complaint.

context: I will praise Thee. Why? For I am fearfully and wonderfully made, marvelous are Thy works, and that my soul knoweth right well.
point_summary: The psalmist's declaration "I will praise thee" in Psalm 139:14 is cited as evidence of deliberate, purposeful praise grounded in the recognition that God's works in creating mankind are marvelous.

context: You read Psalm 150. We won't take time to read it this morning, but if you read Psalm 150, you'll find that the psalmist says, let everything that hath breath praise the Lord. Praise ye the Lord.
point_summary: Psalm 150's six verses are read as a model of praise directed first toward who God is and then toward what He has done, with the implication that praising God for His immutable character ensures unceasing reason to rejoice.

context: As the psalmist said in Psalm 118 and verse 24, this is the day the Lord hath made. We will rejoice and be glad in it
point_summary: Psalm 118:24 grounds the call to intentional, daily rejoicing in the conviction that every day belongs to God and carries His plan and purpose, regardless of circumstances.

context: I will praise thee forever, because thou hast done it, and I will wait on thy name for it is good before thy saints.
point_summary: David's vow to praise God "forever" in Psalm 52:9 grounds persistent, perpetual praise in God's unchanging character rather than in changing circumstances.

context: from the rising of the sun unto the going down of the same, the Lord's name is to be praised
point_summary: From sunrise to sunset, the full span of each day is the appointed time for God's people to praise His name, making praise persistent and perpetual rather than circumstantial.

context: praise ye the Lord, praise, O ye servants of the Lord, praise the name of the Lord
point_summary: Psalm 113:1's threefold call to "praise the name of the Lord" establishes that God's people are commanded to offer persistent, perpetual praise spanning from sunrise to sunset every day.

context: blessed be the name of the Lord from this time forth and forevermore
point_summary: Blessed be the name of the Lord from this time forth and forevermore establishes that persistent, perpetual praise—spanning all of life's changing circumstances—is the fitting response to an unchanging God.

context: every day will I bless thee, and I will praise thy name forever and ever
point_summary: Daily, unceasing praise is the psalmist's personal vow, cited to establish that persistent praise—"every day"—is the expected pattern for God's people.

context: great is the Lord, and greatly to be praised, and His greatness is unsearchable
point_summary: God's greatness being unsearchable grounds the call to persistent, daily praise—every day, not merely in favorable circumstances.

context: Notice what he says in Psalm 9 in verse number 1. I love the phraseology here, and it's certainly convicting.
point_summary: Wholehearted praise, as the psalmist declares in Psalm 9:1, is passionate and total—not half-hearted or coerced, but freely given because God is worthy and abundantly good.

reference_text: make a joyful noise unto the Lord
context: God said, make a joyful noise unto the Lord, and I stood up to sing
point_summary: Wholehearted, passionate praise—not half-hearted or coerced—is the fitting response to a God who is worthy and abundantly good, as the command to make a joyful noise demands.

context: The psalmist says, I will praise thee, but he says, with my whole heart. In Psalm 86, in verse number 12, the psalmist says this, and again, just being consistent with this thought of being passionate, he says, I will praise thee, O Lord, my God, with all my heart, and I will glorify thy name forevermore.
point_summary: Wholehearted praise—"with all my heart"—is held up as the biblical standard for passionate, purposeful worship, contrasting cultural formality with the psalmist's total devotion.

context: The psalmist says this in Psalm 119 and verse number 140, thy word is very pure, therefore thy servant loveth it.
point_summary: Scripture's purity—affirmed in Psalm 119:140 ("thy word is very pure")—is grounded in its divine origin, meaning everything from Genesis to Revelation is absolutely reliable in all it records.

context: More to be desired are they than gold, yea, than much fine gold. Sweeter also than honey and the honeycomb.
point_summary: Scripture's authority is grounded in its divine origin, making God's Word more desirable than gold and sweeter than honey—surpassing any human authority.

context: Moreover, by them is thy servant warned, and in keeping of them there is great reward.
point_summary: Scripture's authority is grounded in what it is—God's own Word—so that its warnings and rewards carry the weight of the eternal, omnipotent God who rules and reigns.

context: I think this morning that it's in our best interest as we find in Psalm 19
point_summary: Scripture's authority rests not in human position but in God's own nature as eternal, omnipotent Lord, making His Word's warnings—as Psalm 19:11 states—binding and beneficial to heed.

context: We find that it was David in Psalm 51. We won't take the time this evening to read really any of that portion of Scripture, but it is of interest to note that in part of that prayer, he says, restore unto me the joy of thy salvation.
point_summary: Psalm 51 is cited as David's model of specific, identified confession—where named sin stole his joy, and restoration required honest acknowledgment before God.

context: have I sinned and done this wickedness? And you read his confession in Psalm 51 and what a powerful passage of Scripture it is.
point_summary: David's confession in Psalm 51—particularly his plea to "restore unto me the joy of thy salvation"—is cited to show that unconfessed sin steals joy, and that genuine repentance involves identifying specific sin before God.

context: Here in Psalm 86 and verse number 5, the Bible says, for thou, Lord, art good and ready to forgive and plenteous in mercy unto all them that call upon Thee.
point_summary: God's readiness to forgive is grounded in His character and nature — He is "good and ready to forgive and plenteous in mercy" — making repentant prayer both possible and confident.

context: In Psalm 130 and verse number 4, the psalmist says that we should rejoice. Why? In Psalm 130 and verse number 4, but there is forgiveness with Thee.
point_summary: Forgiveness is presented as rooted in God's very character, with Psalm 130:4 cited as the psalmist's basis for rejoicing—because there is forgiveness with God.

context: restore unto me the joy of thy salvation
point_summary: Restored joy and fellowship with God — not initial salvation — is the fruit David sought in confessing sin, framing repentance as the believer's ongoing return to God.

context: Children are an heritage of the Lord
point_summary: Children are a heritage and reward from God, and that gift carries with it the exclusive parental responsibility to teach them, for which parents will give account.

context: Turn over to the book of Psalms, if you will, and then we'll close with a New Testament truth as well. But look at Psalm 78, if you will, and I want you to notice the first several verses of this great chapter.
point_summary: Psalm 78's opening verses are cited to underscore the generational obligation to transmit God's truth, warning that failure to do so produces a rising generation that does not know the Lord.

context: and Psalm 78. Notice what the Bible says, give ear, O my people, to my law. Incline your ears to the words of my mouth.
point_summary: Psalm 78's command to declare God's praises and mighty works to the next generation underscores that parents, not cultural institutions, bear the God-given responsibility to teach children of his greatness.

context: for he established a testimony in Jacob and appointed a law in Israel which he commanded our fathers that they should make them known to their children
point_summary: God's establishment of testimony and law in Israel carried an explicit generational mandate: fathers were commanded to make these things known to their children so that future generations would set their hope in God.

context: That the generation to come in verse number six might know them, even the children which should be born who should arise and declare them to their children
point_summary: Faithful transmission of God's testimony across generations is the stated purpose of teaching children—so that those yet unborn would arise, declare God's works, and set their hope in him.

reference_text: in the book of Psalms that we're to give thanks, the remembrance of his holiness
context: we're instructed in the book of Psalms that we're to give thanks, the remembrance of his holiness.
point_summary: Gratitude for God's holiness is grounded in Psalms, which instructs giving thanks at the remembrance of his holiness—a holiness that is not developing but eternally complete.

context: ...set their hope in God, not forget the works of God, keep His commandments...
point_summary: Faithful instruction of the Word across generations aims at this outcome: that a future generation would set their hope in God, not forget His works, and keep His commandments.

context: Psalm 119, we'll begin in verse number 97
point_summary: Psalm 119:97's declaration of love for God's law grounds the sermon's opening affirmation that Scripture is a treasure more precious than gold, a gift divinely inspired and preserved for believers.

context: Notice with me what the psalmist says in Psalm 119. Of course this whole chapter is devoted to the love and to the truth of God's Word and the love of God's Word. And here the psalmist comes in verse number 97, oh how love I thy law, it is my meditation all the day.
point_summary: Meditation on God's Word throughout the day flows naturally from a deep, emotionally felt love for it—the psalmist's exclamation expressing both affection and constant, all-day engagement with Scripture.

context: oh how love I thy law, it is my meditation all the day
point_summary: Positioned near the center of Psalm 119's sustained meditation on Scripture, the psalmist's exclamation "O how love I thy law" expresses emotionally charged, wholehearted love for God's Word as the fitting response of a believer.

context: thy word is very pure, therefore thy servant loveth it
point_summary: The Word's purity is itself the ground of the psalmist's love for it: because Scripture is very pure, the servant's love for it follows as a direct response.

context: consider how I love thy precepts. Quicken me, O Lord, according to thy lovingkindness.
point_summary: Loving God's Word must move beyond verbal declaration to behavioral proof, as the psalmist's appeal—"consider how I love thy precepts"—implies a life that visibly confirms the claim.

context: Thy word is true from the beginning and every one of thy righteous judgments endureth forever.
point_summary: The enduring truth of God's Word ("thy word is true from the beginning") grounds the psalmist's love for Scripture in objective reality, not mere sentiment.

reference_text: I hate and abhor lying, but thy law do I love
context: Notice in verse 163, I hate and abhor lying, but thy law do I love.
point_summary: Hatred of lying and love of God's law are set in direct contrast, illustrating that genuine love for Scripture is proven by what one also despises in behavior.

context: Notice verse 165, great peace have they
point_summary: Those who genuinely love God's law are promised great peace and an unoffendable spirit, linking authentic love for Scripture to a tangible, observable fruit in the believer's life.

reference_text: great peace have they which love thy law, and nothing shall offend them
context: And we find here that the psalmist says, great peace have they which love thy law, and nothing shall offend them.
point_summary: Great peace belongs to those who love God's law, and that peace—contrasted with the restlessness of the world—flows from meditating on and feasting upon Scripture.

reference_text: my soul hath kept thy testimonies, and I love them exceedingly
context: My soul, verse 167, my soul hath kept thy testimonies. And notice what he says, and I love them exceedingly.
point_summary: Obedient keeping of God's testimonies and exceedingly intense love for them are inseparable, the psalmist's language pressing beyond ordinary affection to its fullest degree.

context: I love thy law exceedingly
point_summary: Loving and learning Scripture form a self-reinforcing cycle, so that the psalmist's declaration of exceeding love in verse 167 reflects the deeper intimacy with God gained through diligent study.

context: the heavens declare the glory of God
point_summary: Creation's declaration of God's glory in Psalm 19:1 is presented as the foundation from which the psalmist moves to extol the Word of God, with both creation and Scripture pointing to the same Creator.

context: the law of the Lord is perfect, converting the soul
point_summary: Psalm 19:7–10 presents the Word of God as perfect, sure, right, and pure—surpassing gold and honey in value—before the sermon's focus lands on verse 11's warning function of Scripture.

context: teach me thy way, O Lord. I want to learn it. Why? I will walk in thy truth, unite my heart to fear thy name.
point_summary: Psalm 86:11's cry "teach me thy way, O Lord; I will walk in thy truth" is cited as the psalmist's pattern of loving God's Word, learning it, and committing to live it out.

context: lift our eyes to the hills from whence cometh our help
point_summary: Lifting one's eyes to the hills (Ps. 121:1) is invoked as the corrective to temporal fixation—shifting focus from present hardship to the eternal help that comes from the Lord.

context: Our help cometh from the Lord which made heaven and earth
point_summary: Lifting one's eyes to the hills (Ps. 121:1–2) is invoked as the corrective to temporal fixation—shifting focus from present hardship to the eternal help that comes from the Lord.

context: the title of the message is from Psalm 139 and verse number 14, a very familiar verse of scripture. And the title of the message, I am fearfully and wonderfully made.
point_summary: Psalm 139:14 frames the sermon's central claim that humanity is God's most treasured creation, reaching the climax of the Genesis 1 creation account on day six.

context: I will praise thee, why in verse number 14, for I am fearfully and wonderfully made.
point_summary: Psalm 139:14 grounds personal praise in the conviction that every individual has been uniquely designed by God, making "I am fearfully and wonderfully made" a testimony each believer can claim as his own.

context: My substance was not hid from thee when I was made in secret and curiously wrought in the lowest parts of the earth.
point_summary: Psalm 139:15's reference to being "made in secret and curiously wrought" is presented as testimony to the wonder of God's intimate involvement in the process of human reproduction following creation.

context: Thine eyes did see my substance yet being unperfect. And in thy book, all my members were written, which in continuance were fashioned when as yet there was none of them.
point_summary: God's foreknowledge of each person is implicit in verse 16's declaration that every member was written in his book before any of them existed, affirming divine intentionality in human formation.

context: How precious also are thy thoughts unto me, O God. How great is the sum of them.
point_summary: God's thoughts toward each person are so numerous they exceed the count of sand, underscoring the immeasurable personal attention He gives to every individual He has made.

context: If I should count them, they're more in number than the sand. When I awake, I am still with thee.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I will praise thee for I am fearfully and wonderfully made.
point_summary: David's praise in verse 14 — "I will praise thee, for I am fearfully and wonderfully made" — is presented as a personal, individual testimony that every believer can claim, grounding human dignity in being uniquely designed by God.

context: know ye that the Lord, he is God. It is he that hath made us and not we ourselves. We are his people and the sheep of his pasture.
point_summary: Psalm 100:3 grounds the personal testimony of Psalm 139 in a universal declaration: human existence originates entirely in God's creative act, not in ourselves.

context: For thou hast possessed my reins.
point_summary: Psalm 139:13's reference to God possessing the reins and covering David in the womb is read as Scripture's direct answer to the modern question of when life begins, locating personhood at conception.

context: for thou hast possessed my reins. Thou hast covered me, where? In my mother's womb.
point_summary: Psalm 139:13's reference to God possessing David's "reins" (internal organs) and covering him in the womb directly answers the contemporary debate over when life begins, grounding personhood in God's sovereign creative act before birth.

context: my substance was not hid from thee when I was made in secret and curiously wrought.
point_summary: God's intricate formation of the human body in the womb is likened to skilled needlework or embroidery, with "curiously wrought" conveying the deliberate, artisan-like craftsmanship of the Creator.

context: it's interesting in Psalm 147, and we stand in amazement to know the truth that God, he knows their number, and he calls them all by their names
point_summary: God's intimate knowledge of every star by name, established in Psalm 147:4, grounds the assurance that he likewise knows each individual person — who, unlike the stars, he died for.

context: O Lord, thou hast searched me and known me.
point_summary: God's exhaustive knowledge of every person — thoughts, movements, words, and circumstances — establishes that being "known by God" is the personal counterpart to his cosmic knowledge of the stars.

context: Thou knowest my down-sitting and my up-rising. Notice what he says, thou understandest my thought afar off.
point_summary: God's exhaustive knowledge of every person — thoughts, words, paths, and circumstances — is set in contrast to his knowledge of the stars, magnifying the wonder that he still loves what he fully knows.

context: Thou compassest my path and my lying down, and art acquainted with all my ways,
point_summary: God's exhaustive knowledge of every person — thoughts, paths, words, and circumstances — is presented as the foundation for the assurance that being known by an omniscient God does not diminish but deepens His love for us.

context: for there's not a word in my tongue, but lo, O Lord, thou knowest it all together.
point_summary: God's exhaustive knowledge of every word before it is spoken — cited from verse 4 — grounds the sermon's claim that believers are fully and personally known by God.

context: Notice what the Bible says back in Psalm 139. I want you to look at verses 17 and 18.
point_summary: God's thoughts toward each person are declared precious and innumerable—surpassing the grains of sand—as the ground for the sermon's claim that every human life is valued by God.

context: Notice what the Bible says back in Psalm 139. I want you to look at verses 17 and 18.
point_summary: God's thoughts toward each person outnumber the grains of sand, and waking to find him still present demonstrates that human life is valued by God to the point of his giving eternal life through Christ.

context: After the psalmist in verses 13 through 16 is rejoicing in understanding that we're made by God
point_summary: God's intimate knowledge of each individual—illustrated by knowing the precise number of hairs on every head among eight billion people—demonstrates that being made by God means also being fully known by him.

context: After the psalmist in verses 13 through 16 is rejoicing in understanding that we're made by God
point_summary: God's exhaustive knowledge of every individual — down to the number of hairs on each of eight billion heads — is presented as evidence that every person is not merely made by God but personally known by him.

context: After the psalmist in verses 13 through 16 is rejoicing in understanding that we're made by God
point_summary: God's intimate knowledge of each individual—illustrated by knowing the precise number of hairs on every head among eight billion people—demonstrates that being made by God means also being fully known by him.

context: After the psalmist in verses 13 through 16 is rejoicing in understanding that we're made by God
point_summary: God's exhaustive, individualized knowledge of every person—illustrated by numbering the hairs on eight billion heads—establishes that humanity is not merely made by God but intimately known by him.

context: how precious also are thy thoughts unto me, O God, how great is the sum of them
point_summary: God's thoughts toward us surpass the number of grains of sand, illustrating that we are profoundly valued by God who made us and knows us intimately.

context: If I should count them, they are more in number than the sand. When I awake, I am still with thee
point_summary: God's thoughts toward us surpass the number of grains of sand, illustrating that we are profoundly valued by God who made us and knows us intimately.

context: I am fearfully and wonderfully made.
point_summary: The familiar confession "I am fearfully and wonderfully made" is grounded in the truth that humanity is made *by* God, not of itself, as confirmed by the Genesis account of God forming Adam and breathing life into him.

context: I will praise thee for I am fearfully and wonderfully made.
point_summary: Human dignity and the value of life are grounded in being fearfully and wonderfully made by God, standing in direct contrast to evolutionary teaching that denies divine creation and personal worth.

context: "...I will love Thee, O Lord, my strength. The Lord is my rock, my fortress, and my deliverer, my God, my strength, and whom I will trust, my buckler, and the horn of my salvation, and my high tower."
point_summary: Knowing who God is—rock, fortress, deliverer, buckler—produces a response of love and confident prayer, as the psalmist's accumulated names for God lead directly to "I will call upon the Lord."

context: "...I will love Thee, O Lord, my strength. The Lord is my rock, my fortress, and my deliverer, my God, my strength, and whom I will trust, my buckler, and the horn of my salvation, and my high tower."
point_summary: Knowing God as rock, fortress, deliverer, buckler, and high tower—as David enumerates in Psalm 18:1–2—produces the practical result of calling upon Him in confidence and praise.

context: the psalmist said he daily loaded me with benefits
point_summary: Daily loaded with benefits yet surpassing all those gifts, God himself — as shield and exceeding great reward — is the believer's ultimate inheritance, the one with whom there is no appointment limit.

context: 'It was good for me that I was afflicted.'
point_summary: Affliction is framed as divinely purposeful, with Psalm 119:71 cited to show that suffering produces knowledge of God's statutes and fits his plan to conform believers to Christ's image.

context: I say often as the psalmist says in Psalm 119, it is time, Lord, for you to work.
point_summary: Patience in following God means trusting His timeline, as the psalmist's cry — "It is time, Lord, for you to work" — affirms that God is always working, even when unseen.

context: For God is good and his mercies endure forever.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Turn with me if you will please to Psalm 103. Psalm 103. Psalm 103. Another great passage of scripture.
point_summary: God's mercies being new every morning means He constantly renews His compassions toward undeserving sinners, so that His faithfulness—not human merit—is the sole reason we are not consumed.

context: And Psalm 90, as far as we know, is the only Psalm written by Moses.
point_summary: Psalm 90, attributed to Moses and centered on the theme of time, is invoked as a call to redeem time wisely, rejecting whatever wastes it or distracts from what matters most.

context: bless the Lord, O my soul, and all that is within me, bless His holy name
point_summary: Believers are called to bless God with their whole inner being and to actively remember His benefits, resisting the distraction of a world that crowds out gratitude and focus on the Lord.

context: Bless the Lord, O my soul, and forget not all His benefits
point_summary: Forgetting God's benefits is a failure of memory, not of provision—the call to "forget not" assumes his people are prone to overlook what he has already given.

context: Blessed Lord, oh my soul, and forget not. See that, what it says? Forget not, don't forget.
point_summary: Forgetting God's benefits—both material and spiritual—is the danger the verse warns against, with all possessions reframed as gifts from God's hand to be used for His honor and glory.

context: Verse three, who forgiveth all thine iniquities. I won't say that word all. What a wonderful word that is. He forgiveth all.
point_summary: God's forgiveness of *all* iniquities—with emphasis on the totality of that word—is presented as the foremost spiritual benefit surpassing even the material blessings for which believers must not forget to give thanks.

context: who redeemeth thy life from destruction, who crowneth thee with loving kindness and tender mercy,
point_summary: God's benefits encompass both material provision—which belongs entirely to Him—and spiritual blessings, and the psalmist's command to "forget not" warns against pride of ownership over either.

context: so satisfy thy mouth with good things, so that thy youth is renewed like the eagles, amen?
point_summary: God's benefits encompass both material provision—acknowledged as entirely His—and spiritual blessings, with the psalm's command to "forget not" serving as a warning against ingratitude toward their divine source.

context: The Lord executeth righteousness and judgment for all that are oppressed.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: He made known his ways unto Moses, his acts unto the children of Israel.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: The Lord is merciful and gracious, slow to anger, and plenteous in mercy.
point_summary: God's mercy toward his people is characterized as plenteous and slow to anger, meaning he withholds the punishment our sins deserve.

context: He will not always chide, neither will he keep his anger forever.
point_summary: God's restraint in anger and refusal to repay sin as it deserves (v. 9) grounds the assurance that believers can return to Him in confession and receive forgiveness.

context: He hath not dealt with us after our sins; nor rewarded us according to our iniquities.
point_summary: God's restraint in not dealing with us according to our sins is presented as cause for gratitude, underscoring that divine mercy withholds the punishment sinners deserve.

context: For as the heaven is high above the earth, so great is his mercy toward them that fear him.
point_summary: God's mercy toward those who fear Him is so vast it defies measurement, illustrated by the incomprehensible height of heaven above the earth.

context: As far as the east is from the west, so far hath he removed our transgressions from us.
point_summary: God's removal of transgressions "as far as the east is from the west" is cited as evidence that He is a complete forgiver of sin, prompting gratitude and ongoing confession.

context: Like as a father pitieth his children, so the Lord pitieth them that fear him.
point_summary: God's fatherly pity toward those who fear Him is set alongside His knowledge of human frailty—that we are but dust—as the ground for His compassionate forgiveness of sin.

context: For he knoweth our frame; he remembereth that we are dust.
point_summary: God's knowledge of human frailty — that He remembers we are dust — grounds His compassionate dealings with His people throughout the psalm's catalog of benefits.

context: David said, I acknowledge my sin, it's ever before me in Psalm 51.
point_summary: Psalm 51's declaration "I acknowledge my sin" is cited as the biblical pattern for the first step of repentance—honest admission of sin before God—mirroring the prodigal son's confession.

context: O how love I thy law!
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: the psalmist stands up and says, O how love I thy law!
point_summary: Psalm 119's declaration "O how love I thy law" must be supported by observable evidence, not mere claim, and the psalm itself furnishes that overwhelming proof.

context: O how love I thy law! It is my meditation all the day.
point_summary: Declared love for God's law must be supported by verifiable evidence, as the psalmist's exclamation "O how love I thy law" is treated not as self-evident but as a claim requiring proof.

context: Consider how I love thy precepts. Quicken me, O Lord, according to thy lovingkindness.
point_summary: The psalmist's appeal — "Consider how I love thy precepts" — is presented as a claim that must be supported by observable evidence, not merely declared.

context: Consider how I love thy precepts.
point_summary: The psalmist's call to "consider" his love for God's precepts is an invitation to inspect and examine his life as observable evidence supporting his declared devotion.

context: he's declared his love in verse 97
point_summary: Verse 97's declaration of love for God's law is set against verse 159's call to "consider"—meaning inspect or examine—as the standard that genuine love must be evidenced by observable life and behavior, not mere claim.

context: I will praise thee with uprightness of heart when I shall have learned thy righteous judgments.
point_summary: Genuine love for God's word produces a desire to learn it, evidenced by the psalmist's pledge to praise God "when I shall have learned thy righteous judgments."

context: Blessed art thou, O Lord. Teach me thy statutes.
point_summary: The psalmist's repeated prayer "teach me thy statutes" is presented as evidence that genuine love for God's Word produces a desire to learn it.

context: I have declared my ways and thou heardest me. Teach me thy statutes.
point_summary: Psalm 119:26's repeated petition "teach me thy statutes" is cited as one of several instances demonstrating that genuine love for God's Word produces a desire to learn it.

context: I will keep thy statutes. Oh, forsake me not utterly.
point_summary: Loving God's word produces not merely a desire to learn it but a commitment to obey it, as the psalmist's declaration "I will keep thy statutes" demonstrates.

context: teach me, O Lord, the way of thy statutes, and I shall keep it unto the end.
point_summary: Psalm 119:33 unites a desire to learn with a commitment to obey, as the psalmist's plea "teach me" is inseparable from his vow "I shall keep it unto the end."

context: Notice what he says in verse number 11, a verse that's very familiar to many of us.
point_summary: Hiding God's Word in the heart through memorization is presented as both evidence of love for Scripture and a purposeful plan to guard against sin.

context: Thy word have I hid in mine heart, that I might not sin against thee.
point_summary: Memorizing Scripture is presented as both an expression of love for God's Word and a purposeful plan to guard the heart against sin.

context: thy Word have I hid in mine heart, that I might not sin against thee
point_summary: Deliberate memorization of Scripture is the natural fruit of loving God's Word, and the stored Word becomes a ready defense against sin in moments of temptation.

context: he's able to say, it is written. That's a good read. Look, he says that I might not sin against thee.
point_summary: Hiding God's Word in the heart is presented as a purposeful, love-driven act of memorization that equips the believer to resist sin by recalling Scripture in moments of temptation.

context: Notice in verse 15, I will meditate in thy precepts and have respect unto thy ways.
point_summary: Meditation on God's Word is presented as an act of love, with Psalm 119:15 cited as the psalmist's deliberate plan to dwell on God's precepts and align his ways accordingly.

context: He says in verse 23, princes also did sit and speak against me, but thy servant did meditate in thy statutes.
point_summary: Even amid opposition from princes, the psalmist's response was to meditate on God's statutes, illustrating that love for the Word sustains devotion under pressure.

context: Of course, we're familiar with verse 97. We've used that last week as our main verse of getting into this portion of scripture.
point_summary: Loving God's Word and meditating on it throughout the day are inseparable, with Psalm 119:97 serving as the anchor verse for this section's call to a deliberate plan of meditation.

context: And the psalmist says this in Psalm 119 verse 148, mine eyes prevent the night watches that I might meditate in thy Word.
point_summary: Waking before the night watches to meditate signals that love for God's Word drives the psalmist to prioritize unhurried, intentional reflection on Scripture above the day's competing demands.

context: Notice with me in verse number 94, notice what the Bible says. He says, I am thine, save me for I have sought thy precepts.
point_summary: Genuine love for God's Word is evidenced by diligent, careful seeking of His precepts—not casual searching, but deliberate, attentive study.

context: The Bible says this, I will delight myself in thy statutes. Then he says this, I will not forget thy word.
point_summary: Delighting in God's Word and not forgetting it are inseparably linked—the psalmist's declaration that Scripture is his delight reflects a love proven by consistent, meditative engagement with it.

context: He says again in verse number 24, thy testimonies also are my delight and my counselors.
point_summary: The psalmist's declaration that God's testimonies are his delight and counselors illustrates that genuine love for Scripture is evidenced by the joy and satisfaction it produces in the believer's life.

context: He says in verse 47, notice, and I will delight myself in thy commandments, which I have loved, which I have loved.
point_summary: Delight in God's commandments is presented as evidence of genuine love for His Word, contrasting the fleeting satisfaction the world offers with the lasting joy Scripture provides.

context: And he says, nearing the end of this psalm, he says in verse number 174, I have longed for thy salvation, O Lord, and thy law is my delight.
point_summary: Longing for God's salvation and declaring His law "my delight" are paired as evidence that genuine love for Scripture produces joy, not mere duty.

context: The Bible says, blessed is the man that walketh not in the counsel of the ungodly, nor standeth in the way of sinners, nor sitteth in the seat of the scornful,
point_summary: Psalm 1:1–2 is cited to show that the blessed man's delight in God's law—expressed through continual meditation—evidences genuine love for Scripture, paralleling the psalmist's repeated declarations in Psalm 119.

context: but his delight is in the law of the Lord, and in his law doth he meditate day and night.
point_summary: Psalm 1:2 is cited to show that the blessed man's delight in God's law—expressed through continual meditation—gives evidence that he genuinely loves Scripture.

context: I am thy servant, give me understanding that I may know thy testimonies.
point_summary: The psalmist's cry for understanding in order to know God's testimonies is presented as evidence of a genuine longing for God's word, not merely intellectual knowledge.

context: Therefore, I love thy commandments above gold, yea, above fine gold.
point_summary: Loving God's commandments above gold and fine gold is the foundation from which the psalmist esteems all God's precepts as right and hates every false way.

context: Therefore, I esteem all thy precepts concerning all things to be right, and I hate every false way.
point_summary: Loving God's commandments above gold (v. 127) produces the response of v. 128: esteeming all God's precepts as right and hating every false way.

context: I love how he words this in verse number 128. He says, I esteem. all thy precepts concerning all things.
point_summary: Loving God's commandments above gold produces an all-inclusive esteem for every precept—not a buffet-style selection of preferred passages—extending even to the Old Testament.

context: princes have persecuted me without a cause, but my heart standeth in awe of thy Word
point_summary: Even when persecuted without cause by powerful adversaries, the psalmist's heart stood in awe of God's Word, modeling reverence for Scripture as the proper response regardless of external pressure.

context: I will worship toward thy holy temple and praise thy name for thy loving kindness and for thy truth, for thou hast magnified thy Word above all thy name.
point_summary: God's exaltation of His Word above even His own name grounds the call to stand in awe of Scripture, not merely of God's person.

context: let all the earth fear the Lord. Let all the inhabitants of the world stand in awe of Him.
point_summary: Reverence for God himself grounds and animates the awe that the believer is called to show toward His Word.

context: my tongue shall speak of thy Word for all thy commandments are righteousness
point_summary: Loving God's Word leads to a purposeful resolve to speak it, as the psalmist's declaration "my tongue shall speak of thy Word" flows naturally from meditation on and reverence for Scripture.

context: the mouth of the righteous speaketh wisdom, and his tongue talketh of judgment
point_summary: Purposing to speak God's Word flows naturally from inward devotion, as the righteous man's mouth speaks wisdom precisely because God's law dwells in his heart.

context: Why does he speak the way that he does? Well, the answer is found in verse 31
point_summary: God's law dwelling in the heart is the root cause of righteous speech—the mouth speaks wisdom and the tongue talks of judgment because the Word is already within.

context: The psalmist says, yea, more to be desired are they than gold, yea, than much fine gold.
point_summary: Scripture's preciousness—more desirable than gold—is invoked to frame the written Word as the Heavenly Father's own message to His children, surpassing any earthly inheritance.

context: The Bible begins with, the heavens declare the glory of God,
point_summary: Creation's testimony to God's glory in Psalm 19:1 serves as a gateway into the psalm's fuller movement from natural revelation to the perfecting, life-transforming power of God's written Word.

context: the firmament showeth forth His handiwork
point_summary: Psalm 19:1's declaration that creation reveals a Creator serves as a brief entry point before the sermon pivots to Scripture's written Word as the fuller, life-transforming source of divine instruction.

context: the law of the Lord is perfect, converting the soul
point_summary: Scripture's own testimony that the law of the Lord is perfect is developed here as a life-transforming, soul-converting, and protective word whose warnings preserve believers from hardship.

context: The statutes of the Lord are right, rejoicing the heart
point_summary: Psalm 19:8's declaration that the statutes of the Lord are right and rejoicing the heart anchors a broader reading of Psalm 19:7–11, presenting God's Word as life-transforming truth that warns, preserves, and protects its hearers.

context: The fear of the Lord is clean, enduring forever
point_summary: God's written Word, including its prohibitions, functions as a protective warning that preserves the believer from hardship, making its commands a gift rather than a burden.

context: More to be desired they than gold, yea, than much fine gold
point_summary: Scripture's value surpasses even the greatest human repositories of knowledge, and its warnings and prohibitions are protective gifts rather than burdensome restrictions.

context: Moreover, by them
point_summary: God's warnings in Scripture are a mercy, not a burden—the prohibitions of the Word actively preserve the believer from hardship.

context: We find that Psalm 102 and verse number 11 illustrates our lives as being a shadow
point_summary: Human life's brevity is underscored by pairing James's "vapor" image with Psalm 102:11's depiction of life as a fleeting shadow, both illustrating how little time we actually possess.

context: this is the day the Lord hath made. We will rejoice and be glad in it
point_summary: Each day of physical life is a gift from God to be received with deliberate rejoicing, as the psalmist's declaration "this is the day the Lord hath made" models a chosen response of gladness rather than living under circumstances.

context: The righteous God loveth righteousness, Psalm 11.
point_summary: Psalm 11's declaration that "the righteous God loveth righteousness" grounds the point that God sees and knows all things, making it impossible to hide sin from Him.

reference_text: search me as the psalmist said, and show me if there'd be any wicked way
context: Lord, search me as the psalmist said, and show me if there'd be any wicked way
point_summary: Self-examination before God—inviting him to "search me and show me if there be any wicked way"—is the antidote to the self-deception that causes sinners to judge others while hiding their own sin.

context: speaking about the law of the Lord being perfect and gives all of these attributes and various workings of God's word and how it's effective
point_summary: Psalm 19:7ff establishes that God's Word is perfect and effective to warn, yet its counsel—like all divine instruction—cannot compel obedience; warning and forcing are distinct acts.

context: 'Moreover, by them is thy servant warned, and in keeping of them, there is great reward.'
point_summary: God's Word serves as a warning to his servants, but as Psalm 19:11 illustrates, that warning can be heeded or ignored — it cannot be forced.

context: But in Psalm 119, in verse 126, the psalmist declared, it is time for thee, Lord, to work. For they have made void thy law.
point_summary: Psalm 119:126 — "It is time for thee, Lord, to work" — is used as a springboard to frame the desperate circumstances of 1 Samuel 14 as a moment requiring direct divine intervention.

context: help me, O Lord my God. O save me according to thy mercy.
point_summary: The psalmist's cry in Psalm 109:26–27 is invoked as a longing that when God acts, His intervention would be unmistakably clear — known to all as God's hand, not human scheming.

context: Notice that they may know that this is thy hand, that thou Lord has done it.
point_summary: The psalmist's cry in Psalm 109:27 — that God would work so unmistakably that all would know it was His hand alone — frames the sermon's longing for divine action that cannot be credited to human scheming.

context: It is time for thee Lord to work for they have made void thy law.
point_summary: Psalm 119:126's cry — "It is time for thee, Lord, to work" — is presented as the timeless plea of God's people in desperate circumstances, longing for divine action that is unmistakably God's doing.

context: as we looked at the Psalmist in Psalm 119, when he says it is time for thee to work Lord
point_summary: Psalm 119's declaration "it is time for thee to work, Lord" grounds Jonathan's expectation that God would act, illustrating that God's people throughout history have anticipated and experienced His direct intervention.

context: As the psalmist said in Psalm 109, that they may know that this is thy hand, that thou, Lord, hast done it.
point_summary: Psalm 109:27 is invoked to express the desire that God's mighty acts be unmistakably recognized as His hand alone, so that He—not human instruments—receives all glory.

context: I understand Psalm 119, 126 had not been written yet, but you understand that it's appropriate that he would have that same thought and that same idea.
point_summary: Psalm 119:126's declaration that it is time for God to work is applied retrospectively to Jonathan's bold initiative, showing that his faith-driven action embodied that same conviction centuries before the psalm was written.

context: It is time for thee to work, Lord. Would you agree that it's time for God to work? They have made void thy law. If they made void thy law in Psalm 119, 126, wow, have they made void God's law today.
point_summary: The lawlessness of Psalm 119:126 — "they have made void thy law" — is applied as a present-day parallel, signaling that the current moral collapse makes it urgent for God to act.

context: David, of course, in Psalm 19, the law of the Lord is perfect
point_summary: Psalm 19's declaration that "the law of the Lord is perfect" is cited as scriptural grounding for the preciousness and priority God places on His Word, which Ezra is said to have understood.

context: we studied Psalm 119 in our adult Sunday school class
point_summary: Psalm 119 is invoked as a witness to the preciousness and importance of God's Word, which Ezra's heart-preparation to seek the law likely reflected.

context: moreover verse 11 of Psalm 19 by them is thy servant warned and in keeping of them there is great reward
point_summary: Psalm 19:11's promise of great reward for keeping God's statutes grounds the call to be a doer of the Word, not merely a hearer, as exemplified by Ezra.

context: if you were to read Psalm 119 and you to read through all of those verses and study the entirety of that passage of Scripture
point_summary: Psalm 119's pervasive emphasis on keeping, obeying, doing, and walking in God's law is cited as scriptural grounding for Ezra's defining characteristic: being a doer of the Word, not a hearer only.

context: The Bible says in Psalm 116, in verse number 15, precious in the sight of the Lord is the death of his saints.
point_summary: The death of a saint like Joseph is not a tragedy but something God himself regards as precious, framing the close of Joseph's life within divine care rather than mere mortality.
