reference_text: restore unto me the joy of thy salvation
context: That's why David says, restore unto me the joy of thy salvation
point_summary: Sin forfeits the joy that no external persecution can take, which is why David's cry to have it restored stands as the believer's recourse when joy is stolen from within.

context: Bible there with you, Psalm 25, and last week we started a little mini-series, just a three-part series here going through Psalm 25
point_summary: Psalm 25, a psalm of David, is being read as the transparent cry of one who longed for peace amid adverse and difficult life circumstances.

context: As we continue through the Psalm, let me just do a quick reminder of what we looked at last week in the first few verses, David, it's the Psalm of David
point_summary: Psalm 25, a psalm of David, is introduced as a three-part series exploring how David, amid adverse circumstances, expressed a longing for peace before God.

context: David's approach here in Psalm 25 when he's longing for direction, when he's seeking the Lord
point_summary: David's approach in Psalm 25 is held up as exemplary, demonstrating that when we need direction, we can go to God knowing He is able to provide it.

context: show me thy ways, O Lord
point_summary: David's prayer in Psalm 25:4 is held up as exemplary because he seeks not merely any direction but specifically divine direction—asking God to show and teach him God's own ways and paths.

context: teach me thy paths
point_summary: David's prayer in Psalm 25:4 is held up as exemplary because he seeks not merely any direction but specifically divine direction—asking God to show and teach him God's own ways and paths.

context: lead me in thy truth, and teach me, for thou art the God of my salvation, on thee do I wait all the day
point_summary: Amid personal crisis and uncertainty, David's response is to ask God not merely for an answer but for ongoing divine leading, teaching, and truth — anchored in trust that God alone is his salvation.

context: yea, let none that wait on thee be ashamed

context: in verse number eight. Good and upright is the Lord

context: Thy word is a lamp unto my feet
point_summary: God's word functions as the specific, personal lamp that provides individualized direction for each believer's unique circumstances, making Bible reading inseparable from answered prayer for guidance.

context: verse number eight, that he was confident that God will teach. Therefore, will he teach sinners in the way

context: the Bible says, the meek will He guide in judgment, and the meek will He teach His way

context: the meek will He guide in judgment, the meek will He teach His way
point_summary: Meekness — defined here as humble submission to God's will — is the condition Scripture attaches to receiving divine guidance and instruction.

context: what man is he that feareth the Lord? Him shall He teach in the way that He shall choose
point_summary: Fearing the Lord is presented as a condition for receiving divine direction, with God promising to teach the God-fearing man the way he should choose.

context: His soul shall dwell at ease, and His seed shall inherit the earth
point_summary: Those who fear the Lord receive not only guidance in the way they should choose but also the inheritance of ease and the disclosure of God's covenant secrets.

context: The secret of the Lord is with them that fear Him
point_summary: God reserves intimate disclosure of His covenant for those who fear Him, with the fear of the Lord presented as a condition for receiving divine direction and instruction.

context: The Bible says that the secret of the Lord is with them that fear Him, and He will show them His covenant
point_summary: Divine secrets and covenant understanding are revealed specifically to those who fear the Lord, with meekness and humility identified as the prerequisite posture for receiving God's direction.

context: it says here in verse nine about the meek
point_summary: Divine guidance through meekness and the fear of the Lord are presented as twin conditions: God teaches and reveals His way to those who approach Him with humble submission.

context: verses 12 through 14, those that fear Him
point_summary: Fearing the Lord is presented as the condition God requires before He teaches a person the way He shall choose and reveals the secrets of His covenant.

context: Show me thy paths, right? Show me, teach me thy truth.

context: if you have a Bible with you, Psalm 90

context: For to soon cut off and we fly away
point_summary: Human life's brevity — capped at seventy or eighty years and swiftly cut off — frames the urgency of numbering our days wisely before eternity arrives.

context: For to soon cut off and we fly away
point_summary: Human life's brevity—capped at seventy or eighty years and swiftly cut off—frames the urgency of numbering our days wisely before eternity arrives.

context: now also when I am old and gray headed, oh God, forsake me not. And then notice what he says, until I have showed thy strength unto this generation and thy power to everyone that is to come
point_summary: The psalmist's aged prayer to show God's strength to the next generation frames intentional spiritual investment in others as a defining purpose of a successful life.

context: God sends bread from heaven angels food
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: sing aloud unto God our strength
point_summary: Singing to God is to be done aloud, as demonstrated by Paul and Silas whose midnight praises in prison were audibly heard by fellow prisoners.

context: studying through psalm 81 that this psalm is associated with the Feast of Tabernacles
point_summary: Psalm 81's association with the Feast of Tabernacles anchors its central theme: the appointed remembrance of Israel's deliverance from Egyptian bondage as a statute ordained by God.

context: look at verse 4, 5, and 6 with me notice what the Bible says for this was a statute for Israel in a law of the God of Jacob
point_summary: Psalm 81:4 is identified as a statute and law tied to the Feast of Tabernacles, whose central purpose was to remind Israel of their God-ordained deliverance from Egyptian bondage.

point_summary: Psalm 81:5's reference to God ordaining a testimony "in Joseph" when Israel went out through Egypt grounds the psalm's theme in the Feast of Tabernacles' purpose: reminding God's people of their deliverance from Egyptian bondage.

context: where I heard a language that I understood not I removed his shoulder from the burden his hands were delivered from the pots

context: sing aloud unto God our strength make a joyful noise unto the God of Jacob
point_summary: Joyful worship commanded in Psalm 81:1–3 is not arbitrary religious rule but a divinely prescribed statute—a directive given by a God who loves his people and knows what is good for them.

context: take a psalm and bring hither the timbrel the pleasant harp with the psaltery

context: blow up the trumpet in the new moon in the time appointed on our solemn feast day

context: for this was a statute for Israel
point_summary: The word "statute" in verse 4 identifies Israel's appointed worship — singing, instruments, and the trumpet blast — not as arbitrary rules but as a divinely prescribed task given for the people's good.

context: verse one three for this was a statute for Israel
point_summary: The commands to sing and celebrate in Psalm 81:1–3 are identified as a divinely prescribed statute—a permanent law given by God to Israel, rooted in the feast of tabernacles as established in Leviticus and Exodus.

context: when we started studying into Psalm 81
point_summary: The worship commands of Psalm 81:1–4 are identified as both a "statute" (a prescribed task) and a "law" (a permanent rule from the supreme power), establishing that Israel's appointed feast observances were divinely mandated, not humanly invented.

context: here in verse number four that the author says for this was a statute for Israel
point_summary: The word "statute" in verse 4 identifies Israel's prescribed worship practices as God-ordained directives, while "law" denotes a permanent rule issued by the supreme authority of God himself.

context: this he ordained in Joseph for a testimony
point_summary: God's appointment of the feast as a testimony in Joseph declares that divine worship directives originate with God alone, not human initiative, and carry purposeful witness.

context: this was a statute for Israel and a law of the God of Jacob

context: I removed his shoulder from the burden

context: God is the one that removed his shoulder from the burden, his hands were delivered from the pots

context: We see what's happening in Psalm 81 with the Feast of Tabernacles
point_summary: Just as the Feast of Tabernacles gave Israel a God-ordained directive to remember His deliverance, the Lord's Supper gives the church a parallel divine command to remember redemption through Christ.

context: as we looked in verse number six of Psalm 81, he makes it very plain that it was him, the Lord, that delivered. I removed his shoulder from the burden. His hands were delivered from the pots.
point_summary: The Feast of Tabernacles was divinely ordained as an annual memorial specifically to remind Israel that it was God himself who removed their burden and delivered them from Egypt.

context: if you study Psalm 101 and you look in Hebrews 7
point_summary: Psalm 110 and Hebrews 7 are pointed to as the texts that develop Melchizedek's identity further, establishing that Christ's priesthood follows his order rather than Aaron's.

context: in the Lord put I my trust

context: Look at Psalm 57, if you will,

point_summary: God being known as "the most high over all the earth" is here presented as the goal of prayer itself, not merely a title but a truth the psalmist wants all men to recognize.

point_summary: Asaph's prayer that "men may know" God's name is Jehovah, Most High over all the earth, becomes a call for believers who truly know God to bear a burden for making Him known to others.

context: Psalm 83
point_summary: The psalmist's prayer that "men may know" Jehovah is the Most High parallels Paul's declaration of the unknown God, grounding an evangelistic burden to correct society's misunderstanding of who God truly is.

context: verse 14 is being kind of our starting point for this whole study on the names of God

context: he that dwelleth in the secret place of the Most High

context: shall abide under the shadow of the Almighty. I will say of the Lord, he is my refuge and my fortress, my God in him will I trust

context: He that dwelleth in the secret place of the Most High shall abide under the shadow of the Almighty
point_summary: The wonder of Psalm 91:1 lies in the words "dwelleth" and "abide"—that El Elyon, the Most High and possessor of heaven and earth, would permit sinful, self-knowing people to dwell and remain in His presence.

context: he is a very present help in the time of trouble.

context: Lord, it's time for you to work. It's time, Lord, for you to work. For they've made void thy law

context: wilt thou not revive us again that thy people may rejoice in thee?

context: let the words of my mouth and meditations of my heart be acceptable in thy sight, O Lord

context: purge me with hyssop and I shall be clean. Wash me and I shall be whiter than snow

context: give me a clean heart, O God, and renew a right spirit within me

context: the Bible says be still and know that I am God

context: We read in the Psalms about the Psalmist saying I'm longing for thee

context: The heavens declare the glory of God

context: The firmament showeth forth His handiwork
point_summary: Creation's testimony to God in Psalm 19:1 is cited as evidence mankind suppresses to avoid accountability, not because the proof is lacking.

context: the Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want
point_summary: Psalm 23:1's declaration that "the Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want" grounds the sermon's claim that God is both aware of and able to provide for every need His sheep cannot meet themselves.

context: thy word is a lamp unto my feet and a light unto my path

context: Great peace have they which love thy law, and nothing shall offend them.

context: He daily loadeth me with benefits

context: a very present help at the time of trouble

context: We read Psalm 23, a Psalm that we're familiar with. When David says, The Lord is my shepherd.

context: he's a very present help in a time of trouble

context: I will lift up mine eyes into the hills, from whence cometh my help. My help cometh from the Lord

context: Which maketh heaven and earth

context: remember not the sins of my youth, nor my transgressions. According to thy mercy, remember thou me for thy goodness sake, O Lord

context: turn thee unto me and have mercy upon me

context: For I am desolate and afflicted
point_summary: Verse 16's cry for mercy — "turn thee unto me and have mercy upon me, for I am desolate and afflicted" — is read as a third instance confirming that God's merciful character is the repeated ground of David's longing for forgiveness.

context: for thy name's sake O Lord pardon mine iniquity for it is great

context: chapter 19 in the book of Psalms. Psalm 19

context: keep back thy servant also from presumptuous sins. Let them not have dominion over me. Then shall I be upright and I shall be innocent

context: Let the words of my mouth and the meditation of my heart be acceptable in thy sight O Lord my strength and my Redeemer

context: for thy namesake O Lord pardon mine iniquity for it is great

context: stand in awe and sin not
point_summary: A right view of God — standing in awe of Him — is directly connected to a right view of sin's greatness, so that reverence for God guards against minimizing iniquity.

context: Notice not only will God pardon but we see in verse number 18 David says look upon mine affliction in my pain and forgive all my sins

context: remember, O Lord, thy tender mercies and thy loving kindness, for they have been ever of old

context: remember not the sins of my youth, nor my transgressions. According to thy mercy, remember thou me for my goodness sake, O Lord

context: Remember not the sins of my youth

context: as far as the east is from the west

context: We learn three valuable lessons from Psalm 25 as we study David in the midst of this trial in his life

context: And I pray that you'd help us to be able to take and apply truth in our lives, that we would follow the example of David here in Psalm 25

context: then believed they his words. They sang his praise
point_summary: Belief in God's words—not circumstances or feelings—is the proper condition of his people, evidenced by the fact that believing led Israel to sing his praise.

context: then believed they his words. They sang his praise
point_summary: Genuine belief in God's words produces praise, but Psalm 106:12 stands as a fleeting high point, immediately contrasted with Israel's swift descent into forgetting and lust, illustrating that faith must be sustained, not merely momentary.

context: they soon forget his works. They waited not for his counsel, but lusted exceedingly in the wilderness and tempted God in the desert
point_summary: The swift collapse from believing God's words and singing His praise (v. 12) to forgetting His works and following their own lusts (v. 13) illustrates how quickly God's people can abandon trust in what He has said.

context: they forgot God their Savior, which had done great things in Egypt
point_summary: Israel's swift descent from believing God's words and singing his praise (v. 12) to forgetting God their Savior (v. 21) illustrates how quickly his people can abandon faith for self-directed lust.

context: In Psalm 106 and verse 24, the Bible says, yea, they despised the pleasant land. They believed not his word.
point_summary: Israel's unbelief in Psalm 106:24 is shown to be entirely without justification, since God had never failed, disappointed, or broken a single promise to his people.

context: but murmur in their tents in verse 25 and hearken not unto the voice of the Lord
point_summary: Murmuring in their tents and refusing to heed God's voice flowed directly from unbelief, demonstrating that belief governs behavior—if one doubts God's word, conduct will reflect that doubt.

context: we look here in Psalm 78 and Psalm 106
point_summary: Psalm 78 is cited alongside Psalm 106 as scriptural witness that Israel's wilderness generation was destroyed precisely because they refused to believe God's promise to give them the land.

context: we look here in Psalm 78 and Psalm 106
point_summary: Psalm 106 is cited alongside Psalm 78 as scriptural witness that Israel's wilderness generation was destroyed precisely because they refused to believe God's promise to give them the land.

context: I want you to go back to Psalm 78
point_summary: Psalm 78 is revisited to introduce the "tragic result" of unbelief, contrasting the truth God reveals with the devastating consequences that follow when his word is not trusted.

context: they limited the Holy One of Israel
point_summary: Israel's unbelief is contrasted with Jeremiah's obedience to show that limiting God—as the psalmist describes—means refusing to trust Him when circumstances make His command seem unreasonable.

context: They limited the Holy One of Israel, Psalm 78 and verse 41
point_summary: Unbelief actively limits what God does among His people, as Israel's limiting of "the Holy One of Israel" in Psalm 78:41 is seen fulfilled in Nazareth, where Jesus withheld mighty works not from inability but because of their unbelief.

context: Yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil. Why? For thou art with me. Thy rod and thy staff, they comfort me.
point_summary: Even in the valley of the shadow of death, the presence of God—"thou art with me"—is the source of fearlessness and comfort, illustrated by how a child's fear dissolves when a parent takes their hand.

context: The psalmist said in Psalm 121, I will lift up mine eyes into the hills, from whence cometh my help
point_summary: Divine help comes from the Lord alone — illustrated by Hezekiah spreading Sennacherib's threatening letters before God and receiving overnight deliverance of 185,000 enemies.

context: My help cometh from the Lord, which made heaven and earth
point_summary: Divine help comes from the Lord who made heaven and earth—a truth illustrated by Hezekiah's helplessness before Assyria, where God alone destroyed 185,000 enemies in a single night.

context: Psalm 48, great is the Lord
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Psalm 19, the law of the Lord is perfect
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: unto God our strength, make a joyful noise unto the God of Jacob
point_summary: Psalm 81:1's call to "sing aloud unto God our strength" grounds the sermon's opening claim that music is a God-given means by which humanity offers praise to the Lord.

context: Take a psalm and bring hither the timbrel and pleasant harp with the psaltery
point_summary: Psalm 81:2's call to bring the timbrel, harp, and psaltery grounds the sermon's opening claim that music is a God-given means by which humanity praises the Lord.

context: Blow up the trumpet in the new moon, the time appointed on our solemn feast day
point_summary: Psalm 81:3's call to blow the trumpet, take up the timbrel, harp, and psaltery on appointed feast days illustrates that music is God's ordained means by which his people offer praise.

context: As we look at these three verses, I've simply entitled the message for this evening as we get into Psalm 81, praise with song
point_summary: Psalm 81's opening call to sing, play instruments, and blow the trumpet establishes music as God's appointed means of praise, reflecting its identity as a gift from God to humanity across all cultures.

context: I waited patiently for the Lord, and he inclined unto me and heard my cry. He brought me up also out of a horrible pit, out of the miry clay, and set my feet upon a rock, and established my goings.
point_summary: Salvation brings a new song — the psalmist's deliverance from the pit and established footing serve as the backdrop for God placing praise in the mouth of the redeemed.

context: and he hath put a new song in my mouth. Even praise unto our God, many shall see it, and fear, and shall trust in the Lord.
point_summary: Salvation brings a new song—a transformed musical expression of praise to God—replacing whatever music one loved before conversion.

context: you notice in Psalm 81, that music is how this psalm begins
point_summary: Singing in Psalm 81 establishes that music's purpose is to praise God and minister to hearts, not to entertain—a distinction reinforced by the psalm's opening emphasis on joyful song.

context: sing aloud unto God. Sing aloud unto God our strength. And it continues, and make a joyful noise unto the God of Jacob
point_summary: Psalm 81:1's command to "sing aloud unto God" establishes that congregational singing is directed to God himself, not performed for entertainment, making it an act of worship rather than a filler activity.

context: Notice in verse number one, sing aloud. Sing aloud. It then says this, make a joyful noise unto the God of Jacob.
point_summary: Singing aloud and making a joyful noise in Psalm 81:1 carries the force of shouting out, directing that exuberant praise specifically to God as the primary audience, not the congregation.

context: Listen, God's given us a song, God's given us, we read Psalm 40, think about what he did for us and how he delivered us and how he established our goings and he's given us a reason to sing, he's put a song in my mouth, in my heart
point_summary: Psalm 40's account of God delivering and establishing the believer's steps grounds the call to sing aloud, because such rescue gives genuine reason for unrestrained, joyful praise.

context: sing aloud unto God our strength
point_summary: Singing aloud unto God is not optional timidity but an act of strength, since the phrase "joyful noise" throughout Scripture carries the literal force of a shout.

context: Make a joyful noise unto the God of Jacob
point_summary: Singing out boldly is commanded, not optional — "make a joyful noise" carrying the force of a shout directed to the Lord by all peoples.

context: Look over at Psalm 100 if you will
point_summary: Singing out boldly is warranted because the command to make a joyful noise—a word meaning to shout—is directed to all the earth, giving every believer ample reason to sing without restraint.

context: make a joyful noise unto the Lord all ye lands
point_summary: Psalm 100:1's command to make a joyful noise—understood throughout Scripture as a literal shout—grounds the call to sing boldly before God, regardless of musical ability.

context: Serve the Lord with gladness, come before his presence with singing
point_summary: Singing before God is an act of joyful service commanded by Scripture, and God desires to hear his people sing regardless of musical ability.

context: God inhabits the praises of his people
point_summary: Singing to God in corporate worship should be uninhibited and joyful, because God inhabits the praises of his people and desires his people to enter his presence with song.

context: know ye that the Lord, he is God. It is he that hath made us and not we ourselves
point_summary: Knowing that God made us and we belong to him as his people grounds the call to enter his presence with singing rather than self-conscious silence before others.

context: Enter into his gates with thanksgiving and into his courts with praise
point_summary: Entering God's presence through song is not a performance for others but an act of worship directed to God himself, who inhabits the praises of his people.

context: The Lord is good, he's good all the time. His mercy is everlasting, his truth does endure
point_summary: God's goodness, everlasting mercy, and enduring truth in Psalm 100:5 are presented as the very grounds that compel believers to keep singing his praise without shame or self-consciousness.

context: Psalm 81, not only does God teach us in verse number one, how we're to sing and to whom we're to sing
point_summary: Singing to God should be uninhibited and joyful, offered without self-consciousness about others' opinions, because Psalm 81 instructs both how and to whom we are to sing.

context: we find that God in verse two and three provides us with the use of instruments
point_summary: Psalm 81:2 is cited as scriptural warrant for the use of musical instruments in corporate worship, with the congregation's instrumentalists offered as a living illustration of that command.

context: Take a psalm and bring hither the timbrel, the pleasant harp with the psaltery. Blow up the trumpet in the new moon in the time appointed on our solemn feast day.
point_summary: Psalm 81:2's catalog of instruments—timbrel, harp, psaltery, and trumpet—grounds the sermon's affirmation that musical instruments have always been a legitimate and valued part of corporate worship.

context: praise you the Lord, praise God in his sanctuary, praise him in the firmament of his power, praise him for his mighty acts, praise him according to his excellent greatness
point_summary: Psalm 150:1's call to praise God in His sanctuary grounds the sermon's affirmation that musical instruments—trumpet, strings, piano—are fitting and God-ordained means of corporate worship.

context: praise him with the sound of the trumpet, praise him with the psaltery and harp, praise him with the timbrel and dance, praise him with stringed instruments and organs, praise him upon the loud cymbals, praise him upon the high sounding cymbals
point_summary: Psalm 150:3's catalog of instruments—trumpet, psaltery, harp, timbrel, and cymbals—is cited as scriptural evidence that God ordains the use of instruments in corporate praise.

context: Let everything that hath breath praise the Lord, praise ye the Lord
point_summary: Psalm 150's comprehensive catalog of instruments culminates in "let everything that hath breath praise the Lord," grounding the sermon's argument that God desires his people to sing praise to him.

context: God, I mentioned already, inhabits the praises of his people
point_summary: God inhabits the praises of his people, grounding the call to sing with instruments and voice in the truth that praise is the dwelling place of God.

context: When we introduce Psalm 81 and we see the way that this Psalm begins and the aspect of it being associated with the Feast of Tabernacles
point_summary: Singing aloud to God with joyful noise, as commanded in Psalm 81:1, reflects God's ongoing desire for his people to offer vocal praise to him.

context: to sing aloud unto God our strength, to make a joyful noise unto the God of Jacob
point_summary: Psalm 81:1's call to "sing aloud unto God our strength" establishes that God desires his people to praise him with audible, joyful singing, illustrated by Paul and Silas whose loud midnight praises were heard even from the inner prison.

context: sing aloud unto our God, who is our strength
point_summary: Singing aloud to God is an act of worship that ministers to the believer and bears witness to others, as illustrated by Paul and Silas singing in prison.

context: make a joyful noise
point_summary: Singing aloud to God is presented as an act of worship directed toward Him, illustrated by Paul and Silas whose praise in hardship both ministered to them and impacted those around them.

context: Psalm 23 this evening as we continue with this series
point_summary: Psalm 23:1's designation of God as "Jehovah Ra" — the Lord my Shepherd — is explored through the biblical pattern of God using relational analogies (shepherd/sheep, potter/clay, Father/children) to help his people understand his care.

context: as we look at Psalm 23, notice the confidence that David has
point_summary: David's confidence in Psalm 23:4 — declaring "thou art with me" rather than asking "will you be with me?" — reveals that the shepherd's presence is the sole ground of fearlessness in the valley.

context: yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death
point_summary: David's confidence in Psalm 23:4 rests not on a request for God's presence but on an assured declaration — "thou art with me" — which is the very ground of fearlessness in the valley.

context: I will fear no evil, why? For thou art with me
point_summary: David's declaration "thou art with me" in the valley is framed not as a petition but as a confident assertion, showing that the believer's security in dark times rests on certainty of the Shepherd's presence, not hope for it.

context: thou art with me, thou art with me
point_summary: David's confident declaration "thou art with me" in the valley reflects a shepherd's firsthand knowledge of what his flock suffers in his absence, grounding the believer's assurance in God's real, omnipresent companionship.

reference_text: yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil, for thou art with me
context: yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil, why? For thou art with me
point_summary: Even in the valley's darkest moments, the confidence expressed in "thou art with me" is grounded in God's omnipresence—a truth Hebrews 13:5–6 confirms with the promise that he will never leave nor forsake his people.

context: the Lord my shepherd and yes, he provides for me, yes he protects me
point_summary: God's desire, illustrated by linking Psalm 23's "thou art with me" to Hebrews 13:5–6, is that his people possess the same confident assurance of his never-departing presence that David himself had.

context: Yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death I will fear no evil for thou art with me. Thy rod, thy staff, they comfort me.
point_summary: Peace is not found in the absence of trouble but in the confident assurance of God's presence, as David's declaration in the valley demonstrates—not a request, but a certainty.

context: Thou art with me
point_summary: The phrase "thou art with me" in verse 4 grounds the believer's boldness and fearlessness not in personal confidence but in the promised, never-failing presence of the shepherd-God himself.

context: my shepherd's with me
point_summary: The abiding presence of the Shepherd—"Thou art with me"—is presented as a transforming daily reality whose impact on David, Joshua, Jeremiah, and the disciples the believer is called to personally experience.

context: the psalmist said this, the Lord is my shepherd
point_summary: The personal pronoun in "The LORD is my shepherd" connects directly to verse 6's promise of dwelling in God's house forever, making individual, saving faith the necessary condition for that eternal inheritance.

context: I will dwell in the house of the Lord forever
point_summary: Dwelling in the Lord's house forever (v. 6) is inseparable from calling him "my shepherd" (v. 1), making personal faith the necessary link between the psalm's first and final verse.

context: in the Lord put I my trust
point_summary: Psalm 11:1's opening declaration of trust in the Lord is cited as an example of the psalmist anchoring confidence in Jehovah, illustrating that knowing God's name—including His identity as the God who sees—produces trust.

context: Psalm 9 in verse 10 says, and they that know thy name will put their trust in thee
point_summary: Knowing God's name—here, the God who sees—is the ground of trust, as Psalm 9:10 links personal knowledge of who God is directly to the confidence placed in Him.

context: in verse number four, the Bible says the Lord is in His holy temple, the Lord's throne is in heaven. Notice this, His eyes behold, His eyelids try the children of men
point_summary: God's omniscience is grounded in His heavenly throne: His eyes behold and His eyelids try the children of men, confirming He is the God who sees everything.

context: in Psalm 34 and notice in verse number 15, this specific psalm, a psalm ascribed to David
point_summary: God's omniscient gaze, established throughout the sermon under the name El Roi, is here shown to be not merely surveillance but attentive care—His eyes upon the righteous and His ears open to their cry.

context: I'll bless the Lord at all times, His praise shall continually be in my mouth
point_summary: Continuous praise — "I will bless the Lord at all times" — is grounded in the confidence that God sees and cares for the righteous, making doxology the fitting, constant response to knowing El Roi.

context: Psalm 81, Psalm 81. And if you have your Bible with you, turn there and we've been studying through this Psalm now for a number of weeks
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: verses 8 through 10, Psalm 81 verses 8 through 10 last Wednesday evening
point_summary: God's word to Israel in Psalm 81:8 is framed as fourfold divine action: instruction, warning, reminder of past redemption, and promised satisfaction for those who heed him.

context: open thy mouth wide and I will fill it
point_summary: God's promise to "fill" the open mouth (v. 10) is presented as the Lord's satisfying provision, summarizing one of four observations drawn from the prior week's study of verses 8–10.

context: with honey out of the rock, should I have satisfied thee
point_summary: God's satisfying provision, symbolized by honey out of the rock, is presented as the fulfillment he offered his people had they hearkened to him.

context: in his presence is fullness of joy
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: hear all my people and I will testify unto thee, O Israel
point_summary: God's testimony in Psalm 81:8 is defined as a solemn declaration—verbal or written—intended to communicate to others knowledge they did not previously possess.

context: So I gave them up unto their own heart's lust, and they walked in their own counsels
point_summary: God's judicial response to Israel's persistent refusal to hearken was to give them up to their own heart's lusts, surrendering them to walk in their own counsels.

context: hear, O my people, and I will testify unto thee, O Israel, if thou wilt hearken unto me
point_summary: God's earnest plea in Psalm 81:8 — "hear, O my people" — stands in tragic contrast to verse 11's indictment that his people "would not hearken," making the divine invitation the measure of their failure.

context: he continues in verse number 11 and notice the phrase at the end of verse number 11, in Israel would none of me
point_summary: Israel's refusal to hearken to God's voice is framed not as ignorance but as deliberate rejection—wanting His deliverance while wanting nothing to do with Him as Lord.

context: and they walked in their own counsels
point_summary: God's judicial abandonment — "giving them up" to their own hearts' lust and counsels — is the solemn consequence of Israel's persistent rejection of his repeated calls to repentance and obedience.

context: God gives them up to their own hearts lust, and they walked in their own counsels
point_summary: God's judicial abandonment of Israel — surrendering them to their own hearts' lust — meant they rejected His all-wise counsel and chose self-directed paths, a freedom that carries tragic consequences.

context: he gave them up to their own heart's lust and they walked in their own counsels
point_summary: God's judicial act of abandoning people to their own heart's lust and counsels leads, as Proverbs 16:25 confirms, to a path that seems right but ends in death.

context: they walked in their own counsels
point_summary: God's judgment of abandoning Israel to their own counsels (Ps. 81:12) is contrasted with Proverbs 19:21 to show that rejecting God's counsel in favor of one's own is willful disobedience, not ignorance.

context: They hearken not
point_summary: Israel's failure in Psalm 81 was not ignorance of God's counsel but willful rejection of it, choosing their own way despite having full access to what God said.

context: he says in verse eight that hear all my people and I will testify unto thee, O Israel, if thou wilt hearken unto me
point_summary: God's earnest call to hear and hearken in Psalm 81:8 frames the entire sermon's indictment: Israel's refusal to listen led directly to God giving them over to their own hearts' lusts and self-directed counsel.

context: He told him in verse number 10 as we studied last week, open thy mouth wide and I will fill it
point_summary: God's boundless provision ("open thy mouth wide and I will fill it") was forfeited when Israel refused to hearken, resulting in divine abandonment to their own lusts and self-directed counsel.

context: but my people would not hearken to my voice and Israel would none of me. So I gave them up unto their own hearts lust and they walked in their own councils
point_summary: Israel's refusal to hearken to God's voice resulted in His giving them over to their own hearts' lusts, a self-destructive pattern the sermon likens to the cyclical apostasy recorded in Judges.

context: my people, they would not hearken to my voice
point_summary: Israel's failure to hearken to God's voice in Psalm 81:11 is held as a warning against God's own people—saved, beloved, and benefited by His Word—neglecting to obey it.

context: Let it not be said of any one of us as it was of those in Psalm 81, they hearken not to my voice
point_summary: Israel's failure to hearken stands as a warning directed not at outsiders but at God's own covenant people, who had every reason to obey yet would not hear His voice.

context: The entrance of thy word giveth light, Psalm 119 and verse 130
point_summary: Scripture's inherent illuminating power—"the entrance of thy word giveth light"—is offered as the reason distributed printed Scripture is read eagerly even by those who have never heard the gospel.

context: Psalm 19 says the heavens declare the glory of God
point_summary: Creation's witness to God's existence (Ps. 19:1) is real but incomplete, requiring Scripture—introduced at verse 7—to reveal saving knowledge of Christ and the fullness of who God is.

context: when you get to verse 7, the law of the Lord is perfect
point_summary: While creation witnesses to a Creator, Psalm 19:7's declaration that "the law of the Lord is perfect" establishes that God has also given his Word as a fuller, distinct revelation of himself.

context: Oh, how love I thy law
point_summary: Love for Scripture's content—"Oh, how love I thy law"—drives the assembly and distribution of God's Word, because the Bible that transformed one life can transform others.

context: the entrance of thy words giveth light
point_summary: Distributing Scripture to those without it is grounded in the conviction that God's Word brings light, making its global spread an urgent and worshipful responsibility.

context: If you have your Bible with you this evening, Psalm 23
point_summary: Psalm 23:4 is the focal text for a multi-week series on Jehovah Ra, establishing that the shepherd who provides also protects his sheep.

context: Psalm 23, in verse number one, the Bible says, the Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want
point_summary: The name Jehovah Ra—the Lord my shepherd—is presented as encompassing both provision and protection, with "the Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want" serving as the foundation for a multi-week study of what that title communicates.

context: He maketh me to lie down in green pastures, he leadeth me beside the still waters
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: he restoreth my soul, he leadeth me in the paths of righteousness for his namesake
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: as we look at verse number four
point_summary: Even in the valley of the shadow of death, the sheep need not fear evil because the shepherd's rod and staff signal both his protective power and his abiding presence.

context: yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil, for thou art with me, thy rod and thy staff, they comfort me
point_summary: Verse four's declaration of fearlessness in the valley of death's shadow is grounded in the shepherd's protective presence, drawn from David's own experience as a shepherd responsible for guarding his flock.

context: I will lift up mine eyes unto the hills from whence cometh my help
point_summary: Psalm 121:1's declaration that help comes from the Lord grounds the sermon's argument that the same God who protected David's sheep is the ever-watchful Shepherd who guards believers against real spiritual danger.

context: My help cometh from the Lord, which made heaven and earth
point_summary: God's help coming from the Creator of heaven and earth (v. 2) grounds the assurance of verse 3—that the same Lord who made all things is the sleepless Keeper who will not let the believer's foot be moved.

context: he, who's he? Well, that's the Lord, that's my helper. It says he, the one who created heaven and earth, he will not suffer thy foot to be moved
point_summary: God's ceaseless vigilance over His people is the point drawn here: because the Lord who made heaven and earth never slumbers, His sheep are never left unguarded, even in their most vulnerable moments.

context: he that keepeth thee will not slumber
point_summary: God's ceaseless vigilance over His people is underscored by the double declaration that He neither slumbers nor sleeps, meaning there is never a moment when the Shepherd is unaware of the dangers His sheep face.

context: behold, he that keepeth Israel shall neither slumber nor sleep
point_summary: God's ceaseless vigilance over His people is the point: because He neither slumbers nor sleeps, there is no moment of vulnerability when the Shepherd's protective awareness lapses.

context: Look at Psalm 28
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: blessed be the Lord, because he has heard the voice of my supplications
point_summary: David's declaration that God heard his supplications grounds the assurance that the Lord is his strength and shield—a protector who both listens and defends against the enemy's assault.

context: The Lord is my strength and my shield
point_summary: David's declaration that "the Lord is my strength and my shield" is expounded as God himself functioning as the believer's protector—a shield that guards against the enemy's assault—producing trust, help, and rejoicing.

context: Jehovah-Rah, the Lord is my shepherd
point_summary: Jehovah-Raah — the Lord as shepherd — is the theological anchor from which David's language of shield, rock, and fortress all derive their meaning as expressions of divine protection.

context: in thee, O Lord, do I put my trust. Let me never be ashamed. Deliver me in thy righteousness
point_summary: Psalm 31:1–3 is read as David's declaration that God is his rock and fortress, underscoring the security and safety the Lord provides as the believer's shield and defender.

context: for thou art my rock and my fortress
point_summary: God's dual designation as "rock" and "fortress" in Psalm 31:3 communicates the security and safety the believer has under divine protection, reinforcing the shepherd's role as protector.

context: We find in Psalm 4.8, I know it's some chapters back, but look at Psalm 4.8
point_summary: God's role as protector extends even into sleep, as David's confidence that the Lord alone makes him dwell in safety enables him to lie down in peace.

context: I will both lay me down in peace and sleep, for thou, Lord, only makest me dwell in safety
point_summary: Psalm 4:8's declaration that God alone makes his people dwell in safety is presented as the ultimate security that surpasses every human protective measure, from ancient fortresses to modern security systems.

context: there's so many more that we could look at throughout the book of Psalms
point_summary: True safety belongs to God alone, not to human preparations — a principle illustrated through David's life, where divine protection repeatedly overruled Saul's schemes to destroy him.

context: Jehovah-Rah, the Lord is my shepherd
point_summary: Divine protection is grounded in the covenant name Jehovah-Raah — the Lord my Shepherd — which reveals not only God's provision but his active guardianship over his people, illustrated through David's life.

context: Yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil, for thou art with me, thy rod and thy staff, they comfort me
point_summary: The shepherd's rod and staff, instruments both for guiding wandering sheep and fighting off predators, are presented as the tangible means by which God's protective presence brings comfort even through life's darkest valleys.

context: yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death
point_summary: The shepherd's rod and staff, instruments of both correction and defense, are presented as the basis for fearlessness in life's most dangerous passages, because the shepherd actively fights off predators threatening the sheep.

context: yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death
point_summary: The valley of the shadow of death is identified as a literal, historically traveled passage near Jericho where robbers lay in wait, grounding the shepherd's protective presence in concrete geographical and physical danger rather than only in dying.

context: Jehovah Ra, the Lord's my shepherd
point_summary: The valley of the shadow of death is identified as a literal geographical passage near Jericho where ancient travelers faced real danger, grounding the shepherd's protective presence in historical and physical reality.

context: this poor man cried and the Lord heard him and saved him out of all his troubles
point_summary: God's protection of the humble is grounded in the reality that when the poor man cries, the Lord hears and delivers him from all his troubles.

context: verse 7
point_summary: God's angelic protection is not merely poetic imagery but a present reality, illustrated here by missionary accounts of visible angelic guardians that turned back those who came to kill them.

reference_text: angel of the Lord encampeth round about them that fear him and delivereth them
context: angel of the Lord encampeth round about them that fear him and delivereth them
point_summary: The angel of the Lord encamping around those who fear Him is presented as God's active, surrounding protection—experienced and expected by the people of God through their shepherd, Jehovah-Ra.

context: the eyes of the Lord are upon the righteous and his ears are open unto their cry
point_summary: God's constant awareness of the righteous — seeing their circumstances and hearing their cries — is presented as the basis for confident expectation of His protection, even in dangers they cannot perceive themselves.

context: the righteous cry and the Lord heareth and delivereth them out of all their troubles
point_summary: God's people can cry out with confident expectation of deliverance, because the Lord both hears the righteous and delivers them out of all their troubles.

context: The Lord is my shepherd
point_summary: The divine name Jehovah-Ra, drawn from "The LORD is my shepherd," is presented as the theological ground for God's active, unfailing protection of His people against a real enemy.

context: You make us to dwell in safety
point_summary: God's role as Jehovah-Ra, the Good Shepherd who does not flee when danger comes, guarantees a protection his people often need without even knowing it.

context: You neither slumber nor sleep
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: how say ye to my soul, flee as a bird to your mountain
point_summary: Against the counsel of those urging flight, the Psalmist's declaration "in the Lord put I my trust" stands as a deliberate choice to resist discouragement and remain anchored in God.

context: David says in the Lord, put I my trust
point_summary: Against the counsel of those urging flight, the Psalmist's declaration "in the Lord put I my trust" stands as a deliberate choice to resist discouragement and remain anchored in God.

context: for lo, the wicked bend their bow, they make ready their arrow upon the string that they may privilege shoot at the upright in heart
point_summary: Psalm 11:2 introduces two categories of people — the wicked and the upright in heart — revealing the specific circumstances of opposition David faced while continuing to trust the Lord.

context: the heathen rage and the people imagine a vain thing

context: according to Psalm 91 and Psalm 9, we will trust him

context: according to Psalm 91 and Psalm 9, we will trust him

context: thou callest in trouble and I delivered thee I answered thee in the secret place of thunder I proved thee at the waters of Meribah

context: I'm preaching from Psalm 81 in verse number 7 thou callest in trouble and I delivered thing

context: thou callest in trouble and I delivered thee I answered thee in the secret place of thunder

context: God says thou callest in trouble and I delivered thing I answered the in the secret place of thunder

context: thou callest in trouble and I delivered the

context: I proved thee at the waters of Meribah

context: O taste and see that the Lord is good. Blessed is the man that trusteth in him

context: Psalm 50, notice what the Bible says in Psalm 50 in verse number 15

context: I know all the fowls of the mountains and the wild beasts of the field are mine. If I were hungry, I would not tell thee, for the world is mine in the fullness thereof

context: in the day of trouble and call upon me in the day of trouble, I will deliver thee, and thou shalt glorify me

context: the first part of Psalm 81 in verse number 7, thou callest in trouble, and I delivered thee, I answered thee in the secret place of thunder

context: I thank you for the example here in Psalm 81

context: as you study through Psalm 25

context: unto thee, O Lord, do I lift up my soul

context: unto thee, O Lord, do I lift up my soul

context: We'll see it again in verse number 19

context: I lift up my soul
point_summary: Lifting the soul to God in distress — not withdrawing from Him — is the posture David models in Psalm 25:1, connecting the opening cry to the soul's promised ease in verse 13.

context: mine eyes are ever toward the Lord

context: with my whole heart have I sought thee

context: The psalmist says in Psalm 63 in verse number one, notice what the Bible says, O God, thou art my God. Notice, early will I seek thee. My soul thirsteth for thee, my flesh longeth for thee in this dry and thirsty land where no water is.
point_summary: Wholehearted seeking of God — expressed in Psalm 63:1 as soul-thirst and early pursuit — is held up as the standard against which believers must honestly measure their own devotion.

context: with my whole heart have I sought thee
point_summary: Wholehearted seeking of God is not merely commanded but is the posture the psalmist personally claims, held up here as the standard against which half-hearted, motion-going devotion is measured.

context: He says in verse 162 of the psalm, I rejoice that thy word is one that findeth great spoil.
point_summary: Seeking God's word with one's whole heart yields a joy like finding great treasure — a reward that surpasses anything discoverable on earth.

reference_text: in His presence is fullness of joy
context: the Bible says in His presence is fullness of joy

reference_text: with my whole heart have I sought
context: the psalmist says, with my whole heart have I sought

reference_text: Thy word have I hid in mine heart
context: In verse number 11, the Bible says this. Thy word have I hid in mine heart
point_summary: Hiding God's word in the heart requires genuine internalization, not mere recitation—a distinction the sermon presses as essential to truly treasuring Scripture.

context: thy word have I hid in mine heart
point_summary: Hiding God's Word in the heart, as the psalmist describes, demands genuine memorization—committed, recallable, and hard-won—not mere rapid recitation prompted by prizes or glances at a page.

context: take heed according to thy word
point_summary: Hiding God's Word in the heart, as the psalmist describes, demands genuine commitment, dedication, and work—not mere rapid recitation, but memorization deep enough to recall in moments of temptation.

context: thy word have I hid in mine heart
point_summary: Hiding God's Word in the heart through memorization is presented as essential preparation for the possibility that physical Bibles could be taken away, making internalized Scripture the believer's irreplaceable possession.

context: with my lips have I declared all the judgments of thy mouth
point_summary: Declaring God's judgments with the lips (v. 13) is presented as the natural overflow of a progression: wholehearted seeking (v. 10) and Scripture memorization (v. 11) inevitably shape what comes out of a person's mouth.

context: with my whole heart have I sought thee
point_summary: Wholehearted seeking of God (v. 10) is presented as the root of a progression that moves through hiding the Word in the heart to openly declaring God's judgments with the lips.

context: thy word have I hid in my heart
point_summary: Hiding God's word in the heart, as a deliberate act of memorization, is presented as the middle step in a progression from wholehearted seeking to openly declaring God's judgments.

context: I have rejoiced in the way of thy testimonies as much as in all riches
point_summary: Rejoicing in God's testimonies as much as in all riches is presented as the natural progression from whole-hearted seeking, memorizing, and declaring Scripture — joy being the fruit of sustained commitment to God's Word.

context: Wherewithal shall a young man cleanse his way? By taking heed thereto according to thy word
point_summary: Psalm 119:9's question — "Wherewithal shall a young man cleanse his way?" — is treated as the governing prompt that drives the psalmist's subsequent actions and resolutions toward a life pleasing to God.

context: I will delight myself in thy statutes
point_summary: Delighting in God's statutes (v. 16) is presented as the second of three declarative "I will" resolutions the psalmist makes in response to the question of how a young man cleanses his way.

context: I will meditate in thy precepts and have respect unto thy ways
point_summary: Meditation on God's precepts, as the psalmist resolves in verse 15, means filling the mind with Scripture—the opposite of the Eastern practice of emptying it.

context: I will meditate in thy precepts
point_summary: Biblical meditation, unlike Eastern emptying of the mind, means continually musing upon God's word until its truth permeates one's thinking—a deliberate, ongoing resolution of the will.

context: I will meditate in my precepts
point_summary: Meditating on God's precepts requires deliberate, sustained time—a resolved commitment, not a casual habit—so that Scripture moves from the page into the mind and soul.

context: I will delight myself in thy statutes
point_summary: Delighting in God's statutes is presented as a deliberate, resolved commitment—a chosen source of joy that requires meditative time and a continually fresh approach to Scripture.

context: I will delight myself in thy statutes
point_summary: Delighting in God's statutes and resolving not to forget His Word are active, intentional commitments against the spiritual staleness that familiarity with Scripture can quietly produce.

context: I will not forget thy word
point_summary: Delighting in God's statutes and resolving not to forget His word require active, intentional effort, because familiarity with Scripture can breed staleness rather than sustained joy.

context: I will not forget thy word
point_summary: Delighting in God's statutes and meditating on His Word so thoroughly that Scripture becomes part of a person is presented as the only reliable guard against forgetting it.

context: I will not forget thy word
point_summary: Saturating the heart and mind with Scripture so thoroughly that it becomes part of a person is the psalmist's resolved delight—evidenced even in those whose memory has otherwise failed.

context: let me not wander from thy commandments
point_summary: Saturating the heart with Scripture through wholehearted seeking produces such deep retention that the Word remains even when memory fails, fulfilling the psalmist's resolve never to forget.

context: before I was afflicted, I went astray, but now have I kept thy word
point_summary: Affliction, the psalmist testifies, functioned as a corrective that turned wandering into obedience, illustrating why the plea "let me not wander" carries such personal urgency.

context: before I was afflicted, I went astray, but now, so after the affliction, have I kept thy word

context: that we would hide your word in our hearts
point_summary: Hiding God's word in the heart is presented as a personal discipline of faithfulness, set alongside gospel witness as a practical measure of whether one is truly living for God.

context: The Psalmist said, it is time Lord for thee to work, for they have made void thy law
point_summary: Citing Psalm 119:126 ("It is time for thee to work"), the sermon frames the verse as a congregational longing for God to act decisively, grounded in the recognition that human law-breaking creates the very conditions that call God to intervene.

context: The Psalmist tells us that he's a very present help in the time of trouble
point_summary: God's being a "very present help in the time of trouble" is invoked to affirm that longing for God to act is met by his demonstrated ability and readiness to intervene.

context: God is always available and God has all power and he is a very present help in the time of trouble
point_summary: God's constant availability and omnipotence are affirmed through Psalm 46:1's declaration that he is "a very present help in the time of trouble," contrasting him with unreliable human helpers or failing machinery.

context: The Bible says in Psalm 78 that they limited the Holy One of Israel
point_summary: Human doubt and unbelief actively limits God's working, as Psalm 78 declares that Israel "limited the Holy One of Israel."

context: I want you to turn to the book of Psalms, Psalm 81

context: verse number 12, I gave them up unto their own heart's lust and they walked in their own counsels

context: Oh, that my people had hearkened unto me and Israel had walked in my ways

context: I should soon have subdued their enemies and turned my hand against their adversaries
point_summary: Doubt and unbelief cost Israel the sight of God's power: He would have subdued their enemies, but their failure to hearken meant they forfeited what He was fully prepared to do.

context: this portion of scripture in Psalm 81
point_summary: Doubt and unbelief cost Israel the sight of what God would have done — Psalm 81 frames their failure to hearken as a direct consequence of not believing God.

context: He knows our frame. He knows that we're but dust.
point_summary: God's intimate knowledge of human frailty — that He knows our frame and that we are dust — is the basis for trusting Him to provide daily needs without doubt or unbelief.

context: it is time for thee, Lord, to work, for they have made void thy law
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: it was good for me to be afflicted because then I learned thy statutes

context: if thou wilt hearken unto me in verse number eight

context: hear, O my people, and I will testify unto thee, O Israel, if thou wilt hearken unto me

context: there shall no strange God be in thee, neither shalt thou worship any strange God
point_summary: God's prohibition of strange gods in Psalm 81:9 is framed as the direct content of what Israel was called to hearken to — the blessing contingent on obedience.

context: I am the Lord thy God, which brought thee out of the land of Egypt, open thy mouth wide, and I will fill it

context: the Lord gave the word, and it continues and says, great was the company of those that published it

context: Hold your spot here in the book of Psalms

context: God in Psalm 81, obviously is speaking to his people
point_summary: God's declaration "I will testify unto thee" in Psalm 81 is understood as divine communication of beneficial, previously unknown truth—an advantage God gives to his people through his Word.

context: as we noted in Psalm 81 in verse number eight, he's providing, communicating to them
point_summary: God's declaration in Psalm 81:8 — "I will testify unto thee" — is understood as divine communication of previously unknown, beneficial truth, paralleled in Romans 3:2 with Israel being entrusted with the oracles of God.

context: there shall no strange God be in thee, neither shalt thou worship any strange God

context: Open thy mouth wide, and I will fill it

context: in Psalm 107, notice what the Bible says just a few pages over in Psalm 107

context: for he satisfieth the longing soul, and filleth the hungry soul with goodness

reference_text: open your mouth wide and I will fill it

point_summary: God's invitation to open wide one's mouth carries the promise that He alone—not false gods or worldly substitutes—will fully satisfy every longing of His people.

context: open thy mouth wide, and I will fill it

context: thou has put gladness in my heart

context: thou has put gladness in my heart

context: thou hast put gladness in my heart

context: thou has put gladness in my heart

reference_text: thou hast put gladness in my heart

reference_text: thou hast put gladness in my heart

context: Thou has put gladness in my heart

context: thou hast put gladness in my heart
point_summary: Gladness is God's gift alone—not found in people, possessions, or position—and David's confession that God "put" it in the heart means joy can only be forfeited, never taken.

context: this statement of the psalmist in Psalm four

context: thou hast put gladness in my heart

context: thou hast put gladness in my heart

context: with thee, with the Lord, there is forgiveness

context: the Psalmist here is making this request

context: 'Lord, it's time for thee to work.'

context: Oh Lord, thy word is settled in heaven

context: they've made void thy law, but we notice that he made the request, it's time, Lord, for thee, Lord, to work

context: As I look at the psalm in this statement of the psalmist in Psalm 119, 126

context: Help me, O Lord my God, O save me according to thy mercy

context: Help me, O Lord my God

context: I will lift up mine eyes into the hills from whence cometh my help

context: My help cometh from the Lord which made heaven and earth
point_summary: Psalm 121:2's declaration that help comes from the Lord who made heaven and earth is cited as proof that God's creative power grounds His availability and ability to help in times of difficulty.

context: that they may know that this is Thy hand, that Thou, Lord, hast done it

context: David has declared that he put his trust in the Lord. We find and we spent two weeks on that first statement of verse number 1 of chapter 11

context: we've been in the psalm for this our fifth week looking at Psalm 11 and be looking at verses 3 and 4 this evening

context: flee as a bird to your mountain

context: there was a plan of the wicked

context: for lo the wicked bend their bow they make ready their arrow upon the string they may privily shoot at the upright in heart

context: if the foundations be destroyed

context: the Lord is in his holy temple the Lord's throne is in heaven his eyes behold his eyelids try the children of men

context: if the foundations be destroyed, what can the righteous do?

context: follow what takes place in verse number four. We must shift our focus upward as we see David doing in verse number four

context: His eyes behold, his eyelids try the children of men

context: the Lord is in his holy temple, the Lord's throne is in heaven

context: his eyes behold, his eyelids try the children of men

context: as we mentioned in Psalm nine

context: and they that know thy name will put their trust in thee. For thou Lord has not forsaken them that seek thee

context: the Lord is in his holy temple, that the Lord's throne is in heaven

context: I've put my trust in the Lord

context: his eyes behold in his eyelids, try the children of men
point_summary: God's omniscience — that his eyes behold and his eyelids try the children of men — is the ground for trust and rest when wickedness seems to prevail, because nothing escapes his knowledge.

context: in the Lord put I my trust
point_summary: When everything around David was shaking, his opening declaration — "in the Lord put I my trust" — anchored him, modeling the deliberate, declared position of trust that keeps believers from being defeated by discouraging circumstances.

context: I will lift up mine eyes unto the hills from whence cometh my help
point_summary: Lifting one's eyes to the hills is presented as a deliberate, willed act of redirecting focus away from temporal pressures toward the Lord as the only unfailing source of help.

context: I will lift up mine eyes into the hills from whence cometh my help. My help cometh from the Lord, which made heaven and earth
point_summary: Lifting one's eyes to God is presented as a deliberate, volitional act—a purposeful redirection away from visible, temporal pressures toward the Lord as the only unfailing source of help.

context: Look over at the 71st Psalm, if you will. Psalm 71.
point_summary: Psalm 71:18 frames the psalmist's earnest, aging plea that God not forsake him until he has declared divine strength to the present generation and power to those yet to come.

context: now also, when I am old and gray-headed, oh God, forsake me not, notice the rest of the verse, until I have showed thy strength unto this generation and thy power to everyone that is to come
point_summary: Even in old age, the psalmist's earnest plea that God not forsake him until he has declared divine strength to the present generation and power to those yet to come models a lifelong drive to influence others with God's truth.

context: Oh God, forsake me not until I have showed thy strength unto this generation and thy power to everyone that is to come
point_summary: Citing Psalm 71:18, the sermon frames the psalmist's cry—"forsake me not until I have showed thy strength unto this generation"—as a model plea for intentional, passionate influence over those closest to us.

context: I will extol thee, my God, O King, and I will bless thy name forever and ever. Every day will I bless thee, and I will praise thy name forever
point_summary: David's daily commitment to extol and bless God's name is presented as itself a form of influence—publicly praising God even through trials makes an impact on those around us.

context: Great is the Lord, and greatly to be praised, and His greatness is unsearchable
point_summary: God's unsearchable greatness, declared in Psalm 145:3, is presented as the very content that fuels a believer's daily, intentional influence on others through praise.

context: one generation shall praise thy works, what's it say? To another, and shall declare thy mighty acts
point_summary: Generational transmission of praise and declaration of God's mighty acts is presented as a deliberate, unconditional commitment—"I will," not "I might"—and as a primary form of spiritual influence.

context: I will speak of the glorious honor of thy majesty and of thy wondrous works
point_summary: Daily praise of God—extolling His greatness and declaring His wondrous works—is itself a form of spiritual influence, making an impact even amid trials.

context: I will speak of the glorious honor of thy majesty
point_summary: David's volitional declaration — "I will speak of the glorious honor of thy majesty" — is held up as a model of passionate, deliberate commitment to proclaiming God's greatness as a form of spiritual influence.

context: I will declare thy greatness
point_summary: Declaring God's greatness is framed as a personal, passionate commitment—the psalmist's repeated "I will" expressing an unconditional resolve to speak of God's greatness regardless of audience or circumstance.

context: as he said in verse number three, that His greatness is unsearchable
point_summary: David's declaration to speak of God's majesty and greatness is grounded in Psalm 145:3's affirmation that His greatness is unsearchable—exceeding what the mind can conceive or words can articulate.

context: We read already there in Psalm 145 how he had a passion to influence. I will declare. I will speak.
point_summary: David's declared passion to proclaim God's works, cited from Psalm 145, is shown to have translated into active, personal instruction of his son Solomon.

context: generation to come might know them even the children which should be born who should arise and declare them to their children
point_summary: Generational transmission of God's truth—that children might know who God is and set their hope in Him—is identified as the defining purpose of godly influence.

context: to declare thy greatness to another generation
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: talk of all thy wondrous works
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: be not afraid when one is made rich, when the glory of his house is increased. For when he dieth, he shall carry nothing away
point_summary: Earthly wealth cannot cross into eternity; at death, every possession is left behind, and no accumulated glory descends with its owner.

context: the Bible tells us in Psalm 19
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: more to be desired are they than gold? Yea, then much fine gold sweeter also than honey and the honeycomb
point_summary: God's Word is more valuable than gold and sweeter than honey, and by it the servant is warned — underscoring the Word's role as the purging instrument in the believer's life.

context: moreover by them them is thy servant warned, and in keeping of them there is great reward
point_summary: God's Word both warns the servant and promises great reward in obedience, illustrating the dual function of Scripture as a tool God uses to comfort, convict, and change His people.

context: I will set no wicked thing before mine eyes
point_summary: Psalm 101:3 is cited as a ready, targeted scriptural response to temptation, illustrating the skill of wielding the Word of God against specific sins rather than applying one verse indiscriminately.

context: God knows our frame
point_summary: God's knowledge of human frailty ("God knows our frame") grounds the assurance that the overwhelming demands of life are met with divine care, sustenance, and comfort.

context: as the book of Psalms will talk about there, their wit's end
point_summary: Weariness in parenting is likened to the Psalmist's "wit's end," illustrating how sustained faithfulness is required even when results are delayed and doubt sets in.

context: the entrance of thy words giveth light
point_summary: Psalm 119:130's declaration that God's word gives light undergirds the confidence that even a briefly encountered gospel tract can work in a person's heart, since Scripture does not return void.

context: In Psalm 119, the latter part of the chapter, very lengthy, of course the longest chapter in the Bible, 176 verses. But look at Psalm 119 in verse number 165. I want you to notice how the Bible describes this peace.
point_summary: Those who love God's law are promised a peace so comprehensive that nothing—no offense, no trouble, no unexpected difficulty—can ultimately destabilize it.

context: Psalm 19 says that the heavens declare the glory of God, the firmament showeth forth his handiwork
point_summary: Natural revelation in Psalm 19:1 points beyond itself to Scripture, which alone fully discloses who God is—his goodness, greatness, and immutability.

context: taste and see that the Lord is good
point_summary: Loving God's Word yields great peace precisely because Scripture is the primary means by which believers come to know Christ and every true attribute of God.

context: great is the Lord and greatly to be praised
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: great peace have they which love thy law
point_summary: Loving God's law—understood as loving Christ himself, the Bible's central figure—produces great peace and a life untroubled by offense, because Scripture is the sole source of true knowledge about God.

context: Great peace have they which love thy law and nothing shall offend them
point_summary: Those who love God's law possess a "great peace" that functions as a firm foundation, so that nothing—understood here as a stumbling block—can cause them to fall.

context: I'm lifting up mine eyes to the hills from whence cometh my help
point_summary: Lifting one's eyes to the hills (Ps. 121:1) illustrates the mind "stayed" on God rather than fixed on surrounding problems, the posture from which perfect peace flows.

context: my help cometh from the Lord who made heaven and earth
point_summary: Lifting the eyes to the Lord who made heaven and earth, rather than fixating on surrounding problems, is presented as the posture of a mind "stayed" on God in perfect peace.

context: that great peace from Psalm 119 and verse 165
point_summary: Great peace belongs to those who love God's law (Ps. 119:165), cited here as the fruit of a mind stayed on God through immersion in His Word rather than fixation on problems.

context: great peace have they which love thy law and nothing shall offend them
point_summary: Great peace belongs to those who love God's law and are immune to offense, illustrating the expansive scope of the peace available to God's children.

context: We are but dust. God knows our frame
point_summary: Human frailty and the tendency toward worst-case thinking are grounded in the biblical reminder that God knows our frame, that we are but dust, making divine peace and the keeping of heart and mind all the more necessary.
